# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are **simulated KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) questions** aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme question paper pattern: **Part A (3 marks, 9 questions)** and **Part B (14 marks, choice-based)**. Part B questions include full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Is software architecture a phase in the SDLC? Justify your answer in 3 points."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):**

No, software architecture is **not a single phase** in the SDLC. (1) It is a **set of cross-cutting activities and artifacts** that span concept, requirements, design, implementation, testing, deployment, operation, and evolution. (2) The architecture is *created* in early phases but is *validated, refined, and verified* in later phases (e.g., ATAM in testing, drift analysis in operation). (3) Therefore, architecture is better described as a **continuous concern** woven through the life cycle, in line with ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"List any three architecture activities performed in the requirements phase of the SDLC."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Eliciting architecturally significant requirements (ASRs)** — focusing on quality attributes and constraints rather than every functional detail. *(1 mark)*
2. **Documenting quality attribute scenarios** in the canonical 6-part form (source, stimulus, environment, artifact, response, response measure). *(1 mark)*
3. **Prioritizing ASRs** based on business value and technical risk to focus architectural effort. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Q3A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the role of software architecture in the **various phases of the Software Development Life Cycle**. Support your answer with a phase-wise activity table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Architecture Trade-off Analysis Method (ATAM)** and explain how it is used to evaluate the architecture **at the design and testing phases** of the life cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q3A (Part a)

The software architecture plays the following role in each SDLC phase:

**1. Concept / Inception Phase** — The architect identifies *primary use cases*, *high-level quality attribute goals* (e.g., "system must scale to 1 million users"), and *business constraints* (e.g., "must run on AWS"). This produces the **architecture vision** document. *(1 mark)*

**2. Requirements Phase** — *Architecturally Significant Requirements (ASRs)* are elicited and prioritized. Quality attribute scenarios are documented in the standard 6-part form. *(1 mark)*

**3. Architectural Design Phase** — The architect selects *styles* (e.g., layered, microservices, event-driven), *patterns* (e.g., MVC, broker, pipe-filter), and *tactics* (e.g., caching for performance, redundancy for availability). Multiple **views** (4+1 model: logical, process, physical, development, +1 scenarios) are produced. *(1 mark)*

**4. Detailed Design Phase** — The architecture is decomposed into modules; **interface contracts** are defined; ADD steps are applied iteratively. *(1 mark)*

**5. Implementation Phase** — Developers code *to* the architecture. Architecture conformance is checked via code reviews and static analysis. *(0.5 mark)*

**6. Testing / Verification Phase** — **ATAM** is conducted to validate the architecture against quality attribute scenarios. *(0.5 mark)*

**7. Deployment Phase** — The deployment view is realized (containers, VMs, serverless). *(0.5 mark)*

**8. Operation & Maintenance Phase** — Architecture drift is monitored; performance/availability dashboards feed back into the next evolution cycle. *(0.5 mark)*

**9. Evolution Phase** — The architecture is *re-baselined*; legacy systems are *reverse-engineered* to extract architecture. *(0.5 mark)*

**Summary Table (recommended for full marks):**

| Phase | Architecture Activity | Key Artifact |
|-------|----------------------|--------------|
| Concept | Identify drivers | Architecture Vision |
| Requirements | Elicit ASRs | ASR Catalog |
| Design | Select styles/patterns | 4+1 Views |
| Detailed Design | Decompose modules | Interface Contracts |
| Implementation | Code to architecture | Conformance Log |
| Testing | ATAM evaluation | Evaluation Report |
| Deployment | Map to topology | Deployment View |
| Operation | Drift monitoring | Operational Dashboard |
| Evolution | Re-baseline | New Architecture Baseline |

*(0.5 mark for the table)*

---

### Model Solution — Q3A (Part b)

**ATAM (Architecture Trade-off Analysis Method)** is a scenario-based architecture evaluation technique developed at the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI), Carnegie Mellon University**. *(1 mark)*

**Phases of ATAM (9 steps in 4 phases):**

1. **Partnership & Preparation** — The evaluation team and the project team agree on scope. *(1 mark)*
2. **Evaluation Phase 1** — Collect ASRs, present the architecture, identify architectural approaches. *(1 mark)*
3. **Evaluation Phase 2** — Generate a quality-attribute utility tree (root → quality attributes → attribute refinements → scenarios); analyze each scenario against the architecture. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Evaluation Phase 3** — Brainstorm scenarios, map them to architectural tactics, identify risks, non-risks, sensitivity points, and trade-off points. *(1.5 marks)*

**Use in Design Phase:** ATAM is used to **validate design decisions** before coding begins. For example, a design using *caching* to achieve performance is checked against a *caching-invalidation* scenario; the result is a **risk**: "cache staleness may violate data freshness." The architect then either *accepts the risk*, *changes the architecture*, or *adds a tactic* (e.g., write-through cache). *(1 mark)*

**Use in Testing Phase:** ATAM outcomes guide **integration test scenarios** and **system-level performance/security tests**. The risks become test cases; the trade-off points become **regression test points** to ensure that an improvement in one quality attribute (e.g., performance) does not silently degrade another (e.g., modifiability). *(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** ATAM embeds architecture evaluation at *both* the design and testing phases, making the architecture a **continuously validated** artifact throughout the life cycle. *(0.5 mark for conclusion)*

---

### Q3B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **architecture-centric** and **code-centric** life cycle models. Which is preferred in modern large-scale systems, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **Architectural ROI equation** and show quantitatively that early architecture investment is justified. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q3B (Part a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | Architecture-Centric | Code-Centric |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------|
| Order of activities | Architecture first, then code | Code first, architecture emerges |
| Risk of over-design | Higher | Lower |
| Quality attribute predictability | High | Low |
| Speed of initial delivery | Slower | Faster |
| Suitability for safety-critical systems | Excellent | Poor |
| Suitability for prototypes / research | Overkill | Excellent |
| Cost of late changes | Low | High |
| Common in | Aerospace, banking, telecom | Startups, ML research, throwaway code |

*(3 marks for table and 2 marks for explanation)*

**Modern Preference (2 marks):** In modern large-scale systems (Netflix, Amazon, Uber), a **hybrid iterative model** is used — sometimes called *"sculptor's approach"*. The architect sketches a thin vertical slice of the end-to-end architecture in **Iteration 1** (skeleton), then incrementally adds features in subsequent iterations. This combines the **predictability** of architecture-centric with the **delivery speed** of iterative development.

---

### Model Solution — Q3B (Part b)

**Given:**
- Number of architectural defects: $N = 150$.
- Early-detection rate by architecture: $d = 0.6$.
- Baseline defect cost: $k = 800$ ₹.
- Phase exponent: $\alpha = 0.85$.
- $p_{\text{early}} = 1$ (design), $p_{\text{late}} = 4$ (operation).
- Architecture effort: $E = 300$ hours; rate $r = 2000$ ₹/hr; overhead $T = 80{,}000$ ₹.

**Step 1 — Compute exponentials:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{\alpha \cdot p_{\text{early}}} &= e^{0.85 \cdot 1} = e^{0.85} \approx 2.3396 \\
e^{\alpha \cdot p_{\text{late}}} &= e^{0.85 \cdot 4} = e^{3.4} \approx 29.9641
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator (cost saved):**

$$
(1 - 0.6) \cdot 150 \cdot 800 \cdot (29.9641 - 2.3396) = 0.4 \cdot 150 \cdot 800 \cdot 27.6245
$$

$$
= 60 \cdot 800 \cdot 27.6245 = 48{,}000 \cdot 27.6245 \approx 1{,}325{,}976 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (architecture cost):**

$$
300 \cdot 2000 + 80{,}000 = 600{,}000 + 80{,}000 = 680{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — ROI:**

$$
ROI_{\text{arch}} = \dfrac{1{,}325{,}976}{680{,}000} \approx 1.95 = 195\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The investment of **₹ 6.8 lakh** in architecture activities saves approximately **₹ 13.26 lakh** in downstream defect-fixing cost — a return of nearly **2×**. *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "architecture is a phase"** with "architecture is a phase output." — Worth **2 marks** lost if answered incorrectly in Part A.
> 2. **Skipping the table** in 14-mark questions. — Worth **1–2 marks** lost.
> 3. **Not using proper equation notation** in derivation questions. — Worth **1 mark** lost.
> 4. **Writing the answer as one long paragraph.** — Use **bullets + headings** to match KTU valuation key expectations.
> 5. **Forgetting ISO 42010** when discussing life-cycle concerns. — Worth **0.5–1 mark** lost.
> 6. **Not relating the answer to a real SDLC model** (e.g., RUP, Agile, DevOps). — Worth **0.5 mark** lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Save These 15 Points:**

- [x] Architecture is **NOT a phase** — it is a **set of cross-cutting activities and artifacts** across the SDLC. *(Memorize this — it's the #1 question.)*
- [x] **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010** is the governing standard; it defines **stakeholders, concerns, viewpoints, views, and correspondence rules**.
- [x] **Kruchten's 4+1 view model** = Logical, Process, Physical, Development, + Scenarios. *(Draw it on a blank sheet during the exam.)*
- [x] **ATAM** is the canonical architecture evaluation method, conducted at the **end of design** and at **integration testing**.
- [x] The **architecture document is a living artifact** — Draft → Baseline → Refined → Deprecated → Archived.
- [x] **Architecture-centric** is preferred for safety-critical and large-scale systems; **code-centric** is reserved for prototypes and research.
- [x] Modern practice is a **hybrid iterative approach** with an "architectural skeleton" in Iteration 1.
- [x] **ASRs (Architecturally Significant Requirements)** are quality attributes + critical use cases; document them in **6-part scenarios**.
- [x] **Defect cost grows exponentially** with phase: $C \propto e^{\alpha p}$ — justifies early architecture investment.
- [x] **Conformance Index** $\geq 90\%$ is the typical target; lower signals architectural drift.
- [x] **Architecture activities map to phases:** Identify ASRs (Req) → Choose styles (Design) → Conformance (Code/Test) → Drift analysis (Operation) → Re-baseline (Evolution).
- [x] **DevOps/Cloud-native** life cycles treat architecture as **Infrastructure as Code** (Terraform, Helm, etc.).
- [x] **Stakeholders** include end users, operators, developers, security, legal, and business owners — each with distinct concerns.
- [x] **Reverse engineering** is used in the evolution phase to recover architecture from legacy code.
- [x] Always use **diagrams (mermaid/flowcharts)** and **tables** in 14-mark answers; examiners reward visual clarity with **+0.5 to +1 mark**.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Architecture in the Life Cycle** | *PECST861 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of the Architect vs. Engineer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of the Architect vs. Engineer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Software Architect** (KTU Definition): A *Software Architect* is a senior-level practitioner who makes high-level design choices, dictates technical standards including software coding standards, tools, and platforms, and is responsible for translating business requirements into a robust, scalable, and maintainable system structure. The architect owns the **non-functional requirements (NFRs)** and the long-term technical vision of a software system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Software Engineer** (KTU Definition): A *Software Engineer* is a practitioner who applies the principles of software engineering to the design, development, maintenance, testing, and evaluation of software systems. The engineer owns the **implementation fidelity** — translating the architect's design into working, tested, and deployable code that satisfies the functional requirements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Construction Model

Imagine the construction of a **50-storey skyscraper in Kochi**:

- The **Architect** is the person who decides the building's *shape*, the *type of foundation*, the *load-bearing strategy*, the *material philosophy* (steel vs. concrete), the *fire-safety philosophy*, and the *aesthetic vision*. The architect never lays a single brick, but if the architect's blueprint is wrong, the entire building collapses.

- The **Engineer** (Civil/Structural Engineer) is the person who takes the architect's blueprint and *executes* it — calculates the exact rebar ratios, pours the concrete, tests the soil compaction, supervises the daily construction work, and ensures every brick is laid according to the blueprint. The engineer never decides the building's *shape* — but without the engineer's precision, the architect's vision never becomes reality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In KTU examinations, students often *conflate* these two roles. The cardinal rule is: **The Architect owns the "WHAT" and the "WHY" of the system; the Engineer owns the "HOW" and the "WHEN" of the code.**

## 1.3 Core Distinguishing Metrics (Industry-Standard)

The following metrics are universally accepted in the software industry to differentiate the two roles:

- **Architecturally Significant Requirements (ASRs)** — owned by the architect.
- **Lines of Code (LOC) / Function Points (FP)** — the engineer's daily metric.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** — measured at the *module* level by the engineer.
- **System-Level Coupling & Cohesion** — designed at the *architecture* level.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** and **Availability** — the architect's NFR targets.
- **Defect Density (per KLOC)** — the engineer's quality metric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two roles exist on a **spectrum**, not as a binary switch. A senior engineer gradually evolves into an architect by accumulating **breadth** (cross-cutting concerns) rather than **depth** (single-module mastery).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Architect's Role

The architect operates on a **strategic and systemic** plane. The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following responsibilities:

1. **Requirement Elicitation & Translation:** Converts ambiguous business goals into formal software requirements.
2. **Architectural Style Selection:** Chooses between monolithic, microservices, event-driven, layered, or pipe-filter architectures.
3. **Technology Stack Finalization:** Decides languages, frameworks, databases, and middleware.
4. **Defining Cross-Cutting Concerns:** Logs, security, caching, transactions, exception handling.
5. **Quality Attribute Trade-off Analysis:** Performance vs. Security, Scalability vs. Cost.
6. **Documentation:** Produces architectural views using the **4+1 View Model** (Logical, Development, Process, Physical, plus Scenarios).
7. **Stakeholder Communication:** Bridges the gap between business and engineering teams.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The Engineer's Role

The engineer operates on a **tactical and component** plane:

1. **Detailed Design:** Creates class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and component-level logic.
2. **Coding & Unit Testing:** Implements modules in the chosen language/framework.
3. **Code Reviews:** Performs peer reviews to enforce coding standards.
4. **Debugging & Refactoring:** Resolves defects and improves internal code quality.
5. **Integration Testing:** Ensures modules work together as per the architectural contract.
6. **Performance Profiling:** Optimizes algorithms and database queries.
7. **Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD):** Maintains build pipelines.

## 2.3 The Decision Boundary — Who Decides What?

The boundary is best understood via the **ABBEY (Architecture By Example, Yes!)** framework. The KTU examiner expects students to clearly state *which role owns which decision*:

| Decision Domain | Architect | Engineer |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Choice of programming paradigm (OOP / Functional) | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ |
| Class-level method signatures | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Database selection (SQL vs. NoSQL) | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ |
| SQL query optimization & indexing strategy | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Inter-service communication protocol (REST vs. gRPC) | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ |
| API endpoint implementation & error codes | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Cloud provider selection (AWS / Azure / GCP) | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ |
| Container-level resource limits (CPU / RAM tuning) | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Security policy (OAuth2 vs. SAML) | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ |
| Unit test coverage threshold (e.g., 80 percent) | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In a KTU-affiliated IT company like **TCS Kochi** or **Infosys Thiruvananthapuram**, this division is enforced through the *Solution Architect* vs. *Senior Developer* job ladders. Misalignment of these roles is the **root cause of technical debt** in 70 percent of failed enterprise projects (Standish Group CHAOS Report pattern).

## 2.4 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet — Key Terms to Memorize

| Term | KTU Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Architecturally Significant Requirement (ASR)** | A requirement that deeply influences the architecture (e.g., latency, throughput, security). |
| **Technical Debt** | The implied cost of future rework caused by choosing an easy solution today. |
| **Design vs. Implementation** | Design = what to build (Architect); Implementation = how to build it (Engineer). |
| **Conway's Law** | "Organizations design systems that mirror their communication structure." |
| **Architectural Drift** | When implementation deviates from the documented architecture over time. |
| **Bus Factor** | The number of engineers (or architects) that must be hit by a bus before the project stalls. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 The Skill-Spectrum Analysis (Analytical Derivation)

Let us formally define the *skill vector* of each role. KTU examiners often ask: *"How is an architect's skill-set different from an engineer's?"* — the answer requires a structured analysis.

Let:
- $D_i$ = Depth of expertise in a *single* technical area (e.g., Java backend).
- $B_i$ = Breadth of expertise across *multiple* technical areas (e.g., Java, Python, DevOps, Security).
- $C_i$ = Communication & stakeholder-management proficiency.
- $S_i$ = Strategic / abstraction-level thinking.

A software practitioner $P$ can be modeled as a 4-dimensional vector:

$$
\vec{P_i} = \begin{bmatrix} D_i \\ B_i \\ C_i \\ S_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now we can derive the *characteristic difference vector* between an Engineer ($E$) and an Architect ($A$):

$$
\vec{\Delta} = \vec{P_A} - \vec{P_E} = \begin{bmatrix} D_A - D_E \\ B_A - B_E \\ C_A - C_E \\ S_A - S_E \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

1. **Depth ($D$):** An engineer specializes deeply in *one* stack for years. An architect must know *enough* about many stacks but cannot be a deep expert in all. Therefore:

$$
D_A < D_E \quad \text{ (typically, on average across the entire team)}
$$

2. **Breadth ($B$):** An architect must understand databases, networking, security, UX, cloud, and DevOps. An engineer may only know one or two of these. Therefore:

$$
B_A \gg B_E
$$

3. **Communication ($C$):** An architect presents to CXOs, clients, and product owners daily. An engineer communicates primarily with team members. Therefore:

$$
C_A > C_E
$$

4. **Strategic Thinking ($S$):** The architect reasons in terms of *5-year scalability* and *10-year maintainability*. The engineer reasons in terms of *current sprint velocity*. Therefore:

$$
S_A \gg S_E
$$

5. **Combining the inequalities**, the *characteristic difference vector* resolves to:

$$
\vec{\Delta} \approx \begin{bmatrix} - \\ + \\ + \\ + \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where $(-)$ indicates comparatively lower depth, and $(+)$ indicates comparatively higher value in that dimension. This is the *defining mathematical signature* of the architect's role.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners often frame this as *"Justify why a software architect need not be the best coder in the team."* The derivation above is the gold-standard answer.

## 3.2 The Career Evolution Path (Decision Flow Analysis)

The transition from Engineer to Architect follows a non-linear, multi-step evolution. KTU's NEP 2020-aligned syllabus emphasizes this as a key learning outcome.

**Step 1:** Junior Engineer — Implements assigned tasks under supervision.
**Step 2:** Mid-level Engineer — Owns a complete module; writes design documents for the module.
**Step 3:** Senior Engineer — Reviews others' code; mentors juniors; proposes design alternatives.
**Step 4:** Technical Lead — Owns a subsystem; coordinates with architects.
**Step 5:** **Software Architect** — Owns the *entire* system's structure, NFRs, and technology choices.

The critical *phase transition* occurs between Step 3 and Step 5, where the practitioner must **deliberately trade depth for breadth**. A common KTU examination question is:

> *"Why does a star programmer not automatically become a good architect?"*

**Answer framework (4 marks):**
- A star programmer optimizes for **local maxima** (best solution to the immediate problem).
- An architect optimizes for **global maxima** (the best solution for the entire system's lifetime).
- The mindset shift from local to global optimization is a *learned skill*, not a natural byproduct of coding ability.
- Therefore, star programmers must undergo **conscious upskilling** in communication, abstraction, and trade-off analysis.

## 3.3 The Trade-off Triangle — An Analytical Tool

A core KTU 2024 module-1 concept is the architect's perpetual navigation of the **Trade-off Triangle**, where the vertices are:

$$
\text{Quality} \leftrightarrow \text{Cost} \leftrightarrow \text{Time}
$$

- The **engineer** operates *inside* the triangle (given fixed vertices, produce the best code).
- The **architect** operates *on* the triangle (moves the vertices themselves to satisfy business goals).

The optimal vertex position is derived from the business priority function $\Pi$:

$$
\Pi(Q, C, T) = w_1 \cdot Q + w_2 \cdot \frac{1}{C} + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{T}
$$

Where the weights $w_1$, $w_2$, $w_3$ are assigned by the product owner. The architect's job is to communicate the *consequences* of these weight choices to the stakeholders — a process called **Architectural Decision Records (ADR)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Dimension | Software Engineer | Software Architect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary Output | Working code, tests, bug fixes | Architecture document, ADRs, NFR specs |
| Time Horizon | Sprint-level (2 to 4 weeks) | System-lifecycle (3 to 10 years) |
| Abstract Thinking | Low to Moderate | Very High |
| Decision Impact | Local (module-level) | Global (system-wide) |
| Stakeholders | Developers, QA, immediate manager | CXOs, product owners, clients, ops |
| Tools | IDE, debugger, profiler, Git | Modeling tools (UML), whiteboard, C4 model |
| Failure Consequence | Bug in production | Project collapse / bankruptcy |
| Success Metric | Story points completed | System uptime, scalability index |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Role Interaction in a Software Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    busNeeds[Business Needs and Vision] --> archLead[Software Architect]
    archLead --> adrDoc[Architectural Decision Records]
    archLead --> nfrDoc[Non Functional Requirement Specs]
    archLead --> techStack[Technology Stack Selection]
    archLead --> blueprint[System Blueprint and Interfaces]
    blueprint --> engLead[Senior Engineer and Tech Lead]
    engLead --> module1[Module A Implementation]
    engLead --> module2[Module B Implementation]
    engLead --> module3[Module C Implementation]
    module1 --> codeReview[Peer Code Review]
    module2 --> codeReview
    module3 --> codeReview
    codeReview --> unitTest[Unit and Integration Testing]
    unitTest --> deployPipe[CI CD Pipeline]
    deployPipe --> prodSystem[Production System]
    prodSystem -. feedback loop .-> archLead
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Product Owner
    participant Arch as Software Architect
    participant Eng as Software Engineer
    participant QA as Quality Assurance

    Client->>Arch: Submit business requirements
    Arch->>Arch: Identify ASRs and NFRs
    Arch->>Eng: Hand over architecture document
    Eng->>Eng: Design class and sequence diagrams
    Eng->>Eng: Implement code and unit tests
    Eng->>QA: Submit module for testing
    QA-->>Eng: Report defects
    Eng->>Eng: Refactor and re-test
    Eng-->>Arch: Raise architectural concerns if any
    Arch->>Client: Report progress and trade-offs
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Career Evolution Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[Stage 1 Junior Engineer] --> stage2[Stage 2 Mid Level Engineer]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Senior Engineer]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4 Technical Lead]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5 Software Architect]
    stage5 --> stage6[Stage 6 Enterprise Architect]
    style stage1 fill:#e3f2fd
    style stage2 fill:#bbdefb
    style stage3 fill:#90caf9
    style stage4 fill:#64b5f6
    style stage5 fill:#1e88e5,color:#ffffff
    style stage6 fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Trade-off Triangle (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TriangleVertexA [TRADE OFF TRIANGLE]
        direction LR
        vertexQ[VERTEX Q QUALITY]
        vertexC[VERTEX C COST]
        vertexT[VERTEX T TIME]
    end
    vertexQ -. moves .-> archRole[ARCHITECT MOVES VERTICES]
    vertexC -. moves .-> archRole
    vertexT -. moves .-> archRole
    archRole --> npiFunction[Priority Function Pi Q C T]
    npiFunction --> businessGoal[Business Goal Satisfaction]
    businessGoal --> deploy[System Deployed Successfully]
    deploy -. feedback .-> archRole
    style archRole fill:#fff3e0
    style businessGoal fill:#c8e6c9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the roles of a Software Architect and a Software Engineer in terms of time horizon, decision impact, and primary deliverable. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (KTU Valuation Key Pattern):**

A *Software Engineer* operates on a **sprint-level time horizon (2–4 weeks)**, makes decisions with **local (module-level) impact**, and produces **working code, unit tests, and bug fixes** as the primary deliverable.

A *Software Architect* operates on a **system-lifecycle time horizon (3–10 years)**, makes decisions with **global (system-wide) impact**, and produces the **architecture document, ADR, and NFR specification** as the primary deliverable.

In essence, the engineer executes the architect's vision through code, while the architect safeguards the system's long-term health through design.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Define "Architecturally Significant Requirement" (ASR). Give two examples. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An **Architecturally Significant Requirement (ASR)** is a requirement that has a profound and lasting influence on the software architecture — typically a **non-functional requirement** that constrains the design choices.

**Examples:**
1. The system must handle **10,000 concurrent users** with a response time under 200 ms (Performance ASR).
2. All user passwords must be stored using **AES-256 encryption** at rest and in transit (Security ASR).

Such requirements cannot be addressed by code-level changes alone; they demand **structural** decisions made by the architect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module 1 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently *describe* the roles but fail to *contrast* them with a clear boundary. The KTU 2024 answer key demands (a) **explicit comparison** with 3+ dimensions, (b) **at least one real-world analogy**, and (c) a **concluding summary sentence**. Missing any one of these typically costs **2 to 3 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *"A star programmer does not automatically become a good software architect."* Critically analyze this statement with reference to the shift in **skill dimensions** required for the architectural role. Support your answer with a **skill-vector model**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **Trade-off Triangle** (Quality, Cost, Time) in software architecture. How does the architect's role differ from the engineer's role in navigating this triangle? Derive the priority function $\Pi(Q, C, T)$ and discuss the significance of weights $w_1$, $w_2$, $w_3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Skill Dimension Shift Analysis:**

**Step 1: Define the Skill Vector.** A practitioner $P$ can be modeled as a 4-dimensional vector:

$$
\vec{P_i} = \begin{bmatrix} D_i \\ B_i \\ C_i \\ S_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where $D_i$ is depth, $B_i$ is breadth, $C_i$ is communication, and $S_i$ is strategic thinking.

**Step 2: Define the Difference Vector.**

$$
\vec{\Delta} = \vec{P_A} - \vec{P_E}
$$

**Step 3: Derive the Sign Pattern.** A star programmer has very high $D_E$ (deepest coder), but moderate $B_E$, $C_E$, and $S_E$. A good architect has moderate $D_A$, very high $B_A$, $C_A$, and $S_A$. Therefore:

$$
\vec{\Delta} \approx \begin{bmatrix} - \\ + \\ + \\ + \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Justify the Statement.** The negative sign on $D$ proves that being the best *coder* is *not* a sufficient condition for being a good *architect*. In fact, the deepest coders often fail as architects because they continue to optimize for **local code elegance** rather than **system-level trade-offs**. The architect must deliberately *unlearn* the obsession with local maxima.

**Step 5: Conclude.** The architect's role requires a **conscious trade-off of depth for breadth** — a learned, not innate, skill.

**Part (b) — Trade-off Triangle and Priority Function:**

**Step 1: Define the Triangle.** The three vertices are Quality ($Q$), Cost ($C$), and Time ($T$).

**Step 2: Role of the Engineer.** The engineer operates *inside* the triangle — given the vertices (fixed by the architect and product owner), the engineer must produce the best possible code.

**Step 3: Role of the Architect.** The architect operates *on* the triangle — the architect *moves* the vertices themselves based on business priorities.

**Step 4: Priority Function Derivation.**

$$
\Pi(Q, C, T) = w_1 \cdot Q + w_2 \cdot \frac{1}{C} + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 5: Discuss Weights.** The weights $w_1$, $w_2$, $w_3$ are supplied by the product owner. A startup may set $w_3$ (Time) high; a banking system may set $w_1$ (Quality / Security) high. The architect's job is to **translate these business weights into technical decisions** and document them in an Architectural Decision Record (ADR).

**Step 6: Conclude.** The Trade-off Triangle is the conceptual *playground* of the architect; the engineer executes *within* this playground.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a **real-world analogy**, explain the difference between the role of a Software Architect and a Software Engineer. List **five distinct decision domains** where the architect's decision supersedes the engineer's. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Conway's Law** and its implications on the role of the architect. How does the architect use this law to design **team-aware systems**? Provide an example where violating Conway's Law led to project failure. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Analogy and Decision Domains:**

**Step 1: State the Analogy.** The *Software Architect* is to a software system what a *Building Architect* is to a skyscraper — the architect decides the *structure, materials, and philosophy*; the engineer (civil/structural) *executes* the blueprint by laying the bricks, pouring the concrete, and testing the soil.

**Step 2: List Five Decision Domains Owned by the Architect:**

1. **Choice of architectural style** (monolithic, microservices, event-driven).
2. **Selection of database paradigm** (relational vs. document vs. graph).
3. **Inter-service communication protocol** (REST, gRPC, message queues).
4. **Cloud provider selection** (AWS, Azure, GCP).
5. **Security policy** (OAuth2, JWT, SAML, mTLS).

**Step 3: Contrast with the Engineer's Role.** The engineer *implements* within these constraints — e.g., given REST, the engineer writes the endpoints; given OAuth2, the engineer writes the token-validation middleware.

**Part (b) — Conway's Law and Team-Aware Design:**

**Step 1: State Conway's Law.**

> *"Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations."* — Melvin Conway, 1968.

**Step 2: Architect's Use of the Law.** The architect designs the system's module boundaries to **mirror the team's communication structure**. If there are 3 independent teams, the architect will propose 3 independent microservices — not one monolithic blob owned by all 3 teams (which would create merge conflicts and communication overhead).

**Step 3: Failure Example.** A KTU-style case: A company tried to build a *single microservice* for the entire "Order Management" domain, but the team was split across 3 cities (Kochi, Bengaluru, Chennai). The team's communication structure was *distributed*, but the architecture was *centralized* — this violated Conway's Law. Result: merge conflicts, communication delays, 18-month delays, and project failure.

**Step 4: Conclude.** The architect must treat **team topology** as a first-class design constraint — this is the *Inverse Conway Maneuver*, where the architect deliberately reshapes the team's communication structure to match the desired architecture.

---

## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Block — Memorize These Bullet Points:**

- **Architect** = Strategic, system-wide, long-term, owns NFRs, ASRs, and technology stack.
- **Engineer** = Tactical, module-level, short-term (sprint), owns code quality, unit tests, and CI/CD.
- The architect's primary deliverable is the **architecture document + ADR**; the engineer's is **working, tested code**.
- The architect operates on the **Trade-off Triangle**; the engineer operates *inside* it.
- The skill-vector difference is: $\vec{\Delta} \approx [-, +, +, +]$ (less depth, more breadth, more communication, more strategy).
- **Conway's Law** dictates that the system architecture must mirror team communication structure.
- The **Inverse Conway Maneuver** reshapes teams to match architecture.
- **Architectural Drift** is the gradual deviation of implementation from the original architecture — the architect's job is to prevent it.
- **Bus Factor** is a metric the architect must increase by cross-training and documentation.
- **Technical Debt** is the cost of shortcuts; both roles share responsibility, but the architect sets the debt tolerance threshold.
- The architect is **NOT** the best coder; the architect is the best **abstraction thinker and trade-off navigator**.
- Career path: **Junior → Mid → Senior → Tech Lead → Software Architect → Enterprise Architect**.
- KTU 2024 expects students to know the **4+1 Architectural View Model** (Logical, Development, Process, Physical + Scenarios).
- The KTU board's most-frequently-tested sub-topic in this module is the **comparison of architect vs. engineer across 3+ dimensions with a real-world analogy**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Engineering: Stakeholders, Concerns, and Types of Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Requirements Engineering: Stakeholders, Concerns, and Types of Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Requirements Engineering (RE) — Formal Definition

**Requirements Engineering (RE)** is the systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach to the *elicitation, analysis, specification, validation, and management* of software (or system) requirements. It is the bridge between the *informal real-world problem space* and the *formal solution space* of the engineered system.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, RE is treated as the **foundational phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** that *directly drives* architectural decision-making. As the famous architectural maxim states:

> *"Architecture is the artifact that addresses the **stakeholders' concerns** by **quality-attribute requirements**, while respecting the **functional requirements** and **constraints**."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the Software Architectures course, RE is *not* taught as a generic SDLC topic. It is taught specifically to identify **architecturally significant requirements (ASRs)** — the small, critical subset of all requirements that *constrain the architectural decisions* (e.g., performance under 100ms latency, 99.99% availability, HIPAA compliance).

---

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of RE

The complete RE process rests on three interdependent pillars that this module addresses:

| Pillar | Focus | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| **Stakeholders** | *Who* has an interest? | Stakeholder register / onion model |
| **Concerns** | *What* do they care about? | Concern catalogue / utility tree |
| **Requirements** | *How* do we capture them? | SRS document / requirements spec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitional Clarity:** A **concern** is *what* a stakeholder wants (e.g., "the system must respond fast"). A **requirement** is a *precise, testable, and agreed-upon* statement of that concern (e.g., "the search query shall return results within 200 ms at the 95th percentile under a load of 10,000 concurrent users"). Many concerns never become requirements; they remain design constraints or quality goals.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"

Imagine you are commissioning an **architect to design your dream house**:

- **Stakeholders** = the *people* involved: you, your spouse, kids, the bank manager (loan), the municipal corporation (permits), the contractor, the interior designer, neighbors, future buyers.
- **Concerns** = the *interests* they each voice: "I want 4 bedrooms" (family), "it must withstand floods" (location), "the loan interest must be under 9%" (bank), "it must follow the local FAR rules" (municipality), "the resale value should appreciate" (neighbor).
- **Requirements** = the *written, measurable, contractual specifications* in the blueprint: "Master bedroom = 200 sq.ft, north-facing, attached bathroom, marble flooring, double-glazed windows."
- **Architecture** = the actual *floor plan and structural design* that resolves *conflicting concerns* (you want a glass façade, the engineer says it cannot bear cyclone winds — the architecture must balance them).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In exam questions, students often confuse *concerns* with *requirements*. Remember: **Concerns are subjective wishes; Requirements are objective, verifiable statements.**

---

### 1.4 Why RE Matters for Software Architecture

The architectural decisions of a system — module decomposition, communication patterns, deployment topology, technology stacks — are *driven by* a small set of requirements. The KTU syllabus emphasizes that an architect who fails at RE will:

1. Over-engineer for *non-architectural* concerns (wasting effort on irrelevant quality attributes).
2. Under-engineer for *critical* concerns (causing catastrophic failure post-deployment).
3. Misalign the system with stakeholder *value*, leading to project rejection.

> **Industry Statistic (widely cited in KTU reference materials):** Approximately **60–80% of all software defects** originate from *poorly defined, misunderstood, or missing requirements*. The **Standish Group CHAOS report** historically attributes over **30% of project failures** to requirements issues.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Stakeholders — Detailed Taxonomy

A **stakeholder** (also called a *concern holder* or *actor*) is any individual, group, or organization that:
- Has a **direct or indirect interest** in the system,
- **Influences** or **is influenced by** the system's behavior, structure, or operation.

#### 2.1.1 The **Rozanski & Woods "Onion Model"** of Stakeholders

The onion model classifies stakeholders by their *proximity* to the system. KTU 2024 frequently tests this classification.

| Layer | Stakeholder Type | Examples | Interest |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core (Innermost)** | Developers, Testers, Architects, Project Managers | Engineers on the team | Build-ability, test-ability, maintainability |
| **Inner Ring** | System Administrators, Operators, Support Staff | DevOps, SREs, Helpdesk | Deploy-ability, monitor-ability, operability |
| **Middle Ring** | End Users, Business Managers, Domain Experts | Daily users, line managers, compliance officers | Functionality, usability, business value |
| **Outer Ring** | Acquirers, Customers, Regulators, Legal, Marketing | Procurement, government, advertising team | Cost, ROI, time-to-market, legal compliance |
| **Outermost** | Society, Competitors, Future Generations | Public, rival firms, sustainability advocates | Ethics, environmental impact, market position |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **acquirer** (the one who *pays* for the system) and the **end user** (the one who *uses* it) are *rarely the same person*. Their concerns often conflict — the acquirer wants low cost; the user wants high features. The architect's job is to **balance these concerns**.

#### 2.1.2 Stakeholder Identification Techniques

1. **Direct questioning** — interviews, workshops.
2. **Brainstorming** — cross-functional sessions.
3. **Questionnaire / Survey** — for large, distributed populations.
4. **Observation / Ethnography** — watching real workflows.
5. **Document analysis** — existing process docs, complaint logs.
6. **Stakeholder mapping** — using a **Power/Interest Grid**:

```
                High Power
                    |
     Manage         |       Keep
     Closely        |       Satisfied
   -----------------+-----------------
     Monitor        |       Keep
     (Minimal       |       Informed
      effort)       |
                    |
                Low Power
      Low Interest  |  High Interest
```

---

### 2.2 Concerns — The Architect's True Input

A **concern** is any *interest, goal, or constraint* a stakeholder has about the system's development, operation, or evolution. Concerns are the *raw material* from which requirements are derived.

#### 2.2.1 Classification of Concerns

| Concern Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional concerns** | What the system *does* | "Process credit-card payments" |
| **Quality-of-Service (QoS) concerns** | *How well* it does it | "Process 500 tx/sec with < 1% errors" |
| **Constraint concerns** | Hard limits on design choices | "Must run on Android 9.0+" |
| **Business concerns** | Market, cost, time, legal | "Launch in 6 months under ₹50 lakh" |
| **Evolutionary concerns** | Future changes | "Support 10× user growth in 3 years" |
| **Cross-cutting concerns** | Span multiple components | "All modules must log audit trails" |

#### 2.2.2 The **Architecturally Significant Concerns (ASCs)** Subset

Not all concerns drive architecture. Only a small subset — **Architecturally Significant Concerns** — actually *constrain* the design. Examples:

- *Performance* (latency, throughput, jitter)
- *Availability* (uptime, MTBF, MTTR)
- *Security* (confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation)
- *Modifiability* (modularity, pluggability, configurability)
- *Interoperability* (standards, APIs, protocols)
- *Portability* (across OS, devices, browsers)
- *Testability* (controllability, observability)
- *Usability* (learnability, efficiency of use)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term — "Utility Tree":** A hierarchical decomposition of *quality attributes → scenarios → responses → measures*. It is the *primary input artifact* for the **ATAM** (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method), which KTU expects you to know in Module 2.

---

### 2.3 Types of Requirements — Complete Classification

Requirements form a *hierarchy* of decreasing abstraction. KTU 2024 emphasizes the **FURPS+** model and the **functional vs. non-functional** distinction.

#### 2.3.1 The **FURPS+ Model** (Grady, Casamayor, et al.)

| Letter | Category | Example Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **F** | **Functional** | "System shall allow user to reset password via email." |
| **U** | **Usability** | "90% of users shall complete checkout in ≤ 3 steps." |
| **R** | **Reliability** | "MTBF ≥ 10,000 hours; availability ≥ 99.95%." |
| **P** | **Performance** | "Search response < 200 ms at p95, load = 5,000 QPS." |
| **S** | **Supportability** | "Logs shall be retained for 1 year; config via YAML." |
| **+** | **Design constraints** | "Must be implemented in Java 17+, deploy on Kubernetes." |
| **+** | **Implementation constraints** | "Use PostgreSQL 15 as primary RDBMS." |
| **+** | **Interface constraints** | "Expose REST API per OpenAPI 3.0 spec." |
| **+** | **Physical constraints** | "Must operate at -20°C to +60°C." |

#### 2.3.2 Functional vs. Non-Functional Requirements

| Aspect | Functional Requirements (FR) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specify *what the system does* | Specify *how well* the system does it |
| **Nature** | Behavior, features, business rules | Quality attributes, constraints |
| **Testability** | Verified by *functional test* | Verified by *non-functional test* (load, security, etc.) |
| **Volatility** | Higher (change with market) | Lower (often stable across product versions) |
| **Architectural impact** | Indirect (drives use cases) | Direct (drives architecture) |
| **Example** | "User can upload a 10 MB PDF" | "Upload completes in < 5 sec on 3G" |

#### 2.3.3 Other Important Requirement Categories

- **User Requirements** — abstract, written in natural language, for stakeholders.
- **System Requirements** — detailed, structured, for developers.
- **Domain Requirements** — derived from the application *domain* (e.g., healthcare HIPAA, aviation DO-178C, finance PCI-DSS).
- **Architecturally Significant Requirements (ASRs)** — the small subset that *constrain* architecture.
- **Inverse Requirements** — what the system *shall NOT do* (e.g., "system shall not store CVV after transaction").
- **Emergent Requirements** — those arising from *integration* of components, not from a single component.

#### 2.3.4 The Requirements Pyramid

```
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │  Business / Vision  │    ← Highest abstraction
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
                    ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                    │  User Requirements  │    ← Stakeholder-facing
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
                ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                │   System / Software Req.     │  ← SRS document
                └──┬──────────────┬────────────┘
                   │              │
        ┌──────────┴──┐    ┌──────┴─────────┐
        │ Functional  │    │ Non-Functional │  ← Architecture-driving
        └─────────────┘    └────────────────┘
```

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | One-Line Definition | KTU Exam Trigger Word |
|---|---|---|
| **Stakeholder** | Anyone affected by or affecting the system | "Identify stakeholders for…" |
| **Concern** | A stakeholder's interest, goal, or constraint | "What concerns does the user have about…" |
| **Requirement** | A verifiable, agreed-upon statement of a need | "Write a requirement for…" |
| **ASR** | A requirement that constrains architectural choices | "Which is an architecturally significant requirement?" |
| **FURPS+** | A model classifying requirements | "Classify the following into FURPS+…" |
| **Quality Attribute** | A measurable characteristic of system behavior | "Specify the QA scenario for performance" |
| **Constraint** | A design or implementation limitation | "What constraint is implied by…?" |
| **Onion Model** | Stakeholder classification by proximity | "Place the DBA in the onion model" |
| **Domain Requirement** | Requirement from the application domain | "Why is HIPAA a domain requirement?" |
| **MTBF / MTTR / Availability** | Reliability metrics | "Calculate availability from MTBF and MTTR" |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Requirement Quality Checklist

A *good* requirement must satisfy the **SMART-FINEST** criteria (KTU expects this acronym in exam answers):

| Letter | Property | Test Question |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Specific** | Is it unambiguous and clear? |
| **M** | **Measurable** | Can we objectively verify it? |
| **A** | **Attainable** | Is it technically feasible? |
| **R** | **Realizable** | Can we build it within resources? |
| **T** | **Traceable** | Can we link it back to a stakeholder concern? |
| **F** | **Free of design** | Does it *prescribe* a solution, or *describe* a need? |
| **I** | **Identifiable** | Does it have a unique ID? |
| **N** | **Negotiable** | Is it open to revision if trade-offs demand? |
| **E** | **Executable** | Can it be tested by QA? |
| **S** | **Stable** | Will it remain valid across the project? |
| **T** | **Testable** | Can we define pass/fail criteria? |

---

### 3.2 Derivation Walkthrough 1 — Availability from MTBF and MTTR

This is a **numerical KTU favorite** for converting reliability concerns into measurable requirements.

**Given:**
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) = **720 hours**
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) = **8 hours**

**To find:** Availability $A$

**Step 1 — Identify the standard formula:**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{720}{720 + 8}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{720}{728}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the decimal value:**

$$
A \;\approx\; 0.98901
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage (standard industry form):**

$$
A \;\approx\; 98.90\%
$$

**Step 6 — Express in the "nines" notation:**

$$
A \;\approx\; 98.9\% \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \text{roughly "two nines"}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** An MTBF of 720 h with 8 h repair gives only ~98.9% availability. For an e-commerce site targeting 99.99% ("four nines"), MTTR would need to drop to ~0.072 h (~4.3 minutes) at the same MTBF. This is *exactly the kind of tradeoff* an architect must surface.

---

### 3.3 Derivation Walkthrough 2 — P95 Latency from Throughput Constraint

**Given:**
- A web service must serve **5,000 requests per second (RPS)**.
- The system has **8 application servers**, each with a single-core CPU.
- The Service-Level Objective (SLO) requires **p95 latency ≤ 200 ms**.

**Step 1 — Compute RPS per server (uniform distribution assumed):**

$$
R_{\text{per server}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{8} \;=\; 625 \text{ RPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Little's Law (queueing theory, applied to a single server):**

$$
L \;=\; \lambda \cdot W
$$

Where:
- $L$ = average number of in-flight requests per server
- $\lambda$ = arrival rate (RPS)
- $W$ = average wait time (seconds)

**Step 3 — Substitute values to find concurrency per server at average latency:**

$$
L \;=\; 625 \cdot 0.200 \;=\; 125 \text{ in-flight requests per server}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret architecturally:**

Each server must support **125 concurrent connections** while keeping *p95* (not average) under 200 ms. Since p95 ≥ average, the *server-side latency budget* is tighter — typically 100–150 ms server time, leaving headroom for network.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** This derivation demonstrates *how* a performance concern (latency) is decomposed into *operational metrics* (concurrency, threads, connection pools) that constrain architecture (e.g., choosing async I/O vs. thread-per-request).

---

### 3.4 Derivation Walkthrough 3 — Stakeholder Concern → Requirement Traceability

Below is a **worked example of converting concerns into proper requirements**, which KTU frequently tests in 14-mark questions.

**Step 1 — Identify stakeholders and their concerns (raw, informal):**

| Stakeholder | Raw Concern (informal) |
|---|---|
| Online shopper | "I want to checkout fast." |
| Site owner | "I want to make money." |
| DBA | "I want easy backups." |
| Regulator | "Data must be safe." |

**Step 2 — Transform each into a measurable, testable requirement:**

| ID | Refined Requirement (SRS-quality) |
|---|---|
| **REQ-001** | The checkout flow *shall* complete in ≤ 3 user actions. |
| **REQ-002** | The system *shall* process at least 500 payment transactions per minute at p99 latency ≤ 1 s. |
| **REQ-003** | Database backups *shall* be automated daily at 02:00 IST and retained for 30 days. |
| **REQ-004** | All customer PII *shall* be encrypted at rest using AES-256 and in transit using TLS 1.3. |

**Step 3 — Classify each into FURPS+ and functional/non-functional:**

| ID | FURPS+ Category | FR / NFR |
|---|---|---|
| REQ-001 | Usability | NFR |
| REQ-002 | Performance | NFR |
| REQ-003 | Supportability | NFR |
| REQ-004 | Security (+ Design Constraint) | NFR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In modern architectures, the majority of *architecturally significant* requirements are **non-functional**. This is why KTU devotes Module 1 to teaching the *taxonomy* — without it, you cannot identify what drives the architecture.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Concern-to-Architecture Mapping

| Concern | Architectural Tactic | KTU Question Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| High availability | Redundancy, failover, replication | "How would you achieve 99.99% uptime?" |
| High performance | Caching, async, load balancing | "Reduce response time under 200 ms" |
| Security | Encryption, authn/authz, audit logging | "Design for HIPAA compliance" |
| Modifiability | Modularity, plug-ins, microservices | "Support adding 5 new payment gateways" |
| Scalability | Horizontal scaling, sharding, statelessness | "Scale from 10K to 1M users" |
| Interoperability | Standard APIs (REST, gRPC), adapters | "Integrate with legacy SOAP system" |
| Usability | Responsive UI, accessibility (WCAG) | "Support screen readers and mobile" |
| Testability | Dependency injection, mock interfaces | "Enable automated CI/CD testing" |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

> **Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements with two examples each. Why are non-functional requirements often called the "drivers of architecture"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specify the *behavioral* features the system must provide | Specify the *quality attributes* and constraints governing *how* the system performs |
| **Examples** | (1) "The system shall allow users to reset passwords via email." (2) "The system shall calculate GST at 18% on invoices." | (1) "Password reset email shall be delivered within 60 seconds." (2) "Invoice generation shall complete in under 2 seconds for 1,000 concurrent users." |
| **Focus** | *What* the system does | *How well* the system does it |
| **Test type** | Functional test (unit, integration, UAT) | Non-functional test (load, security, soak) |

**Why NFRs drive architecture (1 mark):** NFRs dictate the *structure*, *technology*, and *deployment topology* of the system. For example, a "p99 latency under 100 ms" requirement forces the architect to choose caching, async I/O, and a specific deployment strategy — none of which are visible from functional requirements alone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supple Set B]**

> **Explain the concept of "Stakeholder" in requirements engineering. List and briefly describe the four layers of the Rozanski & Woods Onion Model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stakeholder** is any individual, group, or organization that has an interest in, influence over, or is affected by the software system under development.

**Onion Model Layers (from innermost to outermost):**

1. **Core Layer** — Direct development participants: developers, testers, architects, project managers. Concerned with build-ability and test-ability.
2. **Inner Ring** — Operational stakeholders: system administrators, support staff, DevOps engineers. Concerned with deployment, monitoring, and operability.
3. **Middle Ring** — Business and end-user stakeholders: end users, business managers, domain experts. Concerned with functionality, usability, and value delivery.
4. **Outer Ring** — External business stakeholders: acquirers, customers, regulators, legal teams. Concerned with cost, ROI, compliance, and time-to-market.
5. **Outermost Layer** — Societal stakeholders: public, competitors, sustainability advocates. Concerned with ethics, environmental impact, and market position.

*(Any 4 layers with description = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Slot]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** For an **Online Food Delivery System** (e.g., a Swiggy/Zomato clone), identify the **stakeholders** using the Onion Model, list at least **three architecturally significant concerns** of each layer, and explain how an architect would resolve conflicting concerns between the acquirer and the end user.

> **(b) [7 marks]** Classify the following requirements using the **FURPS+ model** and state which ones are **architecturally significant**. Justify your answers:
>
> 1. "The system shall allow customers to search for restaurants by cuisine, rating, and distance."
> 2. "The search results page shall load in ≤ 1.5 seconds at the 95th percentile under 10,000 concurrent users."
> 3. "The system shall be developed using Python 3.11 and the Django 5.0 framework."
> 4. "All payment data shall be encrypted using AES-256 and transmitted over TLS 1.3."
> 5. "The application shall be available 99.95% of the time, measured monthly."
> 6. "The system shall send an order-confirmation SMS within 30 seconds of order placement."
> 7. "The codebase shall follow PEP-8 style guidelines."

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Stakeholders per Onion Layer:**

| Layer | Stakeholders |
|---|---|
| **Core** | Backend developers, mobile app developers, DBAs, QA engineers, Lead architect |
| **Inner** | DevOps engineers, Site Reliability Engineers (SREs), Customer support agents |
| **Middle** | Hungry end users (customers), Restaurant owners, Delivery partners (riders), Business operations team |
| **Outer** | Acquirer (company's board / investors), Marketing team, Legal/regulatory bodies (FSSAI for food safety), Payment gateway partners (Razorpay, Paytm) |
| **Outermost** | Public (food safety), Competitors (Swiggy vs. Zomato), Local communities (traffic, pollution) |

**Three Architecturally Significant Concerns per Layer (sample):**

- **Core:** code modifiability, testability, build automation.
- **Inner:** observability (metrics/logs), deployability, incident recovery.
- **Middle:** responsiveness, ease of ordering, accurate ETAs.
- **Outer:** cost per transaction, regulatory compliance, brand reputation.
- **Outermost:** environmental footprint (electric vehicles), ethical AI in recommendations.

**Resolving Acquirer vs. End-User Conflict (2 marks):**

- **Conflicting concerns:** Acquirer wants *low cost & fast ROI* (e.g., minimize infrastructure spend); end user wants *fast delivery, low delivery fee, many features*.
- **Architect's resolution:**
  1. Use **serverless / auto-scaling cloud** to align cost with load — pay only when orders flow.
  2. Adopt **edge caching** and **CDNs** to reduce latency without expensive backends.
  3. Use **A/B testing** to validate which features drive conversion before investing in them.
  4. Apply **QoS prioritization**: free users get slower SLA, premium subscribers get priority routing.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (1 mark per requirement, classification + justification)

| # | Requirement | FURPS+ Category | ASR? | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search restaurants by cuisine/rating/distance | **Functional (F)** | No | Pure behavioral feature; verifiable by functional tests; does not constrain architecture. |
| 2 | Search results ≤ 1.5 s at p95, 10K users | **Performance (P)** | **Yes** | Forces caching, indexing strategy, load balancing, async I/O. |
| 3 | Python 3.11 + Django 5.0 | **Design Constraint (+)** | **Yes** | Hard constraint on language and framework — limits technology choices. |
| 4 | AES-256 + TLS 1.3 for payment data | **Security (R) + Interface Constraint (+)** | **Yes** | Drives crypto module, key management, network protocol layer. |
| 5 | Availability 99.95% monthly | **Reliability (R)** | **Yes** | Mandates redundancy, failover, multi-AZ deployment, health checks. |
| 6 | Order SMS within 30 s | **Performance (P) / Supportability (S)** | Borderline | Drives async messaging (Kafka/SQS) and SMS provider selection. |
| 7 | Follow PEP-8 | **Design Constraint (+) / Supportability (S)** | No | Coding style; enforceable via linter; no architectural impact. |

**ASRs identified:** Req #2, #3, #4, #5 (and arguably #6).

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **FURPS+ classification of requirements**. Provide **two example requirements** for each category and explain why **functional requirements alone are insufficient** to design an architecture.

> **(b) [7 marks]** A banking system has the following reliability data: **MTBF = 4,380 hours** and **MTTR = 1 hour**.
>
> (i) Calculate the system availability as a percentage and express it in "nines" notation.
> (ii) The bank now wants to achieve **99.99% availability** (four nines) while keeping MTBF unchanged. What is the **maximum allowable MTTR** (in minutes) to achieve this?
> (iii) Suggest **two architectural tactics** to reduce MTTR for a banking system.

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**FURPS+ Detailed Classification (1.5 marks for model + 3 marks for examples + 2.5 marks for FR-insufficiency argument):**

The **FURPS+** model, developed by Hewlett-Packard and popularized by Grady, classifies software requirements into:

- **F — Functional:** Capabilities, features, business rules.
  - Ex1: "The system shall allow a customer to transfer funds between linked accounts."
  - Ex2: "The system shall generate a monthly statement on the 1st of each month."

- **U — Usability:** Human factors, aesthetics, accessibility, help systems.
  - Ex1: "The login screen shall be operable by screen readers per WCAG 2.1 AA."
  - Ex2: "New users shall complete onboarding in ≤ 5 minutes."

- **R — Reliability:** Availability, fault tolerance, recoverability, accuracy.
  - Ex1: "The system shall maintain 99.95% availability, measured monthly."
  - Ex2: "Data loss shall be < 1 transaction per 10 million operations."

- **P — Performance:** Speed, efficiency, throughput, capacity, response time.
  - Ex1: "Transaction authorization shall complete in ≤ 500 ms at p99."
  - Ex2: "The system shall support 50,000 transactions per second."

- **S — Supportability:** Maintainability, configurability, installability, localization.
  - Ex1: "All configuration shall be externalized in YAML files."
  - Ex2: "Log retention shall be configurable, defaulting to 90 days."

- **+ Constraints** (sub-categories):
  - **Design constraints:** "Use Java 17 and Spring Boot 3.x."
  - **Implementation constraints:** "Use Oracle 19c as primary RDBMS."
  - **Interface constraints:** "Expose OpenAPI 3.0-compliant REST APIs."
  - **Physical constraints:** "Operate in temperature range 0°C to 40°C."

**Why FRs alone are insufficient for architecture (2.5 marks):**

1. **Quality attributes are invisible in FRs.** A functional requirement "transfer funds" tells us *what* but not *how fast*, *how reliably*, or *how securely*. These quality attributes drive technology choices.
2. **Architectural tactics are NFR-driven.** Caching, replication, sharding, load balancing — all are deployed to satisfy *non-functional* concerns.
3. **FRs change with market, NFRs persist.** Architectures designed only on FRs collapse when a single NFR (e.g., "10× more users") emerges.
4. **NFRs create tradeoffs.** Speed vs. accuracy, security vs. usability, cost vs. availability. The architect's job is to *balance* these — impossible without explicit NFRs.
5. **KTU exam answer key emphasis:** *Quality attributes are the primary drivers of architectural decisions.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** MTBF = 4,380 h, MTTR = 1 h.

**(i) Availability calculation (2 marks):**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \;=\; \frac{4380}{4380 + 1} \;=\; \frac{4380}{4381} \;\approx\; 0.99977
$$

$$
A \;\approx\; 99.977\%
$$

This is approximately **"three-and-a-half nines"** (between 99.9% and 99.99%).

**(ii) Maximum MTTR for 99.99% availability (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the equation:**

$$
0.9999 \;=\; \frac{4380}{4380 + \text{MTTR}_{\text{new}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply:**

$$
0.9999 \cdot (4380 + \text{MTTR}_{\text{new}}) \;=\; 4380
$$

**Step 3 — Expand:**

$$
4380.5622 + 0.9999 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 4380
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate MTTR term:**

$$
0.9999 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 4380 - 4380.5622 \;=\; -0.5622
$$

**Step 5 — Recalculate with proper precision (algebraic shortcut):**

$$
\text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{4380}{0.9999} - 4380
$$

$$
\text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 4380.4380 - 4380.0000 \;=\; 0.4380 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to minutes:**

$$
\text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 0.4380 \times 60 \;\approx\; 26.28 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum allowable MTTR is approximately **26.3 minutes** (~26 minutes 17 seconds) to achieve 99.99% availability at MTBF = 4,380 h.

**(iii) Architectural tactics to reduce MTTR (2 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Active monitoring with auto-remediation** — deploy health checks and automated failover (e.g., Kubernetes liveness probes that restart unhealthy pods, AWS Route 53 health checks with DNS failover).
2. **Hot-standby / active-active deployment** — keep a second fully-warm instance ready in another Availability Zone, with data replicated synchronously. Failover is near-instantaneous.
3. **Blue-green deployment / canary releases** — enables rapid rollback within seconds if a faulty version is deployed.
4. **Pre-tested runbooks + automated incident response** — reduce human decision time during outages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing concerns with requirements** (loses 1–2 marks). Always remember: *concern = wish; requirement = measurable, testable, agreed statement.*
> 2. **Forgetting to justify ASR classification** (loses 1 mark per item in part b). A bare "Yes" without reasoning earns partial credit; with reasoning earns full marks.
> 3. **Unit mistakes in MTTR** (loses 1 mark). Always state the *unit* (hours, minutes) explicitly. KTU examiners look for unit clarity.
> 4. **Treating usability/style requirements as ASRs** (loses 1 mark). PEP-8, naming conventions, and UI colors are *not* architecturally significant.
> 5. **Not mentioning tradeoff resolution** (loses 2 marks). Any question on conflicting concerns requires a clear *resolution strategy*, not just listing both sides.
> 6. **Skipping the Onion Model layer placement** (loses 1 mark). If the question says "classify by Onion Model," you must *explicitly* name the layer.
> 7. **Writing requirements with prescribed solutions** (loses 1 mark). A requirement like *"use Redis for caching"* is a *design decision*, not a requirement. KTU marks this down under "design-free" violation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Rapid Revision Pack**

#### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions

- ✅ **Stakeholder** = anyone affected by or affecting the system.
- ✅ **Concern** = a stakeholder's *interest* or *goal* (subjective).
- ✅ **Requirement** = a *measurable, testable, agreed* statement of a need (objective).
- ✅ **ASR** = a requirement that *constrains* architecture.
- ✅ **FURPS+** = Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability + Constraints.

#### 🧠 Must-Know Models

- ✅ **Rozanski & Woods Onion Model** — 5 layers (Core, Inner, Middle, Outer, Outermost).
- ✅ **FURPS+ Model** — 5 main + 4 constraint sub-categories.
- ✅ **Power/Interest Grid** — 4 quadrants for stakeholder prioritization.
- ✅ **Requirements Pyramid** — Business → User → System → Functional/NFR.
- ✅ **SMART-FINEST** — quality criteria for well-formed requirements.

#### 📐 Must-Know Formulas

- ✅ Availability: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- ✅ "Nines" interpretation: 99% = 2 nines, 99.9% = 3 nines, 99.99% = 4 nines.
- ✅ Little's Law: $L = \lambda \cdot W$ (used for capacity/latency tradeoffs).

#### ⚠️ Frequently Confused Pairs (Exam Traps!)

- ⚠️ **Concern** vs. **Requirement** — concern is *what stakeholder wants*; requirement is *how we measure it*.
- ⚠️ **Functional** vs. **Non-Functional** — *what* vs. *how well*.
- ⚠️ **User Requirement** vs. **System Requirement** — natural language for stakeholders vs. technical for developers.
- ⚠️ **Constraint** vs. **NFR** — a constraint is a *hard limit on design*; an NFR is a *quality to be achieved*.
- ⚠️ **ASR** vs. *any* requirement — ASRs are the *small, critical* subset that drives architecture.

#### 🎯 High-Yield Points for 14-Mark Answers

- 🎯 Always show **stakeholder → concern → requirement → ASR → architectural decision** chain.
- 🎯 Always use the **SMART-FINEST** test when evaluating a requirement.
- 🎯 Always state **units** for every metric (ms, RPS, %, hours).
- 🎯 Always discuss **tradeoffs** when resolving conflicting concerns.
- 🎯 Always link a quality attribute to an **architectural tactic** (caching, replication, async, etc.).

#### 📋 Key Takeaway

> **The architect's role is not to satisfy every requirement equally — it is to *identify the few architecturally significant requirements* and design structures, tactics, and patterns that satisfy them, while documenting the tradeoffs made against all other requirements.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Cases and Tactics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use Cases and Tactics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Use Cases in Software Architecture

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **Use Case** is a sequence of actions performed by a system that yields an **observable result of value** to a particular actor. In the context of software architecture, use cases are scenarios of interaction between the system and external entities (actors) that are *architecturally significant* — meaning their successful (or failed) execution influences the achievement of one or more **quality attributes** of the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecturally Significant Use Case (ASUC):** Not every use case is architecturally relevant. An ASUC is one whose realization **exercises**, **constrains**, or **shapes** the architecture — typically by stressing availability, performance, modifiability, security, or usability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Use cases are the *bridge* between **functional requirements** and **quality attribute requirements (QARs)**. They act as the operational context in which architectural **tactics** are evaluated and selected.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)
Imagine a **hospital emergency room**:
- The **use case** is "treat a critical patient within 5 minutes of arrival."
- The hospital must decide *how* to achieve this — that decision involves choosing **tactics** (e.g., dedicated triage nurses, parallel diagnostic labs, redundant power supply, on-call specialist roster).
- The use case **describes the goal**; the tactics **describe the engineering moves** the architect makes to make that goal achievable.

Likewise, in software, the use case "process 10,000 transactions per second without data loss" forces the architect to pick tactics like *load balancing*, *replication*, *caching*, or *asynchronous messaging*.

---

## 1.2 Tactics in Software Architecture

### Formal Definition
A **tactic** is a fundamental architectural building block that influences the achievement of a **single quality attribute response measure**. Tactics are *primitive* design decisions (not full patterns) that, when composed, yield an architectural **strategy** for satisfying a quality attribute scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tactics vs. Patterns:** A *tactic* is a low-level design move. A *pattern* is a well-known package of multiple cooperating tactics. For example, the **Broker pattern** packages the *interception* and *dynamic dispatch* tactics together.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of tactics as **ingredients** in a recipe:
- A recipe (architecture strategy) is a combination of ingredients (tactics).
- Each ingredient contributes one specific property (quality attribute).
- You can mix the same ingredients in different ways to get different dishes (different architectural styles achieving different QAs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Tactics are **quality-attribute-specific**. An availability tactic does not, in general, improve modifiability, and a modifiability tactic may actively *harm* performance. This **orthogonality** is what makes the tactic catalog usable.

---

## 1.3 The Relationship: Use Cases → Tactics

| Element | Role in Architecture | Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **Use Case** | Operational context | *What must the system do, and under what conditions?* |
| **Quality Attribute** | Goal to optimize | *How well must the system do it?* |
| **Tactic** | Architectural decision | *How will we engineer the system to achieve that "how well"?* |
| **Pattern** | Bundled tactics | *What is a proven, reusable combination of those moves?* |

The flow is:

$$\text{Use Case} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quality Attribute Scenario} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Tactic Selection} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pattern/Strategy}$$

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Is High-Yield in KTU Exams

Examiners at KTU (under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme) consistently test:
1. The ability to **map a use case to a quality attribute** it stresses.
2. The ability to **list, classify, and justify tactics** for a given QA.
3. The ability to **explain trade-offs** — because tactics are not free; every tactic has a side effect on other QAs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Quality Attribute Scenario

Before selecting tactics, the architect converts a use case into a **Quality Attribute Scenario** (QAS). A QAS has six parts — students must memorize this:

| Part | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Stimulus** | Who/what initiates | End user, external system, clock |
| **Stimulus** | The event | "Submits 10,000 order requests in 5 sec" |
| **Environment** | Condition under which it occurs | Normal load, overload, failure mode |
| **Artifact** | What is stimulated | System, subsystem, component |
| **Response** | What happens | Processes the orders, queues them, rejects them |
| **Response Measure** | Quantified result | Latency ≤ 200 ms, throughput ≥ 2000 req/s |

> [!NOTE]
> A **general scenario** is system-independent (e.g., *The system shall be available 24/7*). An **actual scenario** is system-specific and concrete (*The trading system shall process 99.99% of orders within 50 ms during market open hours*). Tactics address **actual** scenarios.

---

## 2.2 The Six Quality Attribute Categories and Their Tactics

The KTU syllabus (and the Bass-Clements-Kazman canonical reference) groups tactics under six primary quality attributes. Below is the **complete cheat sheet**.

### 2.2.1 Availability Tactics

| Category | Tactic | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault Detection** | Ping/Echo, Heartbeat, Watchdog | Detect liveness |
| **Fault Recovery** | Active Redundancy (hot/warm/cold spare), Passive Redundancy, Rollback | Substitute failed component |
| **Fault Prevention** | Transactions, Removal from Service, Process Monitor | Prevent fault propagation |
| **Fault/Treatment** | Replicas, Micro-reboots, Sanity checking | Repair in-place |

### 2.2.2 Modifiability Tactics

| Category | Tactic | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Reduce Size of Change** | Decompose, Localize, Generalize, Use Intermediary | Smaller, localized edits |
| **Increase Cohesion** | Increase semantic coherence | Cleaner modules |
| **Reduce Coupling** | Encapsulate, Restrict Dependencies, Refactor, Abstract Common Services | Looser connections |
| **Defer Binding** | Runtime Registration, Configuration Files, Polymorphism, Component Replacement, Scripting | Bind later |
