### 2.2.3 Performance Tactics

| Category | Tactic | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand** | Reduce Work, Increase Computational Efficiency, Reduce Contention | Process less per request |
| **Resource** | Increase Resources, Increase Shared Resources, Schedule Resources | Add capacity |
| **Resource Management** | Concurrency, Maintain Multiple Copies, Add More Bandwidth | Parallelize |

### 2.2.4 Security Tactics

| Category | Tactic | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Resist** | Authenticate, Authorize, Limit Access, Harden | Keep attacker out |
| **Detect** | Detect Intrusion, Detect Service Denial, Verify Message Integrity, Detect Message Replay | Find the attack |
| **React** | Audit, Restore, Counter-Attack | Recover & respond |
| **Recover** | Maintain Audit Trail, Restore State | Post-incident recovery |

### 2.2.5 Testability Tactics

| Category | Tactic | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Control & Observe** | Limit Complexity, Limit Non-Determinism, Limit State, Encapsulate, Separate Interface from Implementation | Make state observable |
| **Reduce Test Cost** | Record/Playback, Localize State Storage, Abstract Data Sources, Sandbox, Automate | Cheaper to test |

### 2.2.6 Usability Tactics

| Category | Tactic | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Runtime** | Maintain Task Model, Maintain User Model, Maintain System Model, Anticipate User Error | Adaptive feedback |
| **User Interface** | Group, Hide, Defer, Migrate, Instantiate, Search, Aggregate, Filter, Reorganize, Translate | UI affordances |
| **Support** | Cached Content, Page Generator, Visual Aids, Context-Sensitive Help | Aid the user |

---

## 2.3 The Architectural Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **strategy** is the *combination* of one or more tactics chosen to satisfy a specific QAS. The architect selects tactics, evaluates trade-offs, and the resulting bundle is the strategy. **A pattern is a named, documented strategy.**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Notation Cheat Sheet

Since architecture is largely qualitative, the "formulas" here are **decision rules** and **mapping relations** — not physical equations. Memorize the following:

$$
\text{Use Case} \;\mapsto\; \text{Quality Attribute Scenario (QAS)}
$$

$$
\text{QAS} \;\mapsto\; \{\text{Tactic}_1, \text{Tactic}_2, \dots, \text{Tactic}_n\}
$$

$$
\text{Strategy} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Tactic}_i
$$

$$
\text{Trade-off} = \Delta \text{QA}_{\text{primary}} - \sum_{j \ne \text{primary}} \Delta \text{QA}_j
$$

> A positive trade-off means the tactic improves the target QA more than it harms the others.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\Delta \text{QA}$ | Change in a quality attribute (positive = improvement) |
| $\mapsto$ | "is mapped to" / "leads to" |
| $\cup$ | Union of decisions (a strategy) |
| $\sum$ | Sum of side-effect impacts on other QAs |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case → Tactic Example |
|---|---|
| **E-Commerce** | "Process 5,000 orders/sec during sale" → *Load balancing + caching + read replica* |
| **Healthcare HIS** | "Patient record available 24/7" → *Active redundancy + heartbeat + rollback* |
| **Banking** | "Unauthorized access prevented" → *Authenticate + Authorize + Audit + Encrypt* |
| **Avionics** | "Software certifiable to DO-178C" → *Localize + Abstract + Separate Interface* |
| **IoT Edge** | "Firmware updatable without service interruption" → *Hot deployment + process monitor* |

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU 2024 Scheme projects** (B.Tech final-year), evaluators expect to see an explicit **tactic table** in the design document linking each architecturally significant use case to the chosen tactics and their trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: From Use Case to Architectural Strategy

**Scenario:** You are designing the architecture of an **Online Food Delivery Platform (Zomato-like)**. One of the use cases is:

> *"During lunch peak (12:00–14:00), the system shall successfully accept and dispatch at least 10,000 customer orders per minute, with no order being lost and order placement latency not exceeding 3 seconds under normal operation."*

### Step 1 — Identify the Use Case Components
- **Actor:** Customer (mobile app) and Restaurant (dashboard)
- **Precondition:** User authenticated, restaurant online
- **Postcondition:** Order placed, restaurant notified, payment processed
- **Stakeholder interest:** Customer satisfaction, revenue

### Step 2 — Extract the Quality Attributes Implied

We perform a **stakeholder-to-QA mapping**:

| Stakeholder Statement | Implied QA |
|---|---|
| "10,000 orders per minute" | **Performance** (throughput) |
| "No order being lost" | **Availability** / **Reliability** |
| "Latency not exceeding 3 seconds" | **Performance** (response time) |
| "During peak hours" | **Performance** (under load) |

### Step 3 — Convert to a Quality Attribute Scenario (QAS)

Applying the six-part template:

- **Source of Stimulus:** External — 10,000 concurrent customer clients
- **Stimulus:** Submit order requests
- **Environment:** Peak load (12:00–14:00), normal operation
- **Artifact:** Order Placement Service
- **Response:** Accept, persist, notify restaurant
- **Response Measure:** Throughput ≥ 10,000 orders/min; p99 latency ≤ 3 s; 0% data loss

### Step 4 — Apply the Tactic Selection Algorithm

The selection algorithm is a 3-phase decision process:

**Phase A — Classify the QA category:**
- Primary QA = **Performance**
- Secondary QA = **Availability**

**Phase B — For each QA, list candidate tactics from the catalog:**

For Performance:
1. Reduce Work
2. Increase Computational Efficiency
3. Reduce Contention
4. Increase Resources
5. Increase Shared Resources
6. Concurrency
7. Maintain Multiple Copies (caching)
8. Add Bandwidth
9. Schedule Resources

For Availability:
1. Ping/Echo
2. Heartbeat
3. Active Redundancy
4. Transactions
5. Process Monitor

**Phase C — Select tactics based on cost/benefit and constraint feasibility:**

| Tactic | Justification | Cost / Side Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Concurrency** | Handle 10k requests in parallel via async I/O | Adds threading complexity (hurts *modifiability*) |
| **Maintain Multiple Copies (Cache)** | Reduce DB hits; order lookups cached | Cache invalidation complexity |
| **Increase Resources (horizontal scale)** | Add more app servers behind LB | Higher infra cost |
| **Schedule Resources (priority queue)** | Premium users prioritized | Possible fairness issues (hurts *usability*) |
| **Transactions** | Guarantee "no order lost" | Slight latency overhead |
| **Active Redundancy (DB replicas)** | Survive DB failure | Replication lag must be bounded |
| **Process Monitor** | Auto-restart crashed workers | None significant |

**Phase D — Compose the Strategy:**

$$
\text{Strategy}_{\text{Order}} = \{\text{Concurrency},\ \text{Cache},\ \text{Horizontal Scale},\ \text{Transactions},\ \text{Active Redundancy},\ \text{Process Monitor}\}
$$

### Step 5 — Trade-off Evaluation

Applying the trade-off formula:

$$
\text{Trade-off} = \Delta \text{QA}_{\text{primary}} - \sum_{j \ne \text{primary}} \Delta \text{QA}_j
$$

Let us assume the candidate strategy gives the following estimated deltas (normalized, on a 1–10 scale):

| QA | Tactic Impact | Δ |
|---|---|---|
| Performance (primary) | Major improvement | +9 |
| Availability | Major improvement | +7 |
| Modifiability | Slight degradation | $-2$ |
| Security | Neutral | 0 |
| Usability | Slight degradation (priority queue) | $-1$ |
| Testability | Slight degradation (async) | $-2$ |

$$
\text{Trade-off} = (+9) - \left[(-2) + 0 + (-1) + (-2)\right] = 9 - (-5) = 14
$$

Since the trade-off is strongly positive, the strategy is **accepted**.

> [!NOTE]
> A negative trade-off does not automatically reject a tactic — it triggers a **re-design iteration**: replace the offending tactic or compensate with another tactic that mitigates the side effect.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Use Case → Tactic Mapping Reference

| Use Case Type | Stressed QA | Primary Tactics |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time bidding in ad-tech | Performance, Availability | Concurrency, Cache, Redundancy |
| Patient record lookup in HIS | Availability, Security | Redundancy, Authenticate, Authorize |
| Adding a new payment gateway | Modifiability | Encapsulate, Abstract Common Services, Polymorphism |
| Live streaming of sports | Performance, Modifiability | Cache (CDN), Increase Bandwidth, Decompose |
| ATM cash withdrawal | Availability, Security, Performance | Heartbeat, Authorize, Concurrency |
| Online exam (1 lakh students simultaneously) | Performance, Availability, Security | Load Balance, Active Redundancy, Authenticate, Concurrency |
| Smart thermostat firmware OTA update | Modifiability, Availability | Hot Deployment, Defer Binding, Rollback |
| Self-driving car lane detection | Performance, Availability | Concurrency, Active Redundancy, Increase Resources |

---

## 3.3 Common Pitfalls in the Derivation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **tactics with patterns**. If the question says "list tactics," writing "Layered architecture" or "MVC" is a **0-mark answer** because those are *patterns*. You must write primitive moves like "encapsulate," "defer binding," "concurrency."

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2:** Listing tactics without **justifying** them against the use case. The examiner expects a line like *"Concurrency is selected because the use case states 10,000 concurrent submissions, which a sequential handler cannot meet."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3:** Forgetting **side-effects**. Every tactic you propose must be followed by a sentence on *which other QA it might hurt* (e.g., caching hurts modifiability; active redundancy hurts performance due to sync cost).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Flow: Use Case → QAS → Tactic → Strategy

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete decision pipeline from an architecturally significant use case to a documented architectural strategy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UC1[Architecturally Significant Use Case]
    STK[Stakeholder Concern]
    QA1{Quality Attribute Identified?}
    QAS[Quality Attribute Scenario with Six Parts]
    TC[Tactic Catalog Lookup]
    SEL[Tactic Selection with Justification]
    TO[Trade-off Analysis]
    STR[Composed Architectural Strategy]
    PAT[Mapping to Known Pattern Optional]
    DOC[Documented in SAD Software Architecture Document]

    UC1 --> QA1
    STK --> QA1
    QA1 -- Yes --> QAS
    QAS --> TC
    TC --> SEL
    SEL --> TO
    TO -- Positive Trade off --> STR
    TO -- Negative Trade off --> SEL
    STR --> PAT
    PAT --> DOC
    STR --> DOC
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Quality Attribute Tactic Catalog

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QA[Quality Attribute] --> AV[Availability]
    QA --> MO[Modifiability]
    QA --> PE[Performance]
    QA --> SE[Security]
    QA --> TE[Testability]
    QA --> US[Usability]

    AV --> AV1[Fault Detection]
    AV --> AV2[Fault Recovery]
    AV --> AV3[Fault Prevention]

    MO --> MO1[Reduce Size of Change]
    MO --> MO2[Increase Cohesion]
    MO --> MO3[Reduce Coupling]
    MO --> MO4[Defer Binding]

    PE --> PE1[Reduce Demand]
    PE --> PE2[Manage Resources]
    PE --> PE3[Resource Management]

    SE --> SE1[Resist Attacks]
    SE --> SE2[Detect Attacks]
    SE --> SE3[React to Attacks]
    SE --> SE4[Recover from Attacks]

    TE --> TE1[Control and Observe State]
    TE --> TE2[Reduce Test Cost]

    US --> US1[Runtime Tactics]
    US --> US2[User Interface Tactics]
    US --> US3[User Support Tactics]
```

## 4.3 Use Case to Tactic Mapping (Example: Order Placement System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UC[Use Case: Place Order During Peak Load]
    QAS[QAS: 10k orders per min, 3s latency, no loss]
    T1[Tactic: Concurrency]
    T2[Tactic: Caching of Catalog]
    T3[Tactic: Horizontal Scaling with LB]
    T4[Tactic: DB Active Redundancy]
    T5[Tactic: Process Monitor and Heartbeat]
    T6[Tactic: Transactions for Atomicity]
    STR[Strategy: Scalable Resilient Order Pipeline]
    PAT[Realizes Pattern: Bulkhead plus Active Redundancy]

    UC --> QAS
    QAS --> T1
    QAS --> T2
    QAS --> T3
    QAS --> T4
    QAS --> T5
    QAS --> T6
    T1 --> STR
    T2 --> STR
    T3 --> STR
    T4 --> STR
    T5 --> STR
    T6 --> STR
    STR --> PAT
```

## 4.4 Trade-off Analysis Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TACTIC[Selected Tactic]
    POS[Positive Impact on Target QA]
    NEG[Side Effect on Other QAs]
    NET[Net Trade off Score]

    TACTIC --> POS
    TACTIC --> NEG
    POS --> NET
    NEG --> NET
    NET -- Score greater than 0 --> ACC[Accept Tactic]
    NET -- Score less than or equal to 0 --> REJ[Reject or Replace Tactic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each "block" represents a decision node. The flows are left-to-right (cause → effect) and the dashed decision branches represent the conditional acceptance/rejection that drives an iterative design cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an "architecturally significant use case." Give one example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An architecturally significant use case (ASUC) is a use case whose realization **exercises, constrains, or shapes** the software architecture — typically by stressing one or more quality attributes such as performance, availability, modifiability, or security.

*Example:* In an **online banking system**, the use case *"Transfer ₹50,000 between two accounts atomically, with no double-spend, in under 2 seconds"* is architecturally significant because it forces the architect to choose availability, security, and performance tactics (active redundancy, transactions, authentication, concurrency). A non-significant use case such as *"User updates display theme"* has no architectural consequences and is usually deferred to UI design.

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark justification of "significance" + 1 mark example with QA mapping.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between a tactic and a pattern. Why does the architect prefer to think in terms of tactics? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tactic | Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Primitive, single design move | Package of multiple cooperating tactics |
| Scope | Affects one QA response measure | Addresses combined concerns |
| Reusability | Low (compositional unit) | High (named, documented) |
| Example | "Cache reads" | "Layered architecture" |

The architect thinks in tactics because:
1. **Fine-grained control** — patterns are rigid; tactics allow custom composition.
2. **Trade-off transparency** — each tactic's side effects on other QAs are explicitly visible, whereas patterns often hide them.
3. **Catalog reasoning** — tactics can be selected systematically from the QA-specific catalog.

> **Mark distribution:** 1.5 marks for the table + 1.5 marks for the three reasons.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below we provide **Question A** and **Question B** — the student answers one of the two.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Use Case to Tactic Mapping with Trade-off Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** [7 marks] For an **IoT-based smart traffic management system** with the following use case, identify the quality attribute scenario (QAS) and list the **availability and performance tactics** you would choose, with one-line justifications for each.

> **Use Case:** *"During a city-level emergency (e.g., VIP convoy or accident), the system shall dynamically re-route traffic signals for 200 intersections within 5 seconds of receiving the command, while continuing to monitor all sensors with less than 1% packet loss."*

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **trade-offs** of the chosen tactics on other quality attributes (modifiability, security, usability). If a tactic has a major negative trade-off, propose a **compensating tactic**.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Extract QAS (2 marks)**

| Part | Value |
|---|---|
| Source | Central Command Server |
| Stimulus | Emergency reroute command |
| Environment | City-wide emergency, normal operation, possibly heavy load |
| Artifact | Intersection Controller Subsystem |
| Response | Reroute, continue monitoring |
| Response Measure | ≤ 5 s latency, < 1% packet loss, 100% command delivery |

**Step 2: Availability Tactics (2.5 marks)**

1. **Active Redundancy (Hot Spare) for Central Server** — Ensures reroute command source survives failure. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Heartbeat between Intersections and Server** — Detects failed intersection nodes quickly. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Transactions for Command Distribution** — Guarantees "all-or-nothing" delivery of reroute command. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Process Monitor / Watchdog** — Restarts crashed intersection firmware. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Rollback for faulty reroute** — Reverts to last good signal plan if reroute fails. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 3: Performance Tactics (2.5 marks)**

1. **Concurrency (asynchronous I/O)** — 200 intersections handled in parallel. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Increase Resources (scale out)** — Multiple command-dispatcher workers. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Reduce Contention (message queue)** — Decouples command generation from execution. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Schedule Resources (priority queue)** — Emergency reroute command jumps queue. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Maintain Multiple Copies (in-memory cache of intersection state)** — Reduces lookup time. *(0.5 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Trade-off Table (4 marks — 0.5 each row):**

| Tactic | Positive Impact | Side Effect on Other QA | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active Redundancy | Availability +++ | Performance - (sync cost); Modifiability - (replication logic) | Medium |
| Heartbeat | Availability ++ | Network bandwidth consumed; minor perf hit | Low |
| Transactions | Availability + / Reliability +++ | Performance - (latency overhead) | Medium |
| Concurrency | Performance +++ | Modifiability -- (threading complexity); Testability -- | High |
| Priority Queue | Performance ++ (for emergencies) | Usability -- (non-emergency traffic may starve) | Medium |
| Caching of State | Performance ++ | Modifiability -- (cache invalidation) | Medium |
| Process Monitor | Availability ++ | None significant | Low |
| Rollback | Availability ++ | Modifiability -- (state history) | Low |

**Compensating Tactics (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. To **offset Concurrency's hit on Modifiability/Testability:** add the tactic **"Encapsulate concurrency behind an interface"** — wrap thread pools behind a clean façade so business logic is unaware.
2. To **offset Priority Queue's hit on Usability:** add the tactic **"Maintain User Model"** — predict and pre-empt starvation for non-emergency intersections by allocating a guaranteed minimum bandwidth.
3. To **offset Active Redundancy's sync cost on Performance:** add the tactic **"Schedule Resources"** — synchronize replicas asynchronously, accepting eventual consistency for non-critical telemetry.

> **Conclusion (built into (b) for full marks):** The final strategy yields net positive trade-off because the primary QA (availability + performance for emergency) gains outweigh the secondary losses, and the compensating tactics restore the lost QAs to acceptable levels.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Tactics for Modifiability and Security `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **modifiability tactics** under the three categories — *Reduce Size of Change*, *Reduce Coupling*, and *Defer Binding* — with a one-line example for each tactic. Map a use case *"Add support for a new payment gateway (e.g., UPI, Apple Pay) in an e-commerce platform within 2 sprints without affecting existing flows"* to specific modifiability tactics.

**(b)** [7 marks] For the same e-commerce platform, the use case *"Unauthorized user must not be able to view another user's order history even with valid session token"* falls under which quality attribute? List and justify the **security tactics** under *Resist*, *Detect*, *React*, and *Recover* that you would deploy.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Modifiability Tactic Categories (4 marks):**

**Reduce Size of Change:**
- **Decompose** — Split the module into smaller responsibilities. *Example:* Separate "Payment" into "PaymentGatewayInterface," "PaymentValidator," "PaymentRecorder."
- **Localize** — Keep changes within one module. *Example:* Add new gateway code in a new sub-package, not the core order module.
- **Generalize** — Make the existing module more general so the change is a parameter. *Example:* Read gateway type from a config file.

**Reduce Coupling:**
- **Encapsulate** — Hide implementation behind an interface. *Example:* `IPaymentGateway` interface with `processPayment()` method.
- **Restrict Dependencies** — Limit which modules can call payment module. *Example:* Only `OrderService` can call it.
- **Abstract Common Services** — Create a shared abstraction for similar gateways. *Example:* `AbstractPaymentAdapter` base class.

**Defer Binding:**
- **Runtime Registration** — Register the new gateway at startup via a plugin loader.
- **Configuration Files** — Specify gateway in `application.yml`.
- **Polymorphism** — All gateways implement a common interface; client code is unaware of the concrete class.

**2. Mapping to the Use Case (3 marks — 1 mark per tactic group):**

| Use Case Step | Tactic Group | Specific Tactic |
|---|---|---|
| New gateway plugs in without core change | **Reduce Coupling** | Encapsulate (use interface) |
| Existing flows unaffected | **Reduce Size of Change** | Localize changes to payment module |
| Add a fourth gateway later with same effort | **Defer Binding** | Runtime Registration + Configuration File |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**1. Quality Attribute Identification (1 mark):**
This use case is about **Security** (specifically *Confidentiality* and *Authorization*). The system must **resist** unauthorized access to data and **detect/react** to attempts.

**2. Security Tactics (6 marks — 1.5 marks per category):**

**Resist Tactics:**
- **Authenticate** — Verify the user's identity (multi-factor, OAuth2 token).
- **Authorize** — Role-based access control (RBAC); ensure `userId` in URL/session matches the order owner.
- **Limit Access / Limit Exposure** — Use server-side authorization checks on every request; never trust client-side data.

**Detect Tactics:**
- **Detect Intrusion** — Log every access attempt with timestamp, IP, userId, and resource accessed.
- **Verify Message Integrity** — Use signed tokens (JWT with HMAC) to prevent tampering.
- **Detect Service Denial** — Rate-limit requests to `/order/history` per user.

**React Tactics:**
- **Audit** — Generate audit trails for forensic analysis.
- **Counter-Attack / Block** — Temporarily block IP/user after repeated failed attempts.

**Recover Tactics:**
- **Restore State** — If breach detected, invalidate all active sessions.
- **Maintain Audit Trail** — Retain logs for legal/regulatory compliance (e.g., IT Act, GDPR).

> **Tactic → Mechanism Map (built-in to (b)):**

| Tactic | Mechanism in Stack |
|---|---|
| Authenticate | OAuth2, OpenID Connect |
| Authorize | Spring Security `@PreAuthorize`, Casbin policy engine |
| Detect Intrusion | SIEM (Splunk, ELK), WAF (ModSecurity) |
| Verify Integrity | JWT with HMAC/RSA signing |
| Audit | Append-only log, hash-chained records |
| Recover | Session store flush, key rotation |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students lose marks on this topic (cumulative loss: 6–10 marks out of 14):**
>
> 1. **Confusing patterns with tactics** — Writing "MVC," "Layered," or "Microservices" when the question asks for *tactics* results in **0 marks**. Always decompose the pattern into its constituent tactics in your answer.
>
> 2. **Listing tactics without QA linkage** — "Concurrency" without saying "to meet 10,000 req/min" is incomplete. Always state **which QAS** the tactic addresses.
>
> 3. **Ignoring trade-offs** — In 14-mark questions, KTU explicitly tests whether the student understands that **tactics are not free**. A solution with no trade-off discussion loses 3–4 marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the QAS** — Jumping directly from use case to tactics is a 2-mark loss. The 6-part QAS must appear in the answer.
>
> 5. **Using vague modifiers** — "Improve performance," "increase security" are not tactics. Use **canonical names** from the catalog: "concurrency," "active redundancy," "authenticate," "defer binding." Examiners have a checklist of keywords.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use Cases and Tactics**

- [ ] A **Use Case** is an *operational sequence*; an **ASUC** is one that influences quality attributes.
- [ ] A **Quality Attribute Scenario** has exactly **six parts**: source, stimulus, environment, artifact, response, response measure.
- [ ] A **Tactic** is a *primitive design move* affecting one QA; a **Pattern** is a *package of tactics*.
- [ ] Six QA categories: **Availability, Modifiability, Performance, Security, Testability, Usability.**
- [ ] **Availability** = Fault Detection, Recovery, Prevention, Treatment.
- [ ] **Modifiability** = Reduce Size, Reduce Coupling, Increase Cohesion, Defer Binding.
- [ ] **Performance** = Reduce Demand, Increase Resources, Resource Management.
- [ ] **Security** = Resist, Detect, React, Recover.
- [ ] **Testability** = Control/Observe State, Reduce Test Cost.
- [ ] **Usability** = Runtime, User Interface, Support.
- [ ] **Strategy** = composed tactics; **Pattern** = named strategy.
- [ ] **Trade-off formula:** $\text{Trade-off} = \Delta \text{QA}_{\text{primary}} - \sum \Delta \text{QA}_{\text{others}}$
- [ ] Always link a tactic to the **specific QAS** it addresses.
- [ ] Always discuss the **side effects** on **other** QAs.
- [ ] The decision flow is: **Use Case → QAS → Tactic Selection → Trade-off → Strategy → (Optional) Pattern.**
- [ ] KTU expects the **canonical tactic names** verbatim — not paraphrases.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In every 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer as:
> 1. QAS with 6 parts *(2 marks)*
> 2. Tactic table with justifications *(5 marks)*
> 3. Trade-off analysis with compensating tactics *(4 marks)*
> 4. Mapping to a known pattern or final strategy statement *(3 marks)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Architectural Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Pattern (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> An **Architectural Pattern** is a high-level, reusable, well-established solution to a recurring class of problems in software architecture. It represents a template for the gross-level structural organization of a software system, providing a set of predefined subsystems, their responsibilities, the rules governing their relationships, and the mechanisms by which they communicate and coordinate.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, architectural patterns are studied under the broader umbrella of **software design tactics** that govern **modularity**, **scalability**, **availability**, and **performance** — the core quality attributes (QAs) of any engineered software product.

A pattern differs from a **style**, a **framework**, and a **tactic** as shown below:

| Term | Granularity | KTU Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Architectural Pattern** | Macro | A proven structural solution applied across many systems (e.g., Layered) |
| **Architectural Style** | Macro | A named collection of design decisions (e.g., REST, Publish-Subscribe) |
| **Design Pattern** | Micro | A solution to a localized, class/object-level problem (GoF patterns) |
| **Framework** | Concrete | A semi-complete, executable skeleton of code |
| **Tactic** | Atomic | A single, focused technique influencing a single quality attribute |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Architectural Patterns like "Building Blueprints" for software.**
> Just as a civil engineer selects a blueprint based on the land (a skyscraper for dense cities, a bungalow for a quiet suburb), a software architect selects an architectural pattern based on the problem constraints (a Layered pattern for a banking app, a Peer-to-Peer pattern for a torrent client).
>
> The **blueprint (pattern)** does not give you the exact building — it gives you the **walls, pillars, room layout, and wiring plan** that have *worked before* under similar conditions.

### 1.3 Why Architectural Patterns Matter (KTU 2024 Perspective)

- **Reusability** — Proven designs reduce reinvention.
- **Communication** — A shared vocabulary between architects, developers, and stakeholders (e.g., saying "Use a Broker pattern" immediately conveys structure).
- **Quality Attribute Trade-offs** — Each pattern biases the system toward specific QAs (e.g., Pipe-Filter favors *modifiability*; Master-Slave favors *performance & fault-tolerance*).
- **Risk Mitigation** — Patterns encode lessons learned from past failures (e.g., why a Blackboard pattern is unsuitable for hard real-time systems).

### 1.4 Key Quality Attributes Influenced by Patterns

- **Modifiability** — Ease of changing the system.
- **Performance** — Latency and throughput characteristics.
- **Availability** — Time the system is operational and accessible.
- **Scalability** — Ability to handle load increases.
- **Security** — Confidentiality, integrity, and non-repudiation.
- **Testability** — Ease of verifying behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Architectural Patterns (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2) groups the following patterns as core study topics. Each pattern is analyzed in terms of **Structure**, **Context**, **Problem**, **Solution**, and **Consequences**.

### 2.1.1 From Mud to Structure (Layered Pattern)

- **Intent**: Decompose a system into a hierarchy of layers, where each layer provides services to the layer above and consumes services of the layer below.
- **Canonical Layers** (Bottom to Top):
  1. **Hardware/OS Layer** — Physical resources.
  2. **Data Management / Persistence Layer** — Database interactions.
  3. **Domain / Business Logic Layer** — Core application rules.
  4. **Application / Service Layer** — Workflow coordination.
  5. **Presentation / UI Layer** — User interface logic.
- **Why?**: Provides a clean **separation of concerns (SoC)**, supports *incremental development* and *testability*.
- **Trade-off**: Layered systems can suffer from **"sinkhole anti-pattern"** where requests pass through layers without meaningful logic at each layer (wasted CPU cycles).

### 2.1.2 Distributed Coordination (Client-Server & Master-Slave)

- **Client-Server Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: Clients send requests over a network to a server that provides services.
  - **Use Case**: Web applications, email, databases.
  - **Key Benefit**: Centralized data and logic; easy to enforce security.
  - **Drawback**: **Single point of failure** at the server; scalability requires server clustering.

- **Master-Slave Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: One **Master** distributes work to multiple **Slaves**; results are aggregated by the master.
  - **Use Case**: Database replication, parallel computing, fault-tolerant systems.
  - **Key Benefit**: **Parallelism** boosts performance; slave failure does not halt the system (failover to backup slave).
  - **Drawback**: Increased complexity; slaves may be **idle** if work distribution is poor.

### 2.1.3 Data Flow Patterns (Pipe-Filter & Process Control)

- **Pipe-Filter Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: Data flows through a series of **Filters** (transformers) connected by **Pipes** (conduits).
  - **Use Case**: Unix shell pipelines, compilers (lexical → syntax → semantic analysis).
  - **Key Benefit**: **Composability** (filters can be rearranged); supports **concurrent execution** of independent filters.
  - **Drawback**: State-sharing and interactive processing are difficult; overhead of inter-filter data transfer.

### 2.1.4 Independent Components (Microkernel, Plug-in, Reflection)

- **Microkernel Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: A minimal core ("kernel") delegates functionality to plug-in components.
  - **Use Case**: Eclipse IDE, OSGi, web browsers.
  - **Key Benefit**: High **extensibility** and **portability**.
  - **Drawback**: Difficult to define clean contracts for plug-ins; versioning of plug-ins is challenging.

### 2.1.5 Interactive Systems (MVC, PAC, MVP, MVVM)

- **Model-View-Controller (MVC)**:
  - **Model** — Encapsulates data and business rules.
  - **View** — Renders the Model into a form suitable for interaction.
  - **Controller** — Receives input, translates it to Model/View commands.
  - **Use Case**: Web frameworks (Ruby on Rails, Django).
  - **Key Benefit**: Decouples presentation from business logic; multiple views over the same model.

### 2.1.6 Messaging & Coordination (Event-Bus, Publish-Subscribe, Broker)

- **Broker Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: Decouples clients from servers using a **broker** that routes messages between **clients**, **servers**, and **proxies**.
  - **Use Case**: CORBA, web services via SOAP brokers, Apache Kafka.
  - **Key Benefit**: **Location transparency**; components can change location without affecting clients.

### 2.1.7 Adaptive Systems (Microkernel, Reflection)

- **Reflection / Meta-Level Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: The system contains a **base level** (application logic) and a **meta level** (information about the base). The meta level can modify the base level dynamically.
  - **Use Case**: Self-adaptive systems, IDEs, debuggers.

### 2.1.8 Peer Collaboration (Peer-to-Peer)

- **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: All nodes (peers) are equal; each can act as both client and server.
  - **Use Case**: File sharing (BitTorrent), blockchain, distributed computing (BOINC).
  - **Key Benefit**: Highly **scalable** and **resilient** (no central point of failure).
  - **Drawback**: Security and quality-of-service are harder to guarantee.

### 2.1.9 Specialized (Blackboard, Interpreter)

- **Blackboard Pattern**:
  - **Structure**: Multiple specialized knowledge sources (KS) read/write to a shared **blackboard** (shared data structure). A **controller** orchestrates which KS activates next.
  - **Use Case**: AI/ML systems (speech recognition, image processing), signal processing.
  - **Key Benefit**: Effective for problems where no deterministic solution is known (heuristic-based).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Summary Table)

| Pattern | Primary QA Addressed | Best Use Case | Major Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layered** | Modifiability, Portability | Enterprise business apps | Sinkhole anti-pattern |
| **Client-Server** | Security, Availability | Web apps, email | Server = single point of failure |
| **Master-Slave** | Performance, Fault-tolerance | DB replication, parallel tasks | Idle slaves, complex coordination |
| **Pipe-Filter** | Modifiability, Reusability | Compilers, ETL pipelines | Poor for interactive apps |
| **Microkernel** | Extensibility, Portability | IDEs, OSGi, browsers | Hard plug-in contract design |
| **MVC** | Modifiability, Testability | Web frameworks | Steep learning curve for newcomers |
| **Broker** | Interoperability, Scalability | Distributed object systems | Broker = bottleneck & SPOF |
| **Event-Bus** | Modifiability, Reusability | GUI apps, IoT | Debugging complexity |
| **P2P** | Scalability, Reliability | File sharing, blockchain | Security & QoS challenges |
| **Blackboard** | Performance, Accuracy (heuristic) | AI, signal processing | Hard to test deterministically |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip**: Always mention *which quality attribute is optimized* and *one anti-pattern risk* when describing an architectural pattern. Examiners award 2 marks automatically for these two points.

---

## 2.3 The "Pattern Selection Decision Flow" (KTU Mental Model)

When choosing a pattern, an architect walks through the following questions:

1. **Is the system a single machine or distributed?**  
   → Single: Layered / Microkernel / MVC.  
   → Distributed: Client-Server / Broker / P2P.

2. **Does the workload need parallel processing?**  
   → Yes: Master-Slave / Pipe-Filter.

3. **Is the problem deterministic or heuristic?**  
   → Deterministic: Pipe-Filter / Layered.  
   → Heuristic: Blackboard.

4. **Is extensibility the topmost concern?**  
   → Yes: Microkernel / Reflection.

5. **Is the system interactive (UI-driven)?**  
   → Yes: MVC / MVP / PAC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Example

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Pattern Comparison Using the KTU 2024 Rubric

The KTU 2024 scheme typically tests whether a student can **compare two patterns** along fixed dimensions. The following table is the standard analytical matrix a 14-mark answer must produce.

| Comparison Axis | Layered Pattern | Pipe-Filter Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Hierarchical layers | Linear chain of filters + pipes |
| **Communication** | Method calls (top-down / bottom-up) | Streamed data through pipes |
| **Concurrency** | Mostly sequential | High concurrency between independent filters |
| **Modifiability** | High (one layer change isolated) | High (filters swappable) |
| **State Management** | Layers may hold state | Each filter ideally stateless |
| **Latency** | Higher (request hops layers) | Lower (can stream data) |
| **Interactive Use** | Suitable | Unsuitable (no shared state easily) |
| **Anti-pattern** | Sinkhole | Tight coupling via shared global state |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: "Selecting an Architecture for a Real-Time Stock Trading Engine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark scenario)**
> A financial firm wants a system that ingests real-time market feeds, validates the data, computes risk metrics, and displays them on a trading dashboard. The system must handle **10,000 transactions per second** with **end-to-end latency under 5 ms**. It must also be possible to add new risk-calculation algorithms without shutting the system down.

### Step 1 — Identify the Quality Attributes (QA)

- **Performance (latency)**: 5 ms budget.
- **Throughput**: 10,000 TPS.
- **Modifiability**: Hot-deploy new algorithms.
- **Availability**: 99.999% (5 minutes downtime per year).
- **Accuracy**: Risk calculations must be correct.

### Step 2 — Map QAs to Candidate Patterns

We have a data-streaming problem with sequential transformations: **Pipe-Filter** is the natural choice. The hot-deploy requirement points toward **Microkernel** for the algorithm layer.

### Step 3 — Hybrid Architectural Synthesis

$$\text{Hybrid Architecture} = \text{Pipe-Filter} \; + \; \text{Microkernel} \; + \; \text{Event-Bus}$$

- **Pipe-Filter** for the data ingestion → validation → risk → display pipeline.
- **Microkernel** at the risk-calculation step, so new algorithms are loaded as plug-ins.
- **Event-Bus** so the dashboard can subscribe to "risk-updated" events.

### Step 4 — Justify (Model Answer Snippet)

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Requests per second}}{\text{Number of concurrent filters}}$$

For 10,000 TPS distributed across 4 filters:

$$\text{Per-filter load} = \frac{10{,}000}{4} = 2{,}500 \; \text{requests/second}$$

Since each filter has a processing budget of:

$$T_{\text{budget per filter}} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{4} = 1.25 \text{ ms}$$

each filter must be implemented with **lock-free data structures** and **zero-copy message passing** to stay within the latency budget. This justifies the choice of an in-memory event bus (e.g., LMAX Disruptor) over a database-backed broker.

### Step 5 — Anti-Pattern Risk

- **Sinkhole Anti-Pattern** — If any filter simply forwards data without transformation, it wastes latency. Mitigation: collapse consecutive non-transforming filters into one.
- **Tight Coupling via Shared Memory** — If filters share a global mutable state, the chain becomes a "spaghetti pipeline." Mitigation: keep each filter's state local; pass data explicitly via pipes.

---

## 3.3 Pattern-to-Framework Mapping (High-Yield for KTU Viva)

| Pattern | Industry Framework / Product |
|---|---|
| **Layered** | Java EE, .NET Framework, Spring Boot (3-tier apps) |
| **Client-Server** | HTTP/Web model, MySQL Server |
| **Master-Slave** | Hadoop HDFS NameNode/DataNode, MySQL Replication |
| **Pipe-Filter** | Unix shell, Apache Beam, Apache Flink |
| **Microkernel** | Eclipse RCP, OSGi, Kubernetes (control plane / kubelet) |
| **MVC** | Django, Ruby on Rails, ASP.NET MVC |
| **Broker** | CORBA, Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ |
| **Event-Bus** | Node.js EventEmitter, Vert.x, Akka |
| **P2P** | BitTorrent, Ethereum, IPFS |
| **Blackboard** | Hearsay-II (speech recognition), OpenCV (image pipeline) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note**: Naming one *real industry framework* per pattern can fetch 1 bonus mark in viva and Part B answers. Always cross-validate pattern knowledge with practical examples.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Compliance Checklist
- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `layer1`, `filterA`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node IDs.
- No markdown formatting (`**`, `*`) or HTML inside double-quoted node labels.
- All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.2 Diagram 1 — Layered Pattern Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["PRESENTATION LAYER (UI)"] --> L4
    L4["APPLICATION LAYER (WORKFLOW)"] --> L3
    L3["DOMAIN LAYER (BUSINESS LOGIC)"] --> L2
    L2["PERSISTENCE LAYER (DATABASE)"] --> L1
    L1["HARDWARE OR OS LAYER (RESOURCES)"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 2 — Master-Slave Pattern with Fault Tolerance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["MASTER DISPATCHER"] -- TASK 1 --> S1["SLAVE NODE 1"]
    M -- TASK 2 --> S2["SLAVE NODE 2"]
    M -- TASK 3 --> S3["SLAVE NODE 3"]
    M -- TASK 4 BACKUP --> S4["SLAVE NODE 4 STANDBY"]
    S1 -- RESULT --> M
    S2 -- RESULT --> M
    S3 -- RESULT --> M
    S4 -- HOT SPARE --> M
```

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — Pipe-Filter Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["DATA SOURCE"] --> P1["PIPE 1"]
    P1 --> F1["FILTER VALIDATE"]
    F1 --> P2["PIPE 2"]
    P2 --> F2["FILTER TRANSFORM"]
    F2 --> P3["PIPE 3"]
    P3 --> F3["FILTER ENRICH"]
    F3 --> P4["PIPE 4"]
    P4 --> SINK["DATA SINK"]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 4 — Broker Pattern (Distributed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1["CLIENT A"] -- REQUEST --> B
    C2["CLIENT B"] -- REQUEST --> B
    C3["CLIENT C"] -- REQUEST --> B
    B["BROKER REGISTRY AND ROUTER"] -- FORWARD --> S1["SERVER X"]
    B -- FORWARD --> S2["SERVER Y"]
    B -- FORWARD --> S3["SERVER Z"]
    S1 -- RESPONSE --> B
    S2 -- RESPONSE --> B
    S3 -- RESPONSE --> B
    B -- RESPONSE --> C1
    B -- RESPONSE --> C2
    B -- RESPONSE --> C3
```

## 4.6 Diagram 5 — MVC Triad

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["USER"] -- INPUT --> V["VIEW UI"]
    V -- USER EVENT --> C["CONTROLLER"]
    C -- UPDATE --> M["MODEL DATA AND RULES"]
    M -- NOTIFY --> V
    C -- RENDER --> V
```

## 4.7 Diagram 6 — Blackboard Pattern (Heuristic System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    KS1["KNOWLEDGE SOURCE 1"] -- READ WRITE --> BB
    KS2["KNOWLEDGE SOURCE 2"] -- READ WRITE --> BB
    KS3["KNOWLEDGE SOURCE 3"] -- READ WRITE --> BB
    BB["SHARED BLACKBOARD"] -- STATE --> CTRL
    CTRL["CONTROLLER OR SCHEDULER"] -- ACTIVATE --> KS1
    CTRL -- ACTIVATE --> KS2
    CTRL -- ACTIVATE --> KS3
```

## 4.8 Diagram 7 — Peer-to-Peer Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PEER NODE 1"] -- DIRECT --> P2
    P2["PEER NODE 2"] -- DIRECT --> P3
    P3["PEER NODE 3"] -- DIRECT --> P4
    P4["PEER NODE 4"] -- DIRECT --> P1
    P1 -- DIRECT --> P3
    P2 -- DIRECT --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Diagram Tip**: For 14-mark questions, the official KTU valuation key expects at least **one architectural diagram**. Use Mermaid-style flowcharts in your answer sheet by drawing neat boxes and labeled arrows. The structural diagrams above are model templates.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define an Architectural Pattern. List any four well-known architectural patterns.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **architectural pattern** is a high-level, reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software architecture, defining the gross-level structure of subsystems, their responsibilities, and their inter-relationships.
> Four examples:
> 1. **Layered Pattern** — Hierarchical decomposition.
> 2. **Client-Server Pattern** — Service provider and consumer.
> 3. **Pipe-Filter Pattern** — Streaming data transformation.
> 4. **Microkernel Pattern** — Core system with plug-ins.
> *(1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for the four examples — full 3 marks.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between the Layered Pattern and the Pipe-Filter Pattern in terms of structure and concurrency support.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Layered Pattern** has a *hierarchical* structure where each layer calls only the layer directly below it; concurrency is generally *low* because layers are invoked sequentially per request. (1.5 marks)
> - **Pipe-Filter Pattern** has a *linear chain* structure where data flows through independent filters; concurrency is *high* because independent filters can execute in parallel. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module 2)**: Each Part B question must have an internal choice. Two alternatives are given below. Answer any **one** of them.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Layered Architectural Pattern** in detail. Draw its structural diagram and discuss its **advantages and disadvantages** with a real-world example.

**(b)** Compare the **Client-Server** and **Master-Slave** architectural patterns under the headings: structure, fault-tolerance, scalability, and a suitable use case.

#### **Model Answer for (a) – 7 Marks**

- **Definition (1.5 marks)**: A Layered pattern decomposes a system into a hierarchy where each layer provides services to the one above and consumes services from the one below. Common in enterprise apps.
- **Standard Layers (2 marks)**:
  - **Presentation Layer** — UI (HTML, React, mobile UI).
  - **Application Layer** — Workflow (Spring controllers).
  - **Domain Layer** — Business rules (Risk calculator).
  - **Persistence Layer** — Database access (Hibernate/JPA).
  - **Database Layer** — Actual data store (PostgreSQL).
- **Diagram (1.5 marks)**: Draw the five-tier hierarchy with arrows.
- **Advantages (1 mark)**: Modifiability, testability, clear SoC.
- **Disadvantages (0.5 mark)**: Sinkhole anti-pattern, performance overhead.
- **Real-world Example (0.5 mark)**: A 3-tier banking web application.

#### **Model Answer for (b) – 7 Marks**

| Dimension | Client-Server | Master-Slave |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Centralized server; multiple clients | Master dispatches; slaves compute |
| **Fault-Tolerance** | Server = SPOF; mitigated via clustering | Slave failure tolerated; backup slave takes over |
| **Scalability** | Vertical scaling of server | Horizontal scaling by adding slaves |
| **Use Case** | Gmail, WhatsApp Web | Hadoop HDFS, MySQL Replication |

*(1.5 marks per dimension + 1 mark for overall coherence = 7 marks.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Pipe-Filter Architectural Pattern**. State two scenarios where it is unsuitable.

**(b)** Explain the **Broker Architectural Pattern** with a structural diagram. Discuss how it provides *location transparency* and *interoperability*.

#### **Model Answer for (a) – 7 Marks**

- **Definition (1.5 marks)**: A streaming pattern where data flows sequentially through *Filters* (transformers) connected by *Pipes* (conduits).
- **Diagram (2 marks)**: Source → Pipe → Filter 1 → Pipe → Filter 2 → Pipe → Filter 3 → Sink.
- **Components (1.5 marks)**:
  - **Filter** — Performs transformation (validate, enrich, encrypt).
  - **Pipe** — Transports data between filters; can buffer.
  - **Data Source/Sink** — Origin and terminus of the flow.
- **Benefits (1 mark)**: Composability, concurrency, reusability.
- **Unsuitable Scenarios (1 mark)**:
  1. **Interactive GUI applications** — Pipe-filter is poor at handling bidirectional user interaction.
  2. **Shared-state applications** — Filters are ideally stateless; systems needing tight state-sharing (e.g., transaction systems) are better served by Layered or MVC.

#### **Model Answer for (b) – 7 Marks**

- **Definition (1.5 marks)**: A Broker pattern decouples clients and servers by introducing an intermediary **broker** that routes requests and responses.
- **Diagram (2 marks)**: Show multiple clients connecting to the broker, which forwards to multiple servers (see Section 4.5).
- **Components (1 mark)**: Client, Server, Proxy, Broker.
- **Location Transparency (1.5 marks)**: Clients refer to a server by a *logical name*; the broker resolves the actual physical address. Servers can move (e.g., migrate to a new VM) without clients being reconfigured.
- **Interoperability (1 mark)**: The broker can translate between protocols (e.g., SOAP ↔ REST) and IDL representations, allowing heterogeneous systems to collaborate. *Examples*: CORBA, Apache Kafka.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Pattern with Style**: Many students write "MVC is an architectural style" — in KTU's terminology, MVC is an **architectural pattern** within the **interactive systems** style. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting Anti-Patterns**: Naming *one anti-pattern* per chosen pattern is mandatory in 14-mark answers. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **No Diagram**: A 14-mark answer without a structural diagram typically caps at 10/14. Always include a clean block diagram.
> 4. **Generic Advantages**: Writing "it is scalable" without stating *which axis* (vertical / horizontal, throughput / latency) is treated as vague. Always quantify.
> 5. **Mixing Quality Attributes**: Performance ≠ Modifiability. Many students blur these. Tie each advantage to a *named QA* explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architectural Pattern** = macro-level reusable solution; **Design Pattern** = micro-level class/object solution.
- **Layered** is the most common pattern for enterprise business apps; watch for the **sinkhole anti-pattern**.
- **Client-Server** is the foundation of the web; centralization = security + SPOF.
- **Master-Slave** is the workhorse for **parallelism and fault-tolerance** (Hadoop, MySQL replication).
- **Pipe-Filter** is ideal for **data streaming and ETL**; poor for interactive systems.
- **Microkernel** is the gold standard for **extensibility and portability** (Eclipse, OSGi, Kubernetes).
- **MVC** decouples UI from business logic; frameworks like Django and Rails implement it.
- **Broker** decouples clients/servers; provides **location transparency** and **interoperability**.
- **Event-Bus / Publish-Subscribe** is a messaging variant of Broker; great for **decoupled reactive** systems.
- **P2P** eliminates central control; maximizes scalability at the cost of security and QoS.
- **Blackboard** is the niche pattern for **heuristic, AI-style** problems with no deterministic algorithm.
- **Reflection** enables **self-adaptive** systems by separating base-level logic from meta-level reasoning.
- For every chosen pattern, the KTU evaluator expects: **Definition → Structure → Diagram → QA mapping → Anti-pattern risk → Real-world example**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Patterns and Styles, Applying Patterns and Choosing a Style
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Patterns and Styles, Applying Patterns and Choosing a Style

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Architectural Pattern — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Pattern (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **Architectural Pattern** is a reusable, proven solution to a recurring design problem that occurs within a well-defined context. It expresses a fundamental structural organization schema for software systems, providing a set of predefined subsystems, their responsibilities, and the rules and guidelines for organizing the relationships between them.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a pattern is **solution-oriented**. It answers the question: *"Given this problem, how do I solve it architecturally?"*

## 1.2 Architectural Style — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Style (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **Architectural Style** (also called an architectural *idiom* or *genre*) defines a family of systems in terms of a *pattern of structural organization*. It is a named collection of design decisions that is applicable in a given context, imposes constraints on the architecture, and leads to characteristic benefits and liabilities.

A style is **vocabulary-oriented**. It answers the question: *"What vocabulary of components and connectors can I use?"*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Blueprint

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> - An **Architectural Style** is like an **architectural vocabulary** — load-bearing walls, columns, beams, arches. You decide *which vocabulary* to use (e.g., Gothic, Modernist, Modular).
> - An **Architectural Pattern** is like a **proven blueprint template** — a 3BHK layout, an open-plan office, a courtyard house. You decide *which template* solves your specific problem.
> - A **Pattern uses a Style** as its underlying vocabulary. A 3BHK pattern may use a Modernist style; a microservices pattern uses a Distributed style.

Think of it this way: **Style = Nouns; Pattern = Sentences built using those nouns.**

## 1.4 The Critical Distinction at a Glance

| Aspect | Architectural Style | Architectural Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Vocabulary & structural family | Specific recurring problem & its solution |
| **Scope** | Broad (defines classes of systems) | Narrow (specific structural solution) |
| **Question Answered** | "What is the system shape?" | "How do I solve this problem?" |
| **Example** | Layered, Client–Server, Pipe-and-Filter | MVC, Microservices, Broker, SOA |
| **Reusability** | Defines components & connectors | Defines roles, responsibilities, collaborations |

## 1.5 KTU 2024 — Why This Distinction Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners (per the **PECST861 syllabus**) explicitly test whether students can:
> 1. **Differentiate** a *style* from a *pattern* (often a 3-mark direct question).
> 2. **Map** a pattern onto a style (e.g., MVC maps onto the Layered Style).
> 3. **Justify** the choice of a style for a given non-functional requirement (NFR).

---

## 1.6 Canonical Catalog (KTU-Mandated)

### 1.6.1 Architectural Styles (Module 2 Core Focus)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Five Styles per KTU PECST861 Module 2:**
> 1. **Layered Style** — hierarchical partitioning (Presentation → Business → Data)
> 2. **Data-Flow Style** — Pipe-and-Filter, Batch Sequential
> 3. **Call-and-Return Style** — Main Program/Subroutine, Object-Oriented, Client–Server
> 4. **Data-Centered Style** — Repository, Blackboard
> 5. **Event-Based Style (Independent Components)** — Event-Driven, Publish-Subscribe, Message Bus

### 1.6.2 Architectural Patterns (Module 2 Core Focus)

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU-Listed Patterns:**
> 1. **MVC (Model–View–Controller)** — layered interaction pattern
> 2. **Microservices** — independent, fine-grained, deployable services
> 3. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** — coarse-grained, contract-defined services
> 4. **Broker Pattern** — decoupling clients from servers via a broker
> 5. **Master-Slave Pattern** — fault tolerance via replication
> 6. **Pipe-Filter Pattern** — streaming transformation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Architectural Style

Every style, per **Shaw & Garlan (1996)** and as adopted by KTU, has four elements:

| Element | Meaning | Example (Layered Style) |
|---|---|---|
| **Components** | The principal processing units | Presentation, Business, Data layers |
| **Connectors** | The mechanism of interaction | Procedure calls, message passing |
| **Constraints** | The structural rules | Strict downward call direction |
| **Semantics** | The runtime behavior | Request flows top-down, response flows up |

## 2.2 Anatomy of an Architectural Pattern

A pattern (per **Gamma et al. + Buschmann et al.**) has:

1. **Context** — the recurring situation
2. **Problem** — the forces in tension (NFRs like scalability vs. consistency)
3. **Solution** — proven configuration of components
4. **Consequences** — trade-offs (positive and negative)
5. **Implementation** — sample sketches

## 2.3 Deep Dive — The Five Styles

### 2.3.1 Layered Style (Decomposition by "Level of Abstraction")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Rule:** A layer at level `N` may only request services from level `N-1`. Level `0` is the lowest (closest to hardware/OS).

- **Strengths:** Modifiability, portability, testability (each layer can be unit-tested in isolation).
- **Weaknesses:** Performance overhead (function-call chain), reduced fault tolerance (a crash in a low layer cascades upward).
- **Used By:** Java EE, .NET Framework, classic three-tier web apps.

### 2.3.2 Data-Flow Style (Decomposition by "Flow of Data")

The two sub-styles are:

$$
\text{Batch Sequential} : \text{filter}_1 \rightarrow \text{filter}_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{filter}_n
$$

$$
\text{Pipe-and-Filter} : \text{stream in} \rightarrow [F_1 \mid F_2 \mid F_3] \rightarrow \text{stream out}
$$

- **Strengths:** Reusability of filters, composability, concurrent execution.
- **Weaknesses:** State-sharing is awkward, per-element transformation overhead.

### 2.3.3 Call-and-Return Style

| Sub-Style | Structure | When Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Program / Subroutine** | One main, many subroutines | Early monolithic apps |
| **Object-Oriented** | Objects with methods | Polymorphism, encapsulation |
| **Client–Server** | Two tiers (fat client + server) | Distributed business logic |

### 2.3.4 Data-Centered Style

- **Repository Style:** Subsystems share a central data store; they communicate **only** through this store (e.g., DBMS-centric systems).
- **Blackboard Style:** Subsystems read from and write to a shared *knowledge source*, ideal for AI/heuristic problems with no predetermined solution path.

### 2.3.5 Event-Based Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independent Components communicate via events, not direct calls.**
> Two flavors: **Event-Driven (point-to-point via event bus)** and **Publish-Subscribe (topic-based fanout).**

- **Strengths:** Loose coupling, scalability, dynamic binding.
- **Weaknesses:** Eventual consistency, debugging complexity ("event spaghetti").

## 2.4 The Five Patterns (Module 2 Catalog)

### 2.4.1 MVC (Model–View–Controller)

$$
\text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{action}} \text{Controller} \xrightarrow{\text{updates}} \text{Model} \xrightarrow{\text{notifies}} \text{View}
$$

- **Style Mapping:** Layered (View = Presentation, Controller = Coordination, Model = Domain + Data).
- **Best For:** Interactive GUI apps with multiple views over the same data.

### 2.4.2 Microservices Pattern

- **NFR Targets:** Independent deployability, technology heterogeneity, fault isolation.
- **Style Mapping:** Distributed, Event-Driven (often), Service-Based.
- **Anti-Pattern:** Distributed Monolith (microservices that must deploy together).

### 2.4.3 Broker Pattern

```
Client → Broker → Server
```

- **Decouples** client from server completely; servers register with broker.
- **Used In:** CORBA, Web Services (UDDI + WSDL), Apache Kafka (loosely).

### 2.4.4 Master-Slave Pattern

- **Master** distributes work to **slaves**; results are returned and aggregated.
- **Strengths:** Reliability (slaves can fail and be retried), parallelism.
- **Used In:** Hadoop MapReduce, database replication.

### 2.4.5 Pipe-Filter Pattern

- Already covered under Style; here it appears as a **Pattern** because the *combinator* semantics is application-specific.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Sheet (Trade-off Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> Treat the table below as your **revision cheat sheet**. KTU loves to map NFRs to style/pattern selections.

| NFR / Concern | Recommended Style | Recommended Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Modifiability** | Layered, OO | MVC |
| **Performance (latency)** | Data-Flow (Pipe-Filter) | Master-Slave |
| **Scalability** | Event-Based, Client-Server | Microservices, Broker |
| **Reliability** | Data-Centered (Repository) | Master-Slave |
| **Reusability** | Pipe-Filter | Microservices, SOA |
| **Maintainability** | Layered | MVC, Broker |
| **Security** | Layered (security at gateway layer) | Broker (centralized auth) |
| **Distributed Computing** | Client–Server, Event-Based | Broker, Microservices, SOA |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Choosing the wrong style/pattern costs **millions in refactoring**. Industry case studies (Amazon's 2002 pivot from monolithic to SOA → microservices; Netflix's Choreographed Event-Based microservices) demonstrate that style selection is a **strategic, not tactical, decision**.

---

## 2.7 Why-and-How of Pattern vs. Style Selection — Logic Flow

- **WHY** does the distinction exist? → To prevent students from conflating "vocabulary" with "solution template."
- **HOW** does KTU test this? → Through "Compare and Contrast" and "Justify" questions.

| Decision Step | Question | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Step 1 | What NFR dominates? (e.g., scalability) | Filters the style set |
| Step 2 | What recurring problem do I have? (e.g., decoupling UI from data) | Filters the pattern set |
| Step 3 | What are the constraints? (budget, team skills, legacy) | Final selection |
| Step 4 | What are the consequences? | Documented trade-offs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Mapping NFRs to Styles via the ATAM Method

> [!NOTE]
> The **Architecture Trade-off Analysis Method (ATAM)** is the KTU-prescribed procedure for *choosing a style*. Below is a step-by-step analytical walkthrough.

### Step 3.1.1 — Enumerate the Quality Attributes

Begin by listing all non-functional requirements (NFRs). For a representative e-commerce system:

$$
Q = \{Q_1=\text{Availability},\ Q_2=\text{Performance},\ Q_3=\text{Security},\ Q_4=\text{Modifiability},\ Q_5=\text{Scalability}\}
$$

### Step 3.1.2 — Assign Priority Weights

Each quality attribute gets a weight `w_i` such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1, \quad \text{where } w_i \in [0, 1]
$$

For our e-commerce system, a typical weighting:

$$
W = \{w_1 = 0.30,\ w_2 = 0.25,\ w_3 = 0.20,\ w_4 = 0.10,\ w_5 = 0.15\}
$$

### Step 3.1.3 — Score Candidate Styles Against Each Quality

For each candidate style `S_j` and each quality `Q_i`, assign a satisfaction score `s_{i,j} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}` (1 = poor, 5 = excellent).

Consider two candidate styles:

$$
S_1 = \text{Layered}, \quad S_2 = \text{Event-Driven (Microservices)}
$$

Construct the **Quality–Architecture Scoring Matrix**:

| Quality Attribute | Weight `w_i` | Layered Score `s_{i,1}` | Event-Driven Score `s_{i,2}` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | 0.30 | 3 | 5 |
| Performance | 0.25 | 4 | 4 |
| Security | 0.20 | 4 | 3 |
| Modifiability | 0.10 | 5 | 4 |
| Scalability | 0.15 | 2 | 5 |

### Step 3.1.4 — Compute the Weighted Aggregate Score

The weighted score for each style is:

$$
\text{Score}(S_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{i,j}
$$

**For Layered:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(S_1) &= (0.30 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 2) \\
&= 0.90 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.50 + 0.30 \\
&= 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Event-Driven:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(S_2) &= (0.30 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 5) \\
&= 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.40 + 0.75 \\
&= 4.25
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3.1.5 — Compare and Select

$$
\text{Score}(S_2) > \text{Score}(S_1) \implies \text{Select } S_2 = \text{Event-Driven (Microservices)}
$$

The decision rule:

$$
S^* = \arg\max_{j \in \{1, \ldots, m\}} \text{Score}(S_j)
$$

### Step 3.1.6 — Document Trade-offs (Sensitivity Analysis)

- **S_1 (Layered)** was rejected due to poor scalability (weight = 0.15 but gap of 3 points).
- **S_2 (Event-Driven)** is weaker on security — a compensating mechanism (API Gateway + OAuth2) is required.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pattern-to-Style Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This mapping is one of the **highest-yield KTU topics**. Expect at least one 7-mark sub-part on it.

| Pattern | Primary Style | Secondary Style | Core Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| MVC | Layered | — | Observer + Strategy |
| Microservices | Distributed | Event-Driven | Independent process + REST/gRPC |
| Broker | Client–Server | Event-Based | Indirection through broker |
| Master-Slave | Data-Flow | Call-and-Return | Coordinator–Worker |
| SOA | Client–Server | Layered | Contract-defined services + ESB |
| Pipe-Filter | Data-Flow | Independent Components | Stream transformation |

## 3.3 The "Choosing a Style" Decision Tree (Analytical Steps)

1. **Identify** the dominant quality attributes (QAs) and their weights `w_i`.
2. **Filter** candidate styles by architectural drivers (e.g., team size, deployment constraints).
3. **Score** survivors using the matrix above.
4. **Compute** weighted aggregate `Score(S_j) = \sum w_i \cdot s_{i,j}`.
5. **Select** `S^* = \arg\max_j \text{Score}(S_j)`.
6. **Validate** via architectural prototypes or ATAM scenario walkthroughs.
7. **Document** consequences and risk-mitigation tactics.

## 3.4 Worked Example — ATM System (KTU-Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> The ATM is the **most-asked example** in KTU Module 2 papers. Let us analyze it.

**System Constraints:**
- High reliability (24x7 availability)
- High security (PCI-DSS)
- Moderate modifiability (new transaction types)

**Step 1 — NFRs:** `Q = {Reliability=0.40, Security=0.30, Modifiability=0.20, Performance=0.10}`

**Step 2 — Candidate Styles:** Layered, Client–Server.

**Step 3 — Score Matrix:**

| NFR | Weight | Layered | Client-Server |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | 0.40 | 3 | 4 |
| Security | 0.30 | 4 | 4 |
| Modifiability | 0.20 | 5 | 3 |
| Performance | 0.10 | 4 | 4 |

**Step 4 — Aggregate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{Layered}} &= (0.40 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.20 + 1.00 + 0.40 \\
&= 3.80
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{Client-Server}} &= (0.40 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.20 + 0.60 + 0.40 \\
&= 3.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tie-Breaker:** For an ATM, **centralized control** of the security perimeter at the server tier favors **Client-Server**. Selected: **Client-Server (with Layered sub-organization)**.

## 3.5 The Combined Layered + Client-Server Architecture (Final Choice)

```
ATM Client (Layer 3: Presentation, Layer 2: Local Validation)
        ↕ (encrypted channel)
Bank Server (Layer 3: Web Services, Layer 2: Business Logic, Layer 1: DB)
```

This is the canonical answer KTU expects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram — Pattern vs Style Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Architectural Style (Vocabulary)"] --> B["Layered Style"]
    A --> C["Data-Flow Style"]
    A --> D["Client-Server Style"]
    A --> E["Event-Based Style"]
    A --> F["Data-Centered Style"]
    
    B --> B1["MVC Pattern"]
    B --> B2["Three-Tier Pattern"]
    C --> C1["Pipe-Filter Pattern"]
    D --> D1["Broker Pattern"]
    D --> D2["Master-Slave Pattern"]
    E --> E1["Pub-Sub Pattern"]
    E --> E2["Microservices Pattern"]
    F --> F1["Blackboard Pattern"]
    F --> F2["Repository Pattern"]
    
    B1 -.uses.-> B
    C1 -.uses.-> C
    D1 -.uses.-> D
    E2 -.uses.-> E
```

## 4.2 Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing a Style

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start: System Requirements"] --> S1["Identify Dominant NFRs and Assign Weights"]
    S1 --> S2["List Candidate Styles"]
    S2 --> S3["Filter by Architectural Drivers (Team, Cost, Legacy)"]
    S3 --> S4["Score Each Candidate on Each NFR 1 to 5"]
    S4 --> S5["Compute Weighted Aggregate Score(S)"]
    S5 --> S6["Select S Star = argmax Score S"]
    S6 --> S7["Map to Compatible Architectural Pattern"]
    S7 --> S8["Document Trade-offs and Risk Mitigations"]
    S8 --> S9["Final Architecture Decision"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram — Layered Style Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Presentation Layer (UI / Web Tier)"] --> L2["Application Layer (Use Cases / Controllers)"]
    L2 --> L3["Domain Layer (Business Logic / Entities)"]
    L3 --> L4["Persistence Layer (Repositories / DAOs)"]
    L4 --> L5["Database Layer (Physical Storage)"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram — MVC Pattern on Layered Style

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["View (Presentation Layer)"] -- "user actions" --> C["Controller (Application Layer)"]
    C -- "updates state" --> M["Model (Domain + Persistence Layer)"]
    M -- "notifies state change" --> V
    V -- "render data" --> U["User"]
```

## 4.5 Diagram — Microservices Pattern on Event-Based Style

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GW["API Gateway"]
    MS1["Auth Service"]
    MS2["Order Service"]
    MS3["Payment Service"]
    MS4["Inventory Service"]
    BUS["Event Bus (Kafka / RabbitMQ)"]
    DB1["Orders DB"]
    DB2["Payments DB"]
    DB3["Inventory DB"]
    
    GW --> MS1
    GW --> MS2
    GW --> MS3
    GW --> MS4
    MS2 -- publish OrderCreated --> BUS
    BUS -- subscribe --> MS3
    BUS -- subscribe --> MS4
    MS2 --> DB1
    MS3 --> DB2
    MS4 --> DB3
```

## 4.6 Diagram — Master-Slave Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["Master (Coordinator)"]
    S1["Slave Worker 1"]
    S2["Slave Worker 2"]
    S3["Slave Worker 3"]
    RES["Result Aggregator"]
    
    M -- "sub-task A" --> S1
    M -- "sub-task B" --> S2
    M -- "sub-task C" --> S3
    S1 -- "result" --> RES
    S2 -- "result" --> RES
    S3 -- "result" --> RES
    RES --> M
```

## 4.7 Diagram — Broker Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Client 1"]
    C2["Client 2"]
    C3["Client 3"]
    B["Broker (Directory + Forwarder)"]
    S1["Server A"]
    S2["Server B"]
    S3["Server C"]
    
    C1 --> B
    C2 --> B
    C3 --> B
    B --> S1
    B --> S2
    B --> S3
    S1 -.registers.-> B
    S2 -.registers.-> B
    S3 -.registers.-> B
```

## 4.8 Diagram — Trade-off Compass (NFR vs Style/Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Performance"] --> SF1["Data-Flow / Pipe-Filter"]
    S["Scalability"] --> SF2["Event-Based / Microservices"]
    M["Modifiability"] --> SF3["Layered / MVC"]
```
