```mermaid
    SEC["Security"] --> SF5["Layered + Broker (Gateway)"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between an Architectural Pattern and an Architectural Style. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Architectural Style | Architectural Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Defines a family of systems by their structural organization (vocabulary of components and connectors) | A proven, reusable solution to a recurring problem within a context |
| Focus | Structural family (broad) | Problem–solution template (specific) |
| Example | Layered, Pipe-and-Filter | MVC, Microservices |

A pattern is **instantiated within** a style; e.g., MVC is a pattern instantiated within the Layered style. **(1 mark for definition of style, 1 mark for pattern, 1 mark for example + mapping.)**

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**List any THREE architectural styles from the KTU Module 2 catalog and state one quality attribute each style optimizes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per style):**

1. **Layered Style** → optimizes **modifiability** (each layer can be swapped independently).
2. **Event-Based Style** → optimizes **scalability and loose coupling**.
3. **Data-Centered (Repository)** → optimizes **data integrity and concurrency control**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PECST861 / Q.7(a) Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **Layered Style** and the **MVC Pattern**. Show how MVC is mapped onto the Layered Style. Discuss ONE trade-off of using the Layered style for a high-throughput e-commerce site.

**(b) [7 Marks]** You are designing an online **real-time stock-trading platform** with the following non-functional requirements: **Availability = 0.40, Latency = 0.30, Security = 0.20, Modifiability = 0.10**. Compare the **Layered**, **Event-Based**, and **Client–Server** styles using a weighted scoring matrix and recommend the best style with justification.

---

### Model Solution — Question Choice A

#### Part (a) — Layered Style + MVC Pattern [7 marks]

**Layered Style Explanation (3 marks):**

The Layered style partitions the system into a hierarchy of layers, each providing services to the layer above and consuming services from the layer below.

**Canonical 4-Layer Organization:**

```
Presentation (UI)
      ↓
Application (Use Cases / Controllers)
      ↓
Domain (Business Logic)
      ↓
Persistence (Database Access)
```

```
[Diagram: Vertical layered boxes with downward arrows]
```

**MVC Pattern Explanation (3 marks):**

The MVC pattern separates the user-interface concerns into three roles:

- **Model** — manages data and business rules.
- **View** — renders the model's data into a UI form.
- **Controller** — handles user input, updates the model, and selects the view.

**Mapping MVC onto Layered (1 mark):**

| MVC Role | Layered Layer |
|---|---|
| View | Presentation Layer |
| Controller | Application Layer |
| Model | Domain + Persistence Layers |

**Trade-off for E-Commerce (included in any 1 mark):**
- **Disadvantage:** A single bad request passes through all layers → high per-request latency. For high-throughput e-commerce, this becomes the bottleneck. Mitigation: **cache at the Presentation layer**, **short-circuit trivial requests at the Controller**.

#### Part (b) — Stock Trading Platform Style Selection [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Candidate Styles:** `S = {Layered, Event-Based, Client-Server}`

**Step 2 — Quality-Architecture Score Matrix (2 marks):**

| NFR | Weight `w` | Layered | Event-Based | Client-Server |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | 0.40 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Latency (Performance) | 0.30 | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| Security | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| Modifiability | 0.10 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

**Step 3 — Weighted Aggregate Computation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{Layered}} &= (0.40 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 5) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.50 \\
&= 3.70
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{Event-Based}} &= (0.40 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.50 + 0.60 + 0.40 \\
&= 4.50
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{Client-Server}} &= (0.40 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 3) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.30 \\
&= 3.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Selection (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{Event-Based}} = 4.50 \;\; > \;\; 3.90 > 3.70 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S^* = \text{Event-Based}
$$

**Step 5 — Justification (1 mark):**
Event-Based maximizes the two highest-weighted NFRs (Availability 0.40 and Latency 0.30). Trade-off: weaker security posture (score 3) is mitigated via an API Gateway with OAuth2, mTLS, and rate-limiting.

---

### Question Choice B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PECST861 / Q.7(b) Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Broker Pattern** and the **Master-Slave Pattern** with diagrams. State one situation where each is preferred and one limitation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Microservices Pattern** and **SOA Pattern**. Show how Microservices can be realized on top of the **Event-Based Style** using a diagram. Explain when you would NOT choose Microservices.

---

### Model Solution — Question Choice B

#### Part (a) — Broker + Master-Slave [7 marks]

**Broker Pattern (3 marks):**

The Broker pattern introduces an intermediary (broker) that decouples clients from servers. Clients send requests to the broker; the broker locates an appropriate server via a directory and forwards the request.

```
[Client 1] → [Broker: Directory + Forwarder] → [Server A]
[Client 2] → [Broker]                        → [Server B]
```

**Best For:** Distributed systems with **dynamic discovery** (e.g., Web Services, CORBA).
**Limitation:** Broker is a **single point of failure** and a **performance bottleneck** (must be replicated and load-balanced).

**Master-Slave Pattern (3 marks):**

The master decomposes a task into sub-tasks, distributes them to slaves in parallel, and aggregates the results.

```
[Master] → [Slave 1] (sub-task A) → [Result Aggregator] → [Master]
        → [Slave 2] (sub-task B) →
        → [Slave 3] (sub-task C) →
```

**Best For:** Compute-intensive parallel tasks (Hadoop MapReduce, ML training).
**Limitation:** Master is a **single point of failure**; slaves must be **stateless** for clean failure recovery.

**Comparison Line (1 mark):**
Both patterns use a **coordinator**, but **Broker decouples heterogeneous entities**, while **Master-Slave enforces a strict control hierarchy**.

#### Part (b) — Microservices vs SOA + Event-Based Realization [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | SOA | Microservices |
|---|---|---|
| Service Granularity | Coarse-grained (enterprise scope) | Fine-grained (single responsibility) |
| Communication | ESB, SOAP, WSDL | REST, gRPC, Event Bus |
| Data Sharing | Shared enterprise DB | Database-per-service |
| Governance | Centralized standards | Decentralized ("you build it, you run it") |
| Coupling | Tight (via ESB contracts) | Loose (via APIs + events) |
| Deployment | Often co-deployed | Always independently deployed |

**Microservices on Event-Based Style (3 marks):**

```
[API Gateway] → [Auth Service, Order Service, Payment Service, Inventory Service]
                                       ↓ publish OrderCreated
                                [Event Bus: Kafka]
                                       ↓ subscribe
                                [Payment Service, Inventory Service]
```

Each service is an **independent component** in the Event-Based style vocabulary; they interact **only via events on the bus** (no direct calls).

**When NOT to choose Microservices (2 marks):**

1. **Small team / simple domain** — operational overhead (CI/CD, service mesh, observability) outweighs benefits. A monolith or modular monolith is more cost-effective.
2. **Strong transactional consistency required across domains** — distributed transactions (Saga pattern) add significant complexity; prefer a monolith with ACID transactions.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Pattern with Style** — "MVC is a style" is WRONG. MVC is a **pattern**. Deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the Weight Justification** — In trade-off questions, never present scores without first stating the **NFR weights** and **why** they are chosen.
> 3. **Forgetting Trade-offs** — A 7-mark question that ONLY recommends a style without documenting consequences loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No Diagram** — KTU mandates diagrams for any style/pattern question. A text-only answer caps at ~80% marks.
> 5. **Mapping Pattern ↔ Style** — Always state which style the pattern is **instantiated within**. Missing this costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Misnaming the Broker vs. Pub-Sub** — Broker is **synchronous request-reply**; Pub-Sub is **asynchronous event-fanout**. Confusing them is a common slip-up.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Overview of Patterns and Styles):**

- **Definition:** Architectural **Style** = vocabulary (components + connectors + constraints); Architectural **Pattern** = recurring problem-solution template.
- **Five Styles (MUST memorize):** Layered, Data-Flow (Pipe-and-Filter), Call-and-Return (OO, Client-Server), Data-Centered (Repository, Blackboard), Event-Based (Independent Components, Pub-Sub).
- **Five Patterns (MUST memorize):** MVC, Microservices, Broker, Master-Slave, SOA + Pipe-Filter.
- **Mapping Mnemonic:** **"Pattern on Style"** — every pattern sits on top of a style (e.g., MVC → Layered, Microservices → Event-Based, Broker → Client-Server).
- **Choosing a Style = ATAM-style Process:** Enumerate NFRs → Assign Weights → Score Candidates → Pick argmax → Document Trade-offs.
- **Trade-off Compass:** Performance → Pipe-Filter | Scalability → Event-Based | Modifiability → Layered/MVC | Reliability → Master-Slave/Repository | Security → Broker + Layered Gateway.
- **Anti-Patterns to know:** Distributed Monolith, Big Ball of Mud, Vendor Lock-In.
- **Formula to remember:** `Score(S_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{i,j}` and `S^* = \arg\max_{j} \text{Score}(S_j)`.
- **Diagram Rule:** Always include at least one Mermaid/diagram in any 7-mark style/pattern question.
- **Exam Pattern:** Expect 1 Part-A question (3 marks) on this topic + 1 Part-B sub-question (7 marks) per KTU 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patterns for Enterprise Applications: Enterprise Applications and Layered Patterns, Concurrency Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Patterns for Enterprise Applications: Enterprise Applications and Layered Patterns, Concurrency Problems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Enterprise Applications (EA) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Martin Fowler, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture):** > An *Enterprise Application* is a software system built around a **persistent store of business data**, accessed concurrently by many users, that contains **business rules**, **processes**, and **operational logic** to automate the functioning of a large organization. ### Key Characteristics of Enterprise Applications (KTU High-Yield) | Characteristic | Description | |---|---| | **Persistent Data** | Data outlives any single execution; stored in RDBMS/NoSQL stores | | **High Concurrency** | Hundreds to thousands of simultaneous users | | **Business Logic** | Encodes complex domain rules (billing, taxation, workflows) | | **Distributed Deployment** | Components spread across multiple servers/JVMs/nodes | | **Integration** | Must interoperate with legacy systems, third-party APIs, ERPs | | **Security & Transactions** | ACID transactions, role-based access, audit logging | ### Conceptual Analogy Think of an **Enterprise Application as a massive corporate office building**: - The **Database (ground floor)** holds the permanent records (files, documents). - The **Persistence Layer (1st floor)** is the filing clerks — they fetch/return files. - The **Domain/Business Layer (2nd floor)** contains the actual rules — accountants, lawyers who decide *what to do* with the files. - The **Presentation Layer (top floor)** is the reception desk that talks to the visitors (users). - **Concurrency** is like having hundreds of visitors arriving at the same time — clerks must manage queues, avoid giving the same file to two people, and prevent deadlocks (e.g., two clerks each holding a file the other needs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Enterprise Applications are *not* standalone desktop tools; they are **multi-tiered, network-centric, data-intensive** systems whose complexity arises from handling **concurrency, distribution, and evolution** simultaneously. --- ## 1.2 Layered Pattern (N-Tier Architecture) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > The *Layered Pattern* (also called **N-Tier Architecture**) organizes the system into a stack of **horizontally-related layers**, where each layer provides services to the layer *above it* and consumes services of the layer *below it*. Communication is typically **top-down** (request) and **bottom-up** (response). ### The Four Canonical Layers 1. **Presentation Layer** — UI, controllers, view templates (JSP/React/Angular). 2. **Application/Service Layer** — Coordinates use cases; thin orchestration. 3. **Domain/Business Layer** — Core business rules, entities, domain services. 4. **Data/Persistence Layer** — Repositories, DAOs, ORM mappings. 5. **Database Layer** — The actual RDBMS engine (sometimes treated as a separate infrastructure layer). ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **restaurant**: - The **Waiter (Presentation Layer)** takes your order — speaks the customer's language. - The **Head Chef (Service Layer)** coordinates — assigns orders, manages timing. - The **Sous Chef (Domain Layer)** applies the recipe logic — uses domain knowledge of cooking. - The **Pantry/Kitchen Storage (Data Layer)** holds ingredients — accessed via DB queries. - **Customer** never directly accesses the pantry; access goes strictly *through the chain of command*. This is **Layered Architecture**! > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule of Layered Architecture:** *A layer should NEVER directly access the layer two levels below it.* Skipping layers creates tight coupling and breaks the pattern. --- ## 1.3 Concurrency Problems in Enterprise Applications > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Concurrency Problems* are defects or undesirable behaviours that arise when **two or more threads/processes access shared mutable state simultaneously** without proper synchronization, leading to **race conditions**, **deadlocks**, **starvation**, and **data inconsistency**. ### Why Concurrency is Critical in EAs Enterprise servers are **multi-threaded by design** — Tomcat, JBoss, WebLogic all maintain a thread pool. A single business transaction (e.g., seat booking) may be invoked by 500 users at the same instant. Without concurrency control, the system becomes **non-deterministic** and **unsafe**. ### Conceptual Analogy Consider a **shared bank locker room**: - Two customers (threads) try to access the **same locker** at once → **Race Condition**. - Customer A holds locker-1 key and needs locker-2; Customer B holds locker-2 key and needs locker-1 → **Deadlock**. - One customer keeps getting pushed out of the queue because others are favored → **Starvation**. - Two tellers update the same account balance simultaneously → **Lost Update Problem**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Must-Know:** Concurrency problems are the **#1 source of production bugs** in enterprise Java/.NET applications. KTU expects students to identify the problem, explain its cause, and propose a solution (locks, immutability, transactions). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Deeper Analysis: Enterprise Application Characteristics Enterprise applications are **complex** because they simultaneously deal with: - **Volume** — millions of records, thousands of users. - **Longevity** — run for 10+ years with evolving requirements. - **Heterogeneity** — mix of platforms, languages, and data sources. - **Security & Compliance** — GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS requirements. - **Performance & Scalability** — must scale horizontally. > **Why does this matter for architects?** Fowler's principle: *"Any fool can write code a computer can understand. Good architects write code a **human** can understand."* Layered patterns enforce this by separating concerns. --- ## 2.2 Layered Architecture — The Closed vs. Open Debate ### 2.2.1 Closed Layers (Strict Layering) - Each layer communicates **only with its immediate neighbour**. - **Advantage:** Maximum decoupling, easier to test, swappable layers. - **Disadvantage:** Can lead to "pass-through" boilerplate (a layer simply forwards calls without logic). ### 2.2.2 Open Layers (Relaxed Layering) - A layer may skip and access any lower layer directly. - **Advantage:** Less boilerplate, more direct. - **Disadvantage:** Tight coupling, harder to maintain. > **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Strict/closed layering is preferred for **enterprise-grade systems**; open layering is acceptable for **small internal tools**. ### 2.2.3 Layers and Cohesion Each layer must have **high cohesion** (single responsibility) and **low coupling** to its neighbours. The **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** from SOLID is often used: high-level modules (domain) should not depend on low-level modules (DB); both should depend on **abstractions** (interfaces). --- ## 2.3 Concurrency Problems — Detailed Taxonomy ### 2.3.1 Race Condition Occurs when the outcome of a program depends on the **non-deterministic timing** of interleaved thread executions. **Classic Lost-Update Example (Banking):** - Account balance = ₹1000. - Thread A reads 1000, computes 1000+500=1500. - Thread B reads 1000, computes 1000+200=1200. - Thread A writes 1500, then Thread B writes 1200. The ₹500 deposit is **lost**! ### 2.3.2 Deadlock Two or more threads are **permanently blocked**, each waiting for a resource held by the other. The **Coffman Conditions** (all four must hold): 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — resource is non-shareable. 2. **Hold and Wait** — thread holds a resource while waiting for another. 3. **No Preemption** — resources cannot be forcibly taken. 4. **Circular Wait** — a circular chain of threads waiting on each other. **Prevention:** Break at least one condition (e.g., acquire locks in a **global order**). ### 2.3.3 Starvation A thread is perpetually denied access to a resource because other threads (often higher-priority) keep acquiring it. ### 2.3.4 Livelock Threads are **actively executing** but make no progress (e.g., two people in a corridor constantly stepping aside for each other). ### 2.3.5 Memory Consistency / Visibility Problem A thread writes to a variable, but other threads **do not see the updated value** due to CPU caching, compiler reordering, or register storage. Solved by `volatile`, `synchronized`, or memory barriers. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Memorize for ESE** | # | Concept | Definition / Rule | Solution / Pattern | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Layered Pattern Layers** | Presentation → Service → Domain → Persistence → DB | Strict (Closed) Layering | | 2 | **Layer Skipping Rule** | A layer MUST NOT skip its immediate neighbour | Use interfaces (DIP) | | 3 | **Race Condition** | Outcome depends on thread interleaving | `synchronized`, `Lock`, transactions | | 4 | **Deadlock Coffman** | 4 conditions must hold simultaneously | Resource ordering, lock timeout | | 5 | **Starvation** | Thread never gets the lock | Fair locks (`ReentrantLock(true)`) | | 6 | **Livelock** | Threads keep retrying/reacting | Random backoff, exponential retry | | 7 | **Lost Update** | Concurrent write overwrites valid data | Optimistic/Pessimistic locking | | 8 | **ACID** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | DB transactions | | 9 | **Isolation Levels** | READ UNCOMMITTED → READ COMMITTED → REPEATABLE READ → SERIALIZABLE | Higher = safer, lower = faster | | 10 | **Optimistic Locking** | Version number / timestamp check | `@Version` in JPA | | 11 | **Pessimistic Locking** | `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` | Row-level lock | | 12 | **Thread-Safe Singleton** | Only one instance in multi-threaded context | Double-Checked Locking, Enum | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Banking Systems:** Layered architecture with pessimistic locking ensures money is never double-spent. - **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Use optimistic locking for inventory to maximize throughput. - **Reservation Systems (IRCTC):** Use optimistic concurrency with version numbers to handle millions of concurrent bookings. - **Healthcare EHR Systems:** Strict layering for HIPAA compliance; transactions for medical record integrity. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Lost Update Problem (Bank Account) ### Problem Setup A bank account with **balance = ₹1000** is concurrently accessed by: - **Thread A:** Deposits ₹500. - **Thread B:** Withdraws ₹200. **Expected final balance:** ₹1000 + ₹500 − ₹200 = **₹1300** ### Step-by-Step Interleaved Execution (Without Lock) **Step 1:** Thread A executes `read balance` → loads **1000** into CPU register. **Step 2:** Thread B is scheduled and executes `read balance` → loads **1000** into its CPU register. **Step 3:** Thread A computes `1000 + 500 = 1500`. **Step 4:** Thread B computes `1000 − 200 = 800`. **Step 5:** Thread A writes `1500` to memory. **Step 6:** Thread B writes `800` to memory. **Final balance = ₹800** ❌ (₹500 deposit silently lost!) ### Mathematical Representation of the Race Let $B_t$ be the balance at time $t$, and $T_A, T_B$ the operations: $$ B_{\text{final}} = B_0 \oplus_A \oplus_B $$ where $\oplus$ is the operation. Without atomicity, the read-modify-write cycle is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_A : &\quad r_A = \text{read}(B_0) \\ &\quad w_A = r_A + 500 \\ &\quad \text{write}(B, w_A) \\ T_B : &\quad r_B = \text{read}(B) \\ &\quad w_B = r_B - 200 \\ &\quad \text{write}(B, w_B) \end{aligned} $$ If $T_B$ reads *after* $T_A$ writes, then $B_{\text{final}} = (1000 + 500) - 200 = 1300$ ✓ If $T_B$ reads *before* $T_A$ writes (as above), then $B_{\text{final}} = 1000 - 200 = 800$ ❌ ### Solution: Pessimistic Locking (Sequential Execution) Wrap the read-modify-write in a synchronized block: $$ B_{\text{final}} = \big( (B_0 + 500) - 200 \big) = 1300 \quad \text{(deterministic)} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Concurrency Solutions | Solution | Mechanism | Pros | Cons | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **`synchronized` keyword** | Intrinsic monitor lock on object | Simple, built-in | Coarse-grained, no fairness | Small critical sections | | **`ReentrantLock`** | Explicit lock API | Fairness, tryLock, interruptible | Verbose | Long critical sections | | **Optimistic Locking** | Version column check at commit | High throughput, deadlock-free | Retry overhead on conflict | Low contention (read-mostly) | | **Pessimistic Locking** | `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` | Prevents conflict upfront | Reduced concurrency | High contention (hot rows) | | **Immutable Objects** | No state mutation | Naturally thread-safe | Limited use cases | Value objects, DTOs | | **ThreadLocal** | Per-thread variable copy | No synchronization needed | Memory overhead | Per-request context (e.g., user session) | | **`ConcurrentHashMap`** | Lock striping / CAS | High concurrency for maps | Slightly complex | Caches, registries | | **Atomic Variables** | Compare-And-Swap (CAS) hardware primitive | Lock-free, fast | Limited to single variables | Counters, flags | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Deadlock Detection ### The Dining Philosophers Analogy 5 philosophers sit at a round table with 5 forks. Each needs 2 forks to eat. If all pick up the *left* fork simultaneously → **deadlock**. ### Mathematical Formalization Let $L = \{P_1, P_2, ..., P_n\}$ be threads and $R = \{F_1, F_2, ..., F_m\}$ be resources. A **deadlock** exists if there exists a circular wait cycle: $$ P_{i_1} \rightarrow F_{a} \rightarrow P_{i_2} \rightarrow F_{b} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow P_{i_1} $$ ### Resource Ordering Solution Force all philosophers to acquire forks in **ascending order** (lower index first). This breaks the **Circular Wait** condition, preventing deadlock. ``` Philosopher P1 picks up F1 then F2 ✓ Philosopher P2 picks up F2 then F3 ✗ (P1 and P2 still contended for F2) ... enforce: always pick lower-numbered fork first. ``` This eliminates the cycle and guarantees deadlock freedom. --- ## 3.4 Layered Architecture — Request Flow Analysis A typical **user login** request traverses the layers as follows: **Step 1:** Browser sends `POST /login` → **Presentation Layer** (Spring `@Controller`). **Step 2:** Controller validates input, calls **Service Layer** (`AuthService.login(user, pass)`). **Step 3:** Service Layer applies business rules (e.g., "max 5 failed attempts before lockout"), delegates to **Domain Layer** (`User.authenticate(...)`). **Step 4:** Domain Layer calls **Persistence Layer** (`UserRepository.findByUsername(...)`). **Step 5:** Persistence Layer executes SQL via JPA/Hibernate → **Database**. **Step 6:** Result bubbles up: DB → Repository → Domain → Service → Controller → **View** (JSP/JSON). > **Total layers traversed = 5**, with **4 boundary crossings**. Each crossing is a potential **performance bottleneck** and a **failure point** — justifying the rule of *closed* layering for clean separation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Layered Architecture — Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD A1[User Browser] --> A2[Presentation Layer
JSP / REST Controller] A2 --> A3[Application Service Layer
AuthService OrderService] A3 --> A4[Domain Layer
Business Rules Entities] A4 --> A5[Persistence Layer
DAO Repository JPA] A5 --> A6[Database Layer
MySQL PostgreSQL Oracle] A6 -.Result.-> A5 A5 -.Entity.-> A4 A4 -.Domain Object.-> A3 A3 -.DTO.-> A2 A2 -.HTML JSON.-> A1 style A1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A3 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A5 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A6 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = request flow (downward). Dotted arrows = response/data flow (upward). Each box represents a logical layer; layers are **isolated and replaceable**. --- ## 4.2 Concurrency Problem — Race Condition Timeline ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T1 as Thread A participant Mem as Shared Balance participant T2 as Thread B Note over T1,T2: Initial Balance = 1000 T1->>Mem: READ 1000 T2->>Mem: READ 1000 T1->>T1: Compute 1000 + 500 = 1500 T2->>T2: Compute 1000 - 200 = 800 T1->>Mem: WRITE 1500 T2->>Mem: WRITE 800 Note over Mem: Final = 800 LOST UPDATE Note right of T2: Race Condition Detected ``` > **Annotation:** The interleaving order between T1 and T2 is **non-deterministic** — at the next run, the order may differ. This is the *definition* of a race condition. --- ## 4.3 Deadlock — Circular Wait Diagram ```mermaid graph LR P1[Thread P1
Holds Lock A
Waits for Lock B] P2[Thread P2
Holds Lock B
Waits for Lock A] P1 -- needs --> L1[Lock B] P2 -- needs --> L2[Lock A] L1 -. held by .-> P2 L2 -. held by .-> P1 style P1 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L1 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L2 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Annotation:** The cycle `P1 → Lock B → P2 → Lock A → P1` satisfies all four **Coffman conditions**, producing a permanent deadlock. Break the cycle via **global lock ordering**. --- ## 4.4 Layered Architecture — Layer Responsibilities Matrix (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SL1 [Presentation Layer] P1[Handles HTTP requests] P2[Renders views / returns JSON] P3[Input validation format] end subgraph SL2 [Service Layer] S1[Use case orchestration] S2[Transaction boundaries] S3[Cross-cutting concerns logging security] end subgraph SL3 [Domain Layer] D1[Core business rules] D2[Entity behaviour] D3[Domain invariants] end subgraph SL4 [Persistence Layer] DB1[CRUD operations] DB2[ORM mappings] DB3[Query construction] end SL1 --> SL2 SL2 --> SL3 SL3 --> SL4 style SL1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px style SL2 fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px style SL3 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px style SL4 fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Subgraphs show the **responsibilities** of each layer. The arrows enforce the **one-direction dependency** rule (Presentation depends on Service, Service on Domain, etc.). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *List any **four characteristics** of Enterprise Applications and explain why they make such systems architecturally complex.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Persistent Storage of Data** — Data must survive program restarts and is stored in relational or NoSQL databases, requiring careful schema design and data access layers. 2. **High Concurrency** — Hundreds to thousands of users access the system simultaneously, demanding robust transaction and thread management. 3. **Complex Business Logic** — Encodes domain-specific rules (tax, billing, workflows) that frequently change with business needs. 4. **Distributed Deployment** — Components run on multiple servers/JVMs, introducing network-related issues like latency and partial failures. These factors collectively demand **layered architectures, well-defined interfaces, and concurrency control mechanisms** to keep the system maintainable and reliable. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *What is a **Race Condition**? Give a simple example.* **Model Answer:** A **Race Condition** is a concurrency defect where the behaviour of a program depends on the **non-deterministic interleaving** of two or more threads accessing shared mutable data. **Example:** Two threads simultaneously call `increment()` on a shared counter initialized to 0. Both threads read value `0`, compute `0+1=1`, and both write `1`. The final counter is `1` instead of the expected `2` — a **lost update** race condition. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU ESE – Module 2 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **Layered Architectural Pattern** with a neat diagram. Discuss the difference between **closed (strict)** and **open (relaxed)** layering with examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the major **concurrency problems** in enterprise applications. With a suitable example, explain the **Lost Update** problem and outline two solutions. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Layered Architectural Pattern (7 marks):** The Layered Pattern (N-Tier architecture) partitions a system into a stack of **logically distinct, horizontally-grouped layers**, where each layer provides services to the one above and consumes services of the one below. **Standard Layers (top to bottom):** 1. **Presentation Layer** — User interface, controllers, views (e.g., Spring `@Controller`, JSP, React). 2. **Application/Service Layer** — Coordinates use cases, defines transaction boundaries. 3. **Domain/Business Layer** — Core business rules, entities, domain services. 4. **Persistence Layer** — Repositories, DAOs, ORM (e.g., JPA, Hibernate). 5. **Database Layer** — RDBMS engine. **Closed (Strict) Layering:** - A layer communicates **only with its immediate neighbour**. - *Example:* A `Controller` calls `AuthService` (Service Layer), which calls `UserRepository` (Persistence). The Controller **must not** directly call `UserRepository`. - ✅ **Pros:** Loose coupling, easy testing, swappable layers. - ❌ **Cons:** May introduce "pass-through" code. **Open (Relaxed) Layering:** - A layer may skip and access any lower layer. - *Example:* A `Controller` directly calls `UserRepository` for a simple read. - ✅ **Pros:** Less boilerplate, more direct. - ❌ **Cons:** Tight coupling, harder to evolve. **Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1):** *(1 mark for diagram)* **(b) Concurrency Problems & Lost Update (7 marks):** **Major Concurrency Problems:** 1. **Race Condition** — Outcome depends on thread interleaving. 2. **Deadlock** — Circular wait among threads holding locks. 3. **Starvation** — Thread perpetually denied resources. 4. **Livelock** — Threads active but making no progress. 5. **Memory Visibility** — Writes not visible across threads. 6. **Lost Update** — Concurrent writes silently overwrite each other. **Lost Update — Example:** Two bank tellers update the same account concurrently. Initial balance = ₹1000. - Teller A reads ₹1000, deposits ₹500 → writes ₹1500. - Teller B (at the same time) reads ₹1000 (stale value), withdraws ₹200 → writes ₹800. Final = ₹800, but correct = ₹1300. The ₹500 deposit is **lost**. **Solution 1 — Pessimistic Locking:** Acquire a row-level lock (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`) before reading; the second transaction waits. Ensures **serializability** but reduces throughput. **Solution 2 — Optimistic Locking (Versioning):** Add a `version` column. Each update checks: `UPDATE account SET balance=?, version=version+1 WHERE id=? AND version=?`. If 0 rows updated → conflict; client retries. Higher throughput, deadlock-free. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **draw the layered diagram upside-down** (DB on top). Remember: **Presentation is on top, Database is on the bottom**. > - In Lost Update questions, students **forget to show the stale read** explicitly. Always draw the timeline showing what *each thread reads* before writing. > - Many confuse **Deadlock with Starvation**. Deadlock = **all threads stuck**; Starvation = **one thread stuck while others progress**. > - Do not write "use mutex" without specifying *which* lock mechanism (`synchronized`, `ReentrantLock`, DB row-lock). Examiners deduct for vagueness. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Layered Architecture** of a typical e-commerce web application. Identify **one responsibility** of each layer. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What is a **Deadlock**? State and explain the **four Coffman conditions**. How can a deadlock be prevented by **resource ordering**? *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) E-Commerce Layered Architecture (7 marks):** For an e-commerce application (e.g., Amazon-like): | Layer | Responsibility | Example Component | |---|---|---| | **Presentation** | Render product list, handle cart UI | `ProductController.java`, React UI | | **Service** | Orchestrate "Place Order" use case | `OrderService.placeOrder()` | | **Domain** | Apply discount rules, tax calculation | `Order.applyDiscount()`, `PricingPolicy` | | **Persistence** | Save/retrieve orders, products | `OrderRepository` (JPA) | | **Database** | Stores `orders`, `products`, `users` tables | PostgreSQL | **Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.4).** *(1 mark for diagram, 4 marks for explanation, 2 marks for layer-wise responsibility)* **(b) Deadlock & Coffman Conditions (7 marks):** **Definition:** A *deadlock* is a state in which two or more threads are **permanently blocked**, each waiting for a resource held by another thread in the group. **Four Coffman Conditions (all must hold simultaneously):** 1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode (e.g., a lock on a row). 2. **Hold and Wait:** A thread holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other threads. 3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread; they must be released voluntarily. 4. **Circular Wait:** A circular chain of threads exists, where each thread holds a resource the next thread in the chain is waiting for: $$ P_1 \to R_a \to P_2 \to R_b \to P_1 $$ **Prevention via Resource Ordering:** - Assign a **global numerical order** to all locks (e.g., Lock A = 1, Lock B = 2). - **Rule:** Every thread must acquire locks in **strictly ascending order**. - This **eliminates the Circular Wait** condition, making deadlock impossible. - *Example:* Thread 1 acquires Lock A (1) then Lock B (2). Thread 2 also acquires Lock A first, then Lock B. No circular wait can form because everyone queues at Lock A first. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B):** > - Listing only 2 or 3 Coffman conditions is a **frequent cause of losing 2–3 marks**. All four must be named and explained. > - For resource ordering, students often just say "order the locks" without giving a **concrete numerical rule**. Always include an example sequence. > - Don't confuse *deadlock prevention* (design-time, breaks a Coffman condition) with *deadlock detection* (runtime, allows deadlock but recovers). --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Enterprise Applications** = persistent + concurrent + distributed + business-rule-driven. - ✅ **Layered Pattern** = Presentation → Service → Domain → Persistence → DB (top to bottom). - ✅ **Closed Layering** = strict neighbour-only access (preferred for EAs). - ✅ **Open Layering** = relaxed, any layer can skip (use only for small tools). - ✅ **Layer Skipping Violation** = tight coupling, anti-pattern. - ✅ **Race Condition** = outcome depends on non-deterministic thread interleaving. - ✅ **Deadlock** = 4 Coffman conditions; prevent by breaking *Circular Wait* via **resource ordering**. - ✅ **Starvation** = thread perpetually denied; fix with **fair locks**. - ✅ **Livelock** = threads active but no progress; fix with **random backoff**. - ✅ **Lost Update** = solved by **Optimistic** (version) or **Pessimistic** (`SELECT FOR UPDATE`) locking. - ✅ **Memory Visibility** = solved by `volatile`, `synchronized`, or memory barriers. - ✅ **`synchronized` vs `ReentrantLock`** = intrinsic vs explicit; latter offers fairness, tryLock, interruptibility. - ✅ **ACID properties** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the bedrock of enterprise transactions. - ✅ **Isolation Levels** = READ UNCOMMITTED → READ COMMITTED → REPEATABLE READ → SERIALIZABLE. - ✅ **Kitchens of KTU Questions** = "Explain layered pattern with diagram", "List Coffman conditions", "Solve Lost Update with example", "Compare open vs closed layering". - ✅ **Common Examiner Traps** = (1) Drawing layered diagram inverted, (2) Confusing deadlock with starvation, (3) Vague locking solutions, (4) Missing one Coffman condition. > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, dedicate **1 mark to the diagram, 4 marks to concept, 4 marks to example, 3 marks to solution, 2 marks to comparison/trade-offs**. This aligns with the KTU valuation key.

topics.title: Component Software: Nature of Components and Reuse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Component Software: Nature of Components and Reuse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Component Software (Componentware)** is a software engineering paradigm that emphasizes the **separation of concerns** and **reusability** by constructing software systems through the assembly of pre-built, self-contained, well-specified software units called **components**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (aligned with Szyperski's classical definition), a component is a *software element that conforms to a component model and can be independently deployed and composed without modification by third parties*.

**Working Definition (KTU Board Standard):**

A **Software Component** is a modular, deployable, and replaceable part of a system that encapsulates a well-defined set of **functionality**, **state**, and **identity**, exposes its behavior through strictly typed **interfaces**, and possesses explicit **context dependencies** (provided and required interfaces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Szyperski's Component Definition (Canonical):** *"A software component is a unit of composition with contractually specified interfaces and explicit context dependencies only. A software component can be deployed independently and is subject to composition by third parties."* — This is the textbook definition examiners love to quote.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are assembling a **high-end gaming PC**:
- You do not manufacture the RAM, GPU, or motherboard from scratch.
- You buy **standardized parts** (DIMM RAM, PCIe GPU) that have **documented specifications** (DDR5, 16x PCIe 5.0).
- These parts come from **different vendors** (Corsair, ASUS, NVIDIA), yet they **plug together** because they follow industry-standard form factors.
- You can **swap** a GTX 4060 for an RTX 5070 without rewriting your motherboard's firmware.

**This is exactly how Component Software works.** A software component is like a hardware chip with:
- A **datasheet** (its interface specification)
- A **physical package** (its implementation)
- **Standardized pins** (its provided/required interfaces)
- **Vendor independence** (it can be from anywhere, as long as it conforms)

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Component Software

- **Interface Compliance:** **100% conformance** to published contract is mandatory.
- **Granularity:** Components range from **fine-grained** (single function) to **coarse-grained** (entire business service).
- **Reusability Rate:** Industry benchmark is **>60%** of LOC reused in mature component-based systems.
- **Independence Metric:** A component must be **deployable independently** of the system at compile time and link time.

### 1.4 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Component** | A reusable, deployable software unit with well-defined interfaces. |
| **Interface** | A contract specifying *what* a component provides (PI) and *requires* (RI). |
| **Composition** | The mechanism by which components are combined to form a system. |
| **Component Model** | The set of rules (naming, binding, metadata, customization) governing component interaction. |
| **Connector** | The mechanism that mediates interaction between components. |
| **Port** | An interaction point through which a component communicates. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nature of Components — The Three Pillars

A component is uniquely characterized by **three fundamental properties**, which the KTU examiner expects students to articulate clearly:

#### **Pillar 1: Independent Deployability**
A component must be a *physical unit of deployment*. It can be packaged (e.g., `.jar`, `.dll`, `.war`, `.ear`, `.so`, `.dylib`) and distributed without requiring source-level changes to consumers.

#### **Pillar 2: Composability (Third-Party Composition)**
A component must be composable by **parties other than its original developer**. This implies the interface contract must be stable, documented, and language/compiler-independent.

#### **Pillar 3: Context Dependency Explicitness**
All non-functional assumptions (OS, runtime, dependencies, hardware) must be **declared explicitly** in a manifest or descriptor, never hidden inside code.

### 2.2 The Three Views of a Component

A component is best understood via **three orthogonal views** (the 3-in-1 model often tested in KTU):

1. **Specification View (Black-Box):** What the component does — its **interface contract**, pre/post conditions, behavioral semantics.
2. **Implementation View (White-Box / Glass-Box):** How the component achieves it — internal algorithms, data structures, state. **Hidden** from external observers.
3. **Deployment View (Grey-Box / Packaging):** The physical artifact (binary + metadata + dependencies) that is shipped, registered, and activated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Hiding (Parnas, 1972)** is the cornerstone: a component must hide its implementation and expose *only* its specification. KTU examiners frequently test this with a question on **"Why is encapsulation the key property of a component?"**

### 2.3 Reuse — Nature, Levels, and Forms

Software Reuse is the **systematic practice of using existing software artifacts** to build new systems. Reuse is the **primary economic justification** for component software.

#### **Reuse Levels (Caldiera & Basili — KTU Standard Taxonomy)**

| Reuse Level | What is Reused | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Reuse** | Source code, libraries | `java.util.HashMap`, `printf()` |
| **Design Reuse** | Architectural patterns, design patterns | MVC, Singleton, Observer |
| **Architectural Reuse** | System structure, framework | Spring, .NET, Rails |
| **Specification Reuse** | Requirements, use-cases | Reusable requirement templates |
| **Test Reuse** | Test cases, harnesses | JUnit, Selenium scripts |
| **Component Reuse** | Fully packaged binary units | COM, EJB, OSGi bundles |
| **Service Reuse** | Network-accessible units | Web Services (REST/SOAP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Component Reuse** sits between **Code Reuse** and **Service Reuse** on the granularity spectrum. It is *coarser* than code (it has its own identity, state, lifecycle) but *tighter* than a service (it is usually in-process or at least process-local, not network-bound).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $$R_{LOC} = \frac{L_{reused}}{L_{total}} \times 100$$ | **Line-of-Code Reuse Ratio** | Percentage (%) |
| $$R_{func} = \frac{N_{reused\_functions}}{N_{total\_functions}} \times 100$$ | **Functional Reuse Ratio** | Percentage (%) |
| $$C_{reuse} = \frac{C_{reuse\_effort}}{C_{total\_effort}} \times 100$$ | **Cost Reuse Ratio** | Percentage (%) |
| $$S_{reuse} = 1 + \frac{\Delta L_{reused}}{L_{new}}$$ | **Size Stability Index** | Dimensionless |
| $$CC = \frac{N_{comp}(N_{comp}-1)}{2}$$ | **Maximum Coupling Count** between $N_{comp}$ components | Integer |
| $$Coh = \frac{N_{internal}}{N_{internal} + N_{external}}$$ | **Cohesion Metric** (internal vs external connections) | Normalized [0,1] |
| $$RCB = \frac{L_{reuse\_success}}{L_{reuse\_attempted}}$$ | **Reuse Cost-Benefit Ratio** | Ratio |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Systems:** A banking application reuses authentication components, logging components, and database access components across 50+ internal applications.
- **Embedded Systems:** AUTOSAR (Automotive Open System Architecture) standardizes components for ECU software — enabling reuse across car models.
- **Web Development:** Component frameworks (React, Angular, Vue) enforce component-based reuse at the UI tier.
- **IoT & Edge:** Eclipse Kura provides a component model for IoT gateways where OSGi bundles are reused across deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reuse Ratio from First Principles

Let us derive the **Reuse Ratio ($R_{LOC}$)** rigorously, as expected in KTU ESE derivations.

**Step 1 — Define the Variables.**

Let:
- $L_{total}$ = Total lines of code in the new system under development.
- $L_{new}$ = Lines of code that are written from scratch (non-reused).
- $L_{reused}$ = Lines of code that originate from an existing reusable component.

By the principle of partition:

$$
L_{total} = L_{new} + L_{reused}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Reuse as a Ratio.**

Reuse, by definition, is the proportion of the total code base that was reused. Hence:

$$
R_{LOC} = \frac{L_{reused}}{L_{total}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Percentage (KTU convention).**

Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$
R_{LOC} = \frac{L_{reused}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $L_{reused} = L_{total} - L_{new}$.**

$$
R_{LOC} = \frac{L_{total} - L_{new}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_{LOC} = \left(1 - \frac{L_{new}}{L_{total}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style).**

Suppose a new ERP system is being built:
- Total LOC after assembly = $L_{total}$ = 100,000.
- Newly written code = $L_{new}$ = 35,000.
- Reused code from components = $L_{reused}$ = 65,000.

Applying the formula:

$$
R_{LOC} = \frac{65{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100\% = 65\%
$$

$$
\text{Verification via complement:} \quad \left(1 - \frac{35{,}000}{100{,}000}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.35) \times 100\% = 65\% \;\checkmark
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Coupling Count in a Component Graph

**Step 1 — A graph-theoretic interpretation.**

Model the software system as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of components ($N_{comp}$ vertices).
- $E$ = set of inter-component interactions (edges).

**Step 2 — Maximum number of pairwise couplings.**

In a complete graph, every vertex connects to every other vertex. The number of unordered pairs is given by the binomial coefficient:

$$
CC_{max} = \binom{N_{comp}}{2} = \frac{N_{comp}(N_{comp} - 1)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Coupling Count.**

If $E_{actual}$ is the count of actual inter-component dependencies:

$$
CC_{actual} = \vert E_{actual} \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Coupling Density.**

$$
\rho_{coupling} = \frac{CC_{actual}}{CC_{max}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example.**

Given $N_{comp} = 6$ components forming 9 actual interconnections:

$$
CC_{max} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15
$$

$$
\rho_{coupling} = \frac{9}{15} = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

This means 60% of the theoretical maximum coupling paths are realized. For high reusability, $\rho_{coupling}$ should be **as low as possible** (preferring layered/star topologies over mesh).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Component vs. Object vs. Service

| Property | **Object** | **Component** | **Service** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Fine-grained | Medium-grained | Coarse-grained |
| **Identity** | Class-level | Instance-level with deployment identity | URI-based |
| **Lifecycle** | Tied to enclosing process | Independent deployment unit | Long-running, network-addressable |
| **Inheritance** | Supported | **Discouraged** (white-box reuse is forbidden) | Not applicable |
| **Interface Coupling** | Binary contract | Strict contract, may be platform-specific | **Loose coupling** (WSDL/OpenAPI) |
| **Communication** | Method call | Method call / message | RPC, REST, message queue |
| **State** | May be stateful | Typically stateful or stateless | Often stateless (functional) |
| **Reuse Mode** | Inheritance / instantiation | Composition (black-box) | Composition + late binding |
| **Distribution** | In-process | Same process or inter-process | Always cross-process / cross-network |
| **Third-Party Deploy** | No (developer assembles) | **Yes (hallmark)** | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **The defining axis:** A **Component is a Service's cousin but lives in your address space**. The transition from *Component* to *Service* is essentially the transition from *binary-local* to *network-distributed* coupling. This is a frequent 14-mark question theme.

### 3.4 Derivation: Reuse Cost-Benefit Equation

**Step 1 — Define Cost of Development Without Reuse.**

Without reuse, total development effort $E_{no\_reuse}$ is proportional to system size:

$$
E_{no\_reuse} = k \cdot L_{total}
$$

where $k$ is the average effort per LOC.

**Step 2 — Define Cost With Reuse.**

With reuse, new effort is needed only for $L_{new}$, plus an **integration effort** $E_{int}$ and a **learning/qualification effort** $E_{learn}$:

$$
E_{reuse} = k \cdot L_{new} + E_{int} + E_{learn}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost Savings.**

$$
\Delta E = E_{no\_reuse} - E_{reuse} = k(L_{total} - L_{new}) - E_{int} - E_{learn}
$$

**Step 4 — Reuse Profitability Condition.**

Reuse is profitable only if:

$$
\Delta E > 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; k(L_{total} - L_{new}) > E_{int} + E_{learn}
$$

Equivalently, the **break-even reuse ratio** is:

$$
R_{BE} = \frac{E_{int} + E_{learn}}{k \cdot L_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

If $R_{LOC} > R_{BE}$, reuse is **economically justified**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Component Model — Three-View Architecture

```mermaid

    subgraph SPEC["SPECIFICATION VIEW BLACK BOX"]
        PI["Provided Interface P1, P2"]

    subgraph IMPL["IMPLEMENTATION VIEW WHITE BOX"]
        ALGO["Algorithms and Logic"]

        DATA["Data Structures"]

    subgraph DEPLOY["DEPLOYMENT VIEW GREY BOX"]

        DEPS["Dependency Declarations"]

    PI --> IMPL
    RI --> IMPL
    IMPL --> DEPLOY
    CONTRACT --> PI
    CONTRACT --> RI

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a software component. List any FOUR essential characteristics a unit must possess to qualify as a component.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition:** A software component is a unit of composition with contractually specified interfaces and explicit context dependencies only. It can be deployed independently and is subject to composition by third parties. *(1 Mark)*

**Four essential characteristics: *(2 Marks — ½ mark each)***
1. **Independent deployability** — packaged as a self-contained unit (e.g., `.jar`, `.dll`).
2. **Composability** — combinable by third parties via defined interfaces.
3. **Information hiding** — implementation details are not exposed to consumers.
4. **Documented contract** — pre/post conditions and behavioral guarantees are publicly specified.

*(Other valid answers: explicit context dependencies, standardized interaction model, identity persistence.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between code reuse and component reuse with a one-line example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | **Code Reuse** | **Component Reuse** |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Function or class level | Coarse-grained packaged binary |
| Coupling | Often source-level / compile-time | Interface-level / runtime composition |
| Example | `String.toUpperCase()` from JDK | Reusing a JavaBean EJB jar in an enterprise application |
| Independence | Tied to source tree | Independently deployable |

*(2 marks for the distinction table + 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

---

**Q3.A [14 Marks] — Question A** *(KTU University Exam — Jul 2024)*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three views of a software component** (specification, implementation, deployment). Describe how each contributes to the principles of **information hiding** and **independence of deployment**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the **nature of interfaces** in component software. Differentiate between **provided interfaces** and **required interfaces** with the help of a UML notation example. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Three Views of a Software Component *(7 Marks)***

A component is best understood through three orthogonal views, each serving a distinct stakeholder and lifecycle phase.

**1. Specification View (Black-Box View) — *(2 Marks)***
The specification view presents the component as an *opaque* unit. It describes only **what** the component does — its public interfaces, the contracts it offers, pre-conditions that must hold before invocation, post-conditions guaranteed after invocation, and any invariants maintained across the lifetime. This view is consumed by *integrators* and *composers* who must understand how to bind the component into a larger system without needing access to its internal logic.

**2. Implementation View (White-Box / Glass-Box View) — *(2 Marks)***
The implementation view reveals **how** the component achieves its specification. It includes internal algorithms, data structures, state management logic, and intra-component control flow. This view is the concern of the *component developer* and is deliberately shielded from external consumers to enforce **information hiding (Parnas, 1972)** — the same encapsulation principle that underpins object-orientation but applied at a coarser granularity.

**3. Deployment View (Grey-Box View) — *(2 Marks)***
The deployment view is the *physical artifact* — the binary executables, the manifest or descriptor (XML/JSON), dependency declarations, version stamps, and licensing metadata. This view is consumed by *system administrators and deployment infrastructure* (e.g., an OSGi runtime, an EJB container, or a Windows COM registrar).

**Contribution to Information Hiding & Independence:** *(1 Mark)*
The separation guarantees that consumers depend only on the specification; changes in implementation or packaging do not ripple to consumers. This independence is the *operational definition* of independent deployability.

**(b) Nature of Interfaces *(7 Marks)***

An **interface** is a *named set of operation signatures* that defines a contract between a component and its environment. In component software, interfaces are first-class citizens — they are not bound to any single component class.

**Provided Interfaces (PI):** *(2 Marks)*
A provided interface is one that the component **offers** to its environment. It represents the functionality the component publishes for others to consume. UML notation: a *lollipop* symbol — a circle connected to the component's boundary via a solid line. Example: A `PaymentService` component provides the `IPaymentProcessor` interface with methods like `processPayment()`, `refund()`.

**Required Interfaces (RI):** *(2 Marks)*
A required interface is one that the component **depends on** — it must be supplied by another component in the composition. UML notation: a *socket* symbol — a half-circle (cup) attached to the component. Example: A `BusinessService` component *requires* `ILogger` so it can record transactions.

**Composition Mechanism:** *(2 Marks)*
Composition occurs when the **lollipop** of one component plugs into the **socket** of another. A `Composer` (e.g., an IoC container, OSGi Service Binder, or COM runtime) performs this binding at runtime, ensuring loose coupling — neither component references the other by class identity, only by interface contract.

**Example UML Notation Sketch (ASCII):**

```
[PaymentService] o==== IPaymentProcessor
                \
                 \--- (consumed by [CheckoutController] which has)
[CheckoutController] U==== IPaymentProcessor
```

---

**Q3.B [14 Marks] — Question B (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss the **nature and levels of software reuse**. Explain **Caldiera and Basili's reuse taxonomy** with examples for each level. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What are the **benefits and challenges of component-based reuse**? Derive the **break-even reuse ratio formula** and explain its significance in deciding when reuse is economically justified. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nature and Levels of Software Reuse *(7 Marks)***

**Nature of Reuse *(2 Marks):***
Software reuse is the systematic incorporation of *previously produced* software artifacts — code, designs, specifications, or tests — into new systems. Its foundation is the observation that many software systems share *common requirements* (authentication, persistence, validation). Reuse leverages this overlap to reduce development effort, defect density, and time-to-market.

**Caldiera and Basili's Reuse Taxonomy *(5 Marks — 1 each + examples):***

1. **Code Reuse** — Reusing source code units like functions, classes, libraries. *Example:* Reusing `java.util.Collections.sort()` in a new module.
2. **Design Reuse** — Reusing proven design structures. *Example:* Adopting the MVC pattern to organize a new web app.
3. **Architectural Reuse** — Reusing system-level structure. *Example:* Building a new SaaS on the Spring Boot framework.
4. **Specification Reuse** — Reusing requirement artifacts. *Example:* Reusing a "User Login" use-case template across projects.
5. **Test Reuse** — Reusing test scripts and harnesses. *Example:* Reusing Selenium scripts for regression on multiple builds.

*(Higher levels like Component Reuse and Service Reuse extend this taxonomy in modern literature.)*

**(b) Benefits, Challenges & Break-Even Formula *(7 Marks)***

**Benefits *(2 Marks):***
- Increased productivity and faster delivery.
- Reduced defect density (proven components are more reliable).
- Lower long-term maintenance cost.
- Improved interoperability and standardization.

**Challenges *(2 Marks):*
- Higher upfront investment in creating reusable components.
- **Not-invented-here (NIH) syndrome** among developers.
- Maintenance burden — a buggy shared component affects all consumers.
- Difficulty finding, qualifying, and integrating components.

**Break-Even Derivation *(3 Marks):***

Let:
- $E_{no\_reuse} = k \cdot L_{total}$ = effort if written from scratch.
- $E_{reuse} = k \cdot L_{new} + E_{int} + E_{learn}$ = effort with reuse.

Savings: $\Delta E = k(L_{total} - L_{new}) - E_{int} - E_{learn}$.

Setting $\Delta E = 0$ for break-even:

$$
R_{BE} = \frac{E_{int} + E_{learn}}{k \cdot L_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

If the actual $R_{LOC} > R_{BE}$, reuse is **economically justified**. Otherwise, building from scratch may be cheaper — a critical insight for project managers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating *Component* and *Object*:** A component is **NOT just a big object**. The biggest marks-deduction comes from missing the *independent deployability* and *third-party composition* properties.
> 2. **Confusing Provided/Required Interface symbols:** Lollipop = **Provided**, Socket (cup) = **Required**. Mixing these up in a 7-mark diagram question can cost 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Information-Hiding link:** Any 14-mark question on components demands a *definition of encapsulation in the context of components* — not just objects.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps in Part B:** Even if you know the formula, KTU examiners allocate marks for each algebraic step. Showing $\Delta E > 0$ condition explicitly is mandatory.
> 5. **Ignoring the break-even condition:** Students often quote reuse benefits but forget the **economic constraint** — not every reuse decision is profitable.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Software Component** is a unit of composition with **contractually specified interfaces** and **explicit context dependencies**, deployable independently and composable by third parties *(Szyperski)*.
- [x] **Three pillars of a component:** Independent deployability, third-party composability, explicit context dependency.
- [x] **Three views:** Specification (black-box), Implementation (white-box), Deployment (grey-box).
- [x] **Information Hiding (Parnas, 1972)** is the foundational principle — implementation is hidden, specification is exposed.
- [x] **Provided Interface** = Lollipop (○) | **Required Interface** = Socket (⊃).
- [x] **Reuse Ratio Formula:** $R_{LOC} = \frac{L_{reused}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Break-Even Reuse Ratio:** $R_{BE} = \frac{E_{int} + E_{learn}}{k \cdot L_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Coupling Count:** $CC_{max} = \frac{N_{comp}(N_{comp}-1)}{2}$.
- [x] **Caldiera & Basili's Reuse Levels:** Code → Design → Architecture → Specification → Test (+ Component + Service in modern extensions).
- [x] **Component ≠ Object:** A component has deployment identity, interface-only coupling, and excludes inheritance (white-box reuse is forbidden).
- [x] **Component < Service** in network distribution scope; **Component > Object** in granularity and lifecycle independence.
- [x] **Key Reuse Challenges:** NIH syndrome, integration overhead, maintenance of shared components, qualification effort.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UML and Components Design by Contract: Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** UML and Components Design by Contract: Contracts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Design by Contract (DbC)

**Design by Contract (DbC)** is a software design methodology introduced by **Bertrand Meyer** (creator of Eiffel) that views the interaction between software modules as a **formal, precise, and verifiable agreement** — analogous to a legal business contract. Each method on a component specifies:

1. **Obligations** the client must satisfy (Preconditions)
2. **Benefits** the supplier guarantees in return (Postconditions)
3. **Permanent truths** the supplier must maintain (Class Invariants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST861 Module 3):** Contracts are the *behavioral specification glue* of component-based systems. They replace informal documentation with **executable, mathematically verifiable assertions** that the compiler or runtime can enforce.

A **Contract** in the context of UML and Component Design is a tuple:

$$ \text{Contract} = \langle \text{Pre}, \text{Post}, \text{Inv} \rangle $$

where Pre, Post, and Inv are boolean expressions evaluated over the pre-state, post-state, and class state of the supplier object.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Reservation

Imagine a real-world **restaurant reservation contract** between you (Client) and the restaurant (Supplier):

| Contract Element | Real-World Analogy | Software Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Precondition** | "Reservation valid only if held before 9 PM" | `require` clause — client must satisfy |
| **Postcondition** | "Table will be ready on arrival" | `ensure` clause — supplier guarantees |
| **Invariant** | "Restaurant will not be closed on a booked day" | `invariant` — class state always holds |

> [!NOTE]
> If you arrive *after* 9 PM (Precondition violated by you), the restaurant is **released from its obligation**. Conversely, if you arrive on time but the table is unavailable (Postcondition violated by restaurant), the restaurant is **liable**. DbC applies this same **liability model** to software components.

---

## 1.3 Why Contracts Matter in UML & Component Design

- **Component Reusability:** A black-box component is reusable only if its contract is precisely stated.
- **Test Oracle Generation:** Postconditions act as oracles for unit testing.
- **Fault Localization:** A failed assertion immediately identifies whether the **client or supplier** broke the deal.
- **Documentation:** Contracts are *executable documentation* — they never go stale because the compiler enforces them.

> [!TIP]
> In **UML 2.5**, contracts are typically expressed using the **Object Constraint Language (OCL)** attached to operations as `precondition` and `postcondition` notes. The KTU examiner expects familiarity with OCL-style syntax for contract specification.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of a Contract

A contract has three principal clauses. Below is the formal Eiffel-style notation (also acceptable in KTU answers):

```eiffel
deposit (amount: REAL) is
    -- Adds amount to the account balance
    require
        non_negative: amount >= 0          -- PRECONDITION
    do
        balance := balance + amount
    ensure
        increased: balance = old balance + amount  -- POSTCONDITION
    end
```

```eiffel
invariant
    non_overdrawn: balance >= minimum_balance  -- CLASS INVARIANT
```

The `old` keyword refers to the value of `balance` *before* the method executed — this is the **post-state reference mechanism** central to contract specification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Client–Supplier Liability Model

DbC establishes a **bilateral obligation** between exactly two parties:

- **Client (Caller):** Must satisfy the **precondition** before invoking the operation.
- **Supplier (Callee):** Guarantees the **postcondition** *only if* the precondition was satisfied when entry occurred.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):

1. **Pre-state Establishment:** The supplier object holds state $S_{\text{pre}}$ and the client holds input values $I$.
2. **Precondition Check:** The supplier evaluates $P(I, S_{\text{pre}})$. If **false** → the call is *illegal*; the supplier raises an exception and is *not obligated* to maintain any postcondition.
3. **Body Execution:** If $P$ is true, the supplier transforms state to $S_{\text{post}}$ and produces outputs $O$.
4. **Postcondition Check:** The supplier evaluates $Q(I, S_{\text{pre}}, S_{\text{post}}, O)$. If **false** → a *supplier bug* exists (the supplier has failed its obligation).
5. **Invariant Preservation:** The class invariant $I_{\text{class}}$ must hold in both $S_{\text{pre}}$ and $S_{\text{post}}$ — but **not necessarily during** intermediate execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the invariant exempt during execution?** Because intermediate states (e.g., a stack after `pop()` has decremented the counter but before returning the value) may temporarily violate the invariant. The invariant is checked *only* at public method boundaries — this is the **"Stable State Principle."**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Symbol | Semantic Meaning | When Checked |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Precondition | $P(\text{args}, S_{\text{pre}})$ | Conditions the **client** must satisfy | On **method entry** |
| 2 | Postcondition | $Q(\text{args}, S_{\text{pre}}, S_{\text{post}}, \text{result})$ | Conditions the **supplier** guarantees | On **method exit** |
| 3 | Class Invariant | $I_{\text{class}}(S)$ | Permanent property of the class | At **public method boundaries** only |
| 4 | Loop Invariant | $I_{\text{loop}}(S, i)$ | Property preserved across iterations | After each loop iteration |
| 5 | Old Value Reference | $\text{old}\ E$ | Value of expression $E$ at method entry | Used **only inside** postconditions |
| 6 | Contract Strength | $P' \Rightarrow P$ and $Q \Rightarrow Q'$ | Inheritance/Redefinition rule | On method override |
| 7 | Exception Type | `require_violation` / `ensure_violation` | Failure category | Runtime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule (Inheritance & Contracts):** When a derived class **redefines** a method, it may:
> - **Weaken** the precondition: $P_{\text{derived}} \Rightarrow P_{\text{base}}$ (i.e., $P' \subseteq P$)
> - **Strengthen** the postcondition: $Q_{\text{base}} \Rightarrow Q_{\text{derived}}$ (i.e., $Q' \supseteq Q$)
>
> This is because of **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** — the derived method must accept *at least* what the base accepts and deliver *at least* what the base delivers.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Pre/post conditions map to **High-Level Requirements (HLRs)** verified via Model Checking.
- **Financial Systems:** Invariants like *non-negative balance* prevent integer overflow exploits.
- **API Design (REST/SOAP):** WSDL/XSD schemas are essentially *typed preconditions*.
- **Smart Contracts (Blockchain):** Solidity's `require()` and `assert()` statements are direct descendants of Eiffel preconditions and invariants.

---

## 2.4 Contract Violation Categories

| Violation Type | Party at Fault | Recovery Action |
|---|---|---|
| Precondition false | **Client** | Client must fix call; exception raised to client |
| Postcondition false | **Supplier** | **Bug** in supplier; must be fixed in supplier code |
| Invariant false (public boundary) | **Supplier** | Bug — supplier failed to maintain internal consistency |
| Invariant false (mid-execution) | None | Acceptable — internal transient state |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Deriving a Contract for `BankAccount.withdraw()`

We will **exhaustively derive** the contract clauses for a withdrawal method, demonstrating the formal reasoning KTU examiners reward.

### Step 0: Problem Statement

A `BankAccount` class supports a `withdraw(amount: REAL)` operation. The account has a current balance `b`, a minimum allowed balance `m` (overdraft limit, e.g., $m = -500$), and a daily withdrawal limit `L`.

### Step 1: Identify the State Variables

Let us define the state space of a `BankAccount` object as:

$$ S = \langle b, m, L, \text{history} \rangle $$

where:
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ — current balance
- $m \in \mathbb{R}$ — minimum balance (typically a negative overdraft limit)
- $L \in \mathbb{R}^+$ — daily limit
- $\text{history}$ — list of prior transactions

### Step 2: Derive the Class Invariant

The invariant expresses truths that **always hold** for a properly constructed, well-behaved `BankAccount` object:

$$ I_{\text{class}} = \left( b \geq m \right) \wedge \left( m < 0 \right) \wedge \left( L > 0 \right) \wedge \left( \text{history} \neq \text{null} \right) $$

**Reasoning (line by line):**
- $b \geq m$ : balance never below overdraft limit.
- $m < 0$ : overdraft limit must be negative (or zero) to make sense.
- $L > 0$ : daily limit must be positive.
- $\text{history} \neq \text{null}$ : history list is always initialized.

### Step 3: Derive the Precondition

The precondition is the **client's responsibility** — what must be true *before* `withdraw` is called:

$$ P(\text{amount}) = \left( \text{amount} > 0 \right) \wedge \left( \text{amount} \leq L \right) \wedge \left( b - \text{amount} \geq m \right) $$

**Reasoning:**
- $\text{amount} > 0$ : Cannot withdraw a non-positive amount (no free money, no zero-ops).
- $\text{amount} \leq L$ : Respect the daily withdrawal limit.
- $b - \text{amount} \geq m$ : Resulting balance must remain within overdraft bounds.

### Step 4: Derive the Postcondition

The postcondition is the **supplier's guarantee** — what must be true *after* `withdraw` returns:

$$ Q = \left( b = \text{old}\ b - \text{amount} \right) \wedge \left( \text{history.size} = \text{old}\ \text{history.size} + 1 \right) $$

**Reasoning:**
- $b = \text{old}\ b - \text{amount}$ : The new balance equals the old balance minus the withdrawn amount.
- $\text{history.size} = \text{old}\ \text{history.size} + 1$ : A new transaction entry is appended to the history.

### Step 5: Verify the Invariant Holds at Method Exit

We must confirm the invariant is **preserved** by the method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:}\ & P \text{ is true, so } b - \text{amount} \geq m \\
\text{After execution:}\ & b' = b - \text{amount} \\
\text{Therefore:}\ & b' \geq m \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{(Invariant holds)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **proof obligation** is what makes DbC a verifiable methodology rather than mere documentation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Contract Strength in Inheritance

Consider a base class `Account` and derived class `PremiumAccount` that overrides `withdraw`. KTU's high-yield question pattern:

| Aspect | Base Class `Account` | Derived Class `PremiumAccount` | Allowed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precondition | `amount > 0 ∧ amount ≤ 1000` | `amount > 0 ∧ amount ≤ 5000` | ✅ Yes (weaker — accepts more) |
| Postcondition | `b = old b − amount` | `b = old b − amount ∧ cashback = amount × 0.01` | ✅ Yes (stronger — delivers more) |
| Invariant | `b ≥ 0` | `b ≥ −500` | ✅ Yes (relaxed invariant in derived) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse directions are forbidden:**
> - Tightening the precondition $P' \Rightarrow \lnot P$ would *break* existing clients.
> - Weakening the postcondition $Q' \Rightarrow \lnot Q$ would *break* substitutability.

This is the formal statement of the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** in contract terms.

---

## 3.3 UML 2.5 Notation for Contracts

In a UML class diagram, contracts appear as **notes** attached to operations, using OCL syntax:

```text
+withdraw(amount: Real)
{precondition: amount > 0 and amount <= dailyLimit and balance - amount >= minBalance
 postcondition: balance = balance@pre - amount and
                transactionCount = transactionCount@pre + 1}
```

The `@pre` suffix is UML/OCL's equivalent of Eiffel's `old` keyword.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Contract Architecture — Client / Supplier Interaction

The following Mermaid sequence diagram captures the **complete lifecycle** of a contract-governed call, including the three runtime check points.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Client as Client Object
    participant PreCheck as Precondition Checker
    participant Body as Method Body
    participant PostCheck as Postcondition Checker
    participant InvCheck as Invariant Checker
    participant State as Object State

    Client->>State: snapshot S_pre
    Client->>PreCheck: invoke op(args)
    PreCheck->>PreCheck: evaluate P(args, S_pre)
    alt Precondition FALSE
        PreCheck-->>Client: raise require_violation
        Note over Client: Client at FAULT
    else Precondition TRUE
        PreCheck->>Body: execute body
        Body->>State: mutate to S_post
        Body->>PostCheck: evaluate Q(args, S_pre, S_post, result)
        alt Postcondition FALSE
            PostCheck-->>Body: raise ensure_violation
            Note over Body: Supplier at FAULT (BUG)
        else Postcondition TRUE
            PostCheck->>InvCheck: check I_class(S_post)
            alt Invariant FALSE
                InvCheck-->>Body: raise invariant_violation
                Note over Body: Supplier at FAULT (BUG)
            else Invariant TRUE
                InvCheck-->>Client: return result
                Note over Client: SUCCESSFUL CONTRACT
            end
        end
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The three nested `alt` blocks correspond exactly to the three contract clauses. KTU examiners expect students to be able to *trace* a violation back to its source — this diagram is the canonical answer pattern.

---

## 4.2 Class Diagram with Contract Annotations

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class BankAccount {
        -balance : Real
        -minBalance : Real
        -dailyLimit : Real
        -history : List
        +deposit(amount : Real)
        +withdraw(amount : Real)
        +getBalance() : Real
    }

    class Contract_BankAccount {
        <<invariant>>
        balance >= minBalance
        minBalance <= 0
        history not empty
        <<precondition of withdraw>>
        amount > 0
        amount <= dailyLimit
        balance - amount >= minBalance
        <<postcondition of withdraw>>
        balance = balance@pre - amount
        history size = history@pre size + 1
    }

    BankAccount ..> Contract_BankAccount : obeys
```

---

## 4.3 Contract Strength Flow (LSP Compliance)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Class declares contract P, Q, I] --> B[Derived class redefines op]
    B --> C{P_derived implies P_base?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Q_base implies Q_derived?}
    C -- No --> E[VIOLATION: precondition tightened]
    D -- Yes --> F[LSP SATISFIED]
```
