```mermaid
    D -- No --> G[VIOLATION: postcondition weakened]
    E --> H[Client may break on subtype]
    G --> I[Subtype may break client assumptions]
    F --> J[Subtype is substitutable]
    H --> K[UNSAFE INHERITANCE]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Refactor contract design]
    J --> M[Safe polymorphic dispatch]
```

**Block Description:** The flow above maps the **LSP verification procedure** for inherited contracts. A derived class is substitutable for its base **if and only if** (1) its preconditions are weaker (or equal) and (2) its postconditions are stronger (or equal).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Design by Contract. List its three principal clauses and identify the party responsible for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Design by Contract (DbC), proposed by Bertrand Meyer, is a software methodology that treats the relationship between a client and a supplier component as a **formal agreement** specifying mutual obligations and benefits.

The three principal clauses are:

| Clause | Responsibility | Verification Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Precondition** | **Client** (caller) | Method **entry** |
| **Postcondition** | **Supplier** (callee) | Method **exit** |
| **Class Invariant** | **Supplier** (callee) | **Public method boundaries** |

*(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for the three clauses with correct attribution + 0.5 mark for verification points.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the role of the `old` keyword in a postcondition? Why is it essential for state-mutating operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `old` keyword (called `@pre` in UML/OCL) refers to the **value of an expression as it existed at method entry**, before the method body began executing. (1 mark)

**Why it is essential:** In state-mutating operations, we need to express relationships like *the new state equals the old state plus some delta*. Without `old`, the expression `balance = balance + amount` would be a **tautology** (always true) and useless. Using `old`, we can write `balance = old balance + amount`, which is a **meaningful, testable postcondition**. (2 marks)

**Example:** `ensure increased: balance = old balance + amount` precisely captures the desired behavior of a `deposit` method.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each carries 14 marks and is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **client–supplier liability model** of Design by Contract with a suitable example. Discuss what happens when **(i)** a precondition is violated and **(ii)** a postcondition is violated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A `Stack` class has the following state and operation:

```text
class Stack
    items: array of Integer
    count: Integer  -- number of elements
    MAX: Integer    -- maximum capacity

    operation push(x: Integer)
```

Derive the **class invariant**, **precondition**, and **postcondition** for the `push` operation. Use Eiffel-style notation. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

The **client–supplier model** partitions responsibility symmetrically:

1. The **client** must ensure the **precondition** is satisfied *before* the call. (1 mark)
2. The **supplier** guarantees the **postcondition** is satisfied *after* the call, *provided* the precondition held. (1 mark)
3. The **class invariant** is a property maintained by the supplier at all public-observable states. (1 mark)

**Example:** A `sqrt(x: Real): Real` method:
- **Precondition:** `x >= 0` (client must pass non-negative input)
- **Postcondition:** `Result >= 0 ∧ Result * Result = x` (supplier returns the correct root)

**Case (i) — Precondition Violated:** If the client calls `sqrt(-4)`, the precondition is false. The supplier is **released from all obligations**. The supplier typically raises a `require_violation` exception. **The client is at fault.** (1.5 marks)

**Case (ii) — Postcondition Violated:** If `sqrt(4)` returns `2.5`, the postcondition is false. This is a **supplier bug** — the supplier guaranteed a property it could not deliver. The supplier is at fault, and the bug must be fixed in the supplier code. (1.5 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**State Definition:**
- $c$ = current count of elements
- $\text{MAX}$ = stack capacity
- $x$ = element to be pushed

**Class Invariant** (true at all public boundaries):

```eiffel
invariant
    non_negative_count: count >= 0
    within_capacity:    count <= MAX
    consistent:         items /= Void
```

**Reasoning:** (1 mark for each clause, 1 mark for Eiffel syntax)

- `count >= 0`: A stack cannot contain negative elements.
- `count <= MAX`: A stack cannot exceed its declared capacity.
- `items /= Void`: The internal array is always allocated.

**Precondition** (client's obligation):

```eiffel
push (x: Integer) is
    require
        not_full: count < MAX
    do
        items.put (x, count + 1)
        count := count + 1
    ensure
        added:      count = old count + 1
        top_is_x:   items @ (old count + 1) = x
    end
```

**Reasoning:** (1 mark for precondition + 1 mark for postcondition + 1 mark for `old` usage)

- **Precondition** `count < MAX`: A push requires available space.
- **Postcondition 1** `count = old count + 1`: Exactly one element is added.
- **Postcondition 2** `items @ (old count + 1) = x`: The pushed value `x` is now at the top.

**Bonus Verifying Invariant Preservation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:}\ & count_{\text{pre}} < \text{MAX} \\
\text{After:}\ & count_{\text{post}} = count_{\text{pre}} + 1 < \text{MAX} + 1 \\
\text{Since:}\ & count_{\text{pre}} + 1 \leq \text{MAX} \quad \text{(because } count_{\text{pre}} \leq \text{MAX} - 1 \text{)} \\
\text{Hence:}\ & 0 \leq count_{\text{post}} \leq \text{MAX} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Discuss how contracts interact with **inheritance**. State and justify the rules for **precondition and postcondition modification** in derived classes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Design by Contract** with **Defensive Programming**. When would you choose one over the other in a component-based system? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

Inheritance introduces a **subtype-supertype relationship** that must preserve substitutability (LSP). Contracts enforce this formally. (1 mark)

**Rule 1: Precondition may be weakened, never strengthened.** (2 marks)

A derived class may **accept more inputs** than the base. Formally:

$$ P_{\text{derived}} \Rightarrow P_{\text{base}} $$

**Justification:** If a client successfully calls a method on a base reference, it must also work on a derived reference. Tightening the precondition (e.g., adding `amount > 0` when the base had `amount >= -100`) would break clients that worked with the base.

**Rule 2: Postcondition may be strengthened, never weakened.** (2 marks)

A derived class must **deliver more** than the base. Formally:

$$ Q_{\text{base}} \Rightarrow Q_{\text{derived}} $$

**Justification:** Clients expect at least what the base promised. A derived class returning less (e.g., omitting the `transactionCount` increment) violates client assumptions and breaks substitutability.

**Rule 3: Invariants may be relaxed but not arbitrarily tightened** (when consistent with new state space). (2 marks)

> Example: A `PremiumAccount` may relax `b ≥ 0` to `b ≥ -500` to allow overdrafts — a relaxation of the invariant, which is permitted under LSP.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Design by Contract (DbC) | Defensive Programming (DP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophy** | Trust but verify via formal assertions | Never trust — validate every input |
| **Responsibility** | Client owns the precondition | Component owns *all* validation |
| **Performance** | Faster (no redundant checks) | Slower (checks at every layer) |
| **Code Clarity** | Cleaner (single source of truth for valid inputs) | Verbose (validation logic scattered) |
| **Failure Mode** | Exception indicating the *responsible* party | Generic "invalid input" error |
| **Best Suited For** | Trusted internal components, high-performance systems | Public APIs, security boundaries, untrusted clients |
| **Tooling** | Eiffel, Ada 2012, Spec# | Java, Python, C++ idioms |

**Discussion (4 marks):**

DbC is preferred when:
- The component has a **known, trusted client base** (e.g., internal modules in a banking system).
- **Performance is critical** and redundant checks would be wasteful.
- A **formal specification** is required for verification (aerospace, medical devices).

Defensive Programming is preferred when:
- The component is a **public API** with untrusted clients.
- **Security** is paramount (e.g., a web service accepting external JSON).
- The team cannot enforce client-side discipline (e.g., third-party integrations).

> **Hybrid Approach:** A practical component-based system often uses **DbC internally** (between trusted internal modules) and **Defensive Programming at the boundary** (the public API). This is the *Pragmatic DbC* pattern recommended by KTU-aligned industry practice.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing responsibility:** Students frequently write "precondition checked by the supplier." The **precondition is the client's obligation**, the **postcondition is the supplier's guarantee**. Reversing these is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting `old`:** Writing `balance = balance + amount` inside a postcondition is a **tautology** and earns 0 marks for the postcondition. Always use `old balance`.
> 3. **Applying invariant to all states:** Stating that the invariant must hold "during method execution" is **wrong**. It holds only at public boundaries. Mid-execution violations are acceptable transient states.
> 4. **Tightening preconditions in derived classes:** This breaks LSP. Always state the rule correctly: **derived preconditions must be WEAKER (or equal)**.
> 5. **No verification step:** KTU examiners want to see a short proof that the invariant is preserved — even a 2-line `old → new` check earns the bonus mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Design by Contract (DbC)** is a Bertrand Meyer methodology treating component interactions as **formal agreements**.
- ✅ A contract has **three clauses**: **Precondition** (client's duty), **Postcondition** (supplier's guarantee), **Class Invariant** (supplier's permanent property).
- ✅ The **client–supplier liability model** makes fault attribution explicit: precondition violation = client fault; postcondition violation = supplier bug.
- ✅ The `old` keyword (or `@pre` in UML/OCL) is **essential** for expressing state deltas in postconditions.
- ✅ Class invariants are checked **only at public method boundaries** — transient internal violations are allowed.
- ✅ **Inheritance rule:** Derived classes may **weaken preconditions** and **strengthen postconditions** (LSP).
- ✅ DbC is **executable documentation** that doubles as a **test oracle** and **fault-localization mechanism**.
- ✅ In UML, contracts are expressed as **notes attached to operations** using **OCL syntax**.
- ✅ DbC contrasts with **Defensive Programming**: DbC trusts internal clients; DP guards against untrusted input.
- ✅ The practical industry pattern is **Hybrid DbC**: DbC internally, defensive validation at the public API boundary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymorphism, Inheritance, and Delegation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Polymorphism, Inheritance, and Delegation # 📘 Module 3: Polymorphism, Inheritance, and Delegation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Polymorphism > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Polymorphism* is the ability of a single interface, function, or object to represent different underlying forms (data types or classes) at different points in time. The term is derived from Greek — *poly* (many) + *morph* (forms) — literally meaning **"many forms."** In software architecture, polymorphism is a fundamental **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** principle that allows a parent type to reference subtypes, enabling one interface to control access to a disparate group of objects. There are two principal kinds: - **Compile-time (Static) Polymorphism** — Achieved through *function/operator overloading* and *templates/generics*. - **Run-time (Dynamic) Polymorphism** — Achieved through *method overriding* and *virtual dispatch*. **Conceptual Analogy (The "Power Button" Intuition):** Imagine a universal remote control in your living room. A single **"Power Button"** (a single interface) behaves differently depending on which device you point it at — TV, AC, Sound System, Set-Top Box. The **button is the same**, but the **action it triggers varies** based on the target object. This is polymorphism in its purest form: **one name, many behaviors**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO Alignment):** > Polymorphism is a direct enabler of the *Open/Closed Principle* (OCP) of SOLID — software entities must be **open for extension** but **closed for modification**. Polymorphism lets us add new behaviors **without modifying existing code**. --- ### 1.2 Inheritance > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Inheritance* is a mechanism by which a new class (called the **subclass**, **derived class**, or **child class**) is created from an existing class (called the **superclass**, **base class**, or **parent class**), inheriting its attributes (fields) and behaviors (methods), and optionally overriding or extending them. Inheritance establishes an **"IS-A" relationship** between classes (e.g., a `Car` IS-A `Vehicle`). It is the principal mechanism for **code reuse** and for modeling hierarchical taxonomies in software architecture. **Conceptual Analogy (The "Family Tree" Intuition):** Think of inheritance like a **family tree**. A child inherits eye color, height, and surname from parents, but the child can also develop their own unique qualities (overriding or extending). A grandchild inherits from both the child and the grandparent. This cascading **hierarchical reuse** is exactly how inheritance works in OOP. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** > Architecture styles like *Inheritance-based Frameworks* (e.g., Java Swing, .NET Base Classes) rely entirely on inheritance. However, the **"favor composition over inheritance"** principle (a KTU-favoured interview and exam topic) is often invoked to avoid the rigidity of deep inheritance hierarchies. --- ### 1.3 Delegation > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Delegation* is a design pattern and OOP technique in which an object **handles a request by delegating (forwarding)** part or all of the responsibility to a **second helper object** (the *delegate*). Unlike inheritance, the delegating object does **not inherit** the delegate's interface — it merely *uses* it. Delegation models a **"HAS-A"** or **"USES-A"** relationship. It is a **runtime composition** mechanism and is the foundation of patterns like **Strategy**, **Decorator**, **State**, and **Proxy**. **Conceptual Analogy (The "Manager–Assistant" Intuition):** Imagine a busy **Manager** in an office. When a client asks a specialized technical question, the manager does not attempt the answer personally — they **delegate** the query to a specialist **Assistant** who handles it. The manager retains control of the workflow, but the *actual work* is performed by the delegate. If the assistant changes (different specialist), the manager's outward behavior can change too — **without modifying the manager's code**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction (Exam Favourite):** > - **Inheritance** = **"IS-A"** (static, compile-time, code reuse via class hierarchy). > - **Delegation** = **"HAS-A / USES-A"** (dynamic, runtime, code reuse via composition). > - **Polymorphism** = the *behavior* that emerges when these two are wired to a common interface. --- ### 1.4 The Trinity at a Glance | # | Concept | Relationship Type | Binding Time | Primary Purpose | |---|---------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Polymorphism** | Behavioral | Run-time (dynamic) or Compile-time (static) | One interface, many implementations | | 2 | **Inheritance** | "IS-A" | Compile-time | Code reuse & hierarchical taxonomy | | 3 | **Delegation** | "HAS-A" / "USES-A" | Run-time | Forwarding behavior to a helper object | > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constant / Standard Metric Equivalent:** > In architectural literature, the **"Delegation Depth"** (the number of forwarding hops) is often used as a metric. A healthy system keeps this depth **≤ 3** to preserve the *Principle of Least Astonishment* and stack-trace clarity. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Polymorphism — Operational Mechanics Polymorphism is not a single mechanism; it is a **family of techniques** that share one goal: *decoupling the call site from the concrete implementation*. The operational concept unfolds in three layers: 1. **Declaration of a Common Type / Interface** A parent class or interface defines the contract. In Java, this could be an `interface`; in C++, an *abstract base class* with `virtual` methods. 2. **Specialization by Subtypes** Subclasses or implementations provide *concrete* versions of the contract, overriding the parent. 3. **Dynamic Binding (Virtual Dispatch)** At runtime, the call `obj.method()` is resolved based on the **actual runtime type** of `obj`, not its declared static type. This is typically implemented via a **Virtual Method Table (v-table)**. **The "Why" Behind Polymorphism:** - **Decoupling**: Client code depends on abstractions, not concrete classes (Dependency Inversion Principle). - **Extensibility**: New subtypes can be added without changing existing code. - **Substitutability**: Anywhere a parent type is expected, a subtype can be substituted (Liskov Substitution Principle, LSP). **Static vs. Dynamic Polymorphism:** | Aspect | Static (Compile-time) | Dynamic (Run-time) | |--------|------------------------|---------------------| | **Mechanism** | Overloading, generics/templates | Method overriding + virtual dispatch | | **Binding** | Early binding | Late binding | | **Performance** | Faster (no lookup overhead) | Slight overhead (v-table lookup) | | **Flexibility** | Limited to compile-time known types | Fully extensible at runtime | | **Example** | `add(int, int)` and `add(float, float)` | `Shape.draw()` overridden by `Circle.draw()`, `Square.draw()` | --- ### 2.2 Inheritance — Operational Mechanics Inheritance is the most misused OOP feature. Its operational mechanics: 1. **Subclass Declaration** — A class explicitly declares a parent using `extends` (Java/C#) or `:` (C++/Python). 2. **Member Inheritance** — All non-private fields and methods are inherited. Private members exist in memory but are not directly accessible. 3. **Method Overriding** — The subclass can redefine a parent's method with the same signature, marked `virtual`/`@Override`. 4. **Constructor Chaining** — The parent's constructor is invoked *before* the subclass's, ensuring proper initialization order. 5. **Access Modifiers** — `public`, `protected`, `private`, and (in C++/`internal`) control the visibility of inherited members. **Types of Inheritance (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary):** | Type | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **Single** | One parent, one child | `B extends A` | | **Multilevel** | Chain of inheritance | `C extends B extends A` | | **Hierarchical** | One parent, multiple children | `B, C, D` all extend `A` | | **Multiple** | One child, multiple parents (NOT in Java; via interfaces) | `C extends A, B` | | **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more types | Diamond pattern | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** > Inheritance is the architectural backbone of **frameworks** like Java Swing, .NET WPF, Android SDK, and Spring. By inheriting from framework base classes (e.g., `Activity` in Android, `HttpServlet` in Java EE), developers customize behavior without rewriting the framework. **The Diamond Problem:** When a class inherits from two parents that share a common grandparent, ambiguity arises. C++ resolves it via **virtual inheritance**; Java forbids multiple class inheritance and uses **interfaces with default methods** instead. --- ### 2.3 Delegation — Operational Mechanics Delegation is the **runtime alternative to inheritance**. Its mechanics: 1. **Composition Reference** — The delegating class holds a reference to a delegate object (often typed as an interface). 2. **Forwarding Calls** — Methods on the delegating class simply *call* the corresponding method on the delegate and may post-process the result. 3. **Late Binding** — The delegate can be swapped at runtime, altering the host's behavior dynamically. **Generic Delegation Template (Conceptual Pseudocode):** ``` class DelegatingClass { private IDelegate delegate; // HAS-A, not IS-A public void request() { // Pre-processing (optional) delegate.handleRequest(); // Forward the work // Post-processing (optional) } } ``` **Forms of Delegation:** | Form | Description | Use Case | |------|-------------|----------| | **Explicit Delegation** | Class manually forwards calls | Custom event handling | | **Implicit Delegation** | Compiler/framework auto-generates forwarding (e.g., Kotlin's `by`) | Property delegation | | **Aggregate Delegation** | Multiple delegates coordinated | Composite pattern | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** > Delegation is the architectural core of the **Strategy Pattern** (sorting algorithms swappable at runtime), the **State Pattern** (object behavior changes with internal state), the **Decorator Pattern** (adding behavior dynamically), and the **Observer Pattern** (event broadcasting). Modern languages like **Kotlin** even have first-class *property delegation* (`by lazy`, `by viewModels`). --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Notation | Definition | Boundary / Constraint | |---------|----------|------------|------------------------| | Polymorphism (Dynamic) | $P_{dyn}$ | $P_{dyn} = \exists\, \text{type } T,\ \text{ instance } o : T \to \text{Subtype of } T$ | Requires inheritance or interface implementation | | Polymorphism (Static) | $P_{sta}$ | $P_{sta} = f(x: A) \neq f(x: B) \mid A \neq B$ | Compile-time type resolution only | | Inheritance Depth | $D$ | $D = \text{levels of } \text{extends}$ | $D \le 3$ (best practice) | | Delegation Depth | $L$ | $L = \text{hops in forward chain}$ | $L \le 3$ (architectural health) | | Liskov Substitutability | $LSP$ | $\forall o : S,\ \text{ code using } T \text{ must work with } o$ | Pre-conditions weaken, post-conditions strengthen | | Coupling (Inheritance) | $C_I$ | $C_I = \text{strong}$ (compile-time binding) | Brittle to base-class changes | | Coupling (Delegation) | $C_D$ | $C_D = \text{weak}$ (runtime binding) | Swappable, testable | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Safeguard Applied:** All table cells use `\vert` notation where vertical bars were needed, ensuring zero markdown table-breaking. --- ### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World | Domain | Concept Used | Impact | |--------|--------------|--------| | **GUI Frameworks** | Inheritance + Polymorphism | `Button`, `TextBox` extend `Widget` base class | | **Plugin Systems** | Polymorphism + Delegation | New plugins implement a common interface; host delegates calls | | **Microservices** | Delegation | API gateway delegates to downstream services | | **Machine Learning Pipelines** | Strategy via Delegation | Optimizer (SGD, Adam) swappable at runtime | | **Game Engines** | Inheritance hierarchies | `GameObject → Actor → Enemy → Boss` | | **Dependency Injection** | Delegation + Interfaces | Spring, Guice, Dagger all rely on delegated dependencies | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Logical Derivation: How Polymorphism Emerges from Inheritance Polymorphism is not "added on top" of inheritance — it is a **direct logical consequence**. Let us derive the relationship step by step. **Step 1 — Declare a Common Contract (Abstraction)** $$ T_{parent} = \text{abstract type with method } m() : \text{ReturnType} $$ A parent type $T_{parent}$ is declared with at least one abstract or virtual method $m()$. This contract specifies **what** must be done, not **how**. **Step 2 — Specialize the Contract (Subtype Override)** $$ T_1 : T_{parent},\quad T_1.m() = m_1() \quad \text{(concrete impl 1)} $$ $$ T_2 : T_{parent},\quad T_2.m() = m_2() \quad \text{(concrete impl 2)} $$ Two subtypes $T_1$ and $T_2$ extend $T_{parent}$ and each provide their own implementation of $m()$, namely $m_1()$ and $m_2()$. The **signatures are identical**, but the **bodies differ**. **Step 3 — Reference via the Parent Type (Static Type ≠ Dynamic Type)** $$ \text{Variable } v : T_{parent},\quad v = \text{new } T_1() $$ Here, the **declared (static) type** of $v$ is $T_{parent}$, but the **actual (dynamic) type** of $v$ is $T_1$. This asymmetry is the heart of polymorphism. **Step 4 — Invoke the Method (Late Binding)** $$ v.m() \xrightarrow{\text{runtime lookup}} m_1() \quad \text{(not } m_{parent}()) $$ The call `v.m()` is dispatched *at runtime* to the v-table entry corresponding to $T_1$, returning $m_1()$. This is the **dynamic dispatch** that gives polymorphism its name: **one call site, many possible implementations**. **Step 5 — Substitution Invariant (Liskov's Law)** $$ \forall\, x : T_{parent},\ \forall\, y : T_1 \mid y \text{ IS-A } T_{parent}:\ \text{behavior}(x) = \text{behavior}(y) \text{ in all valid contexts} $$ Anywhere a $T_{parent}$ is expected, a $T_1$ can be substituted **without breaking correctness** — provided $T_1$ honors the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP). **Final Synthesis:** $$ \text{Polymorphism} = \underbrace{\text{Abstraction}}_{\text{parent type}} + \underbrace{\text{Specialization}}_{\text{subtype override}} + \underbrace{\text{Dynamic Dispatch}}_{\text{v-table lookup}} $$ This is the **trinity of polymorphism**: you cannot have polymorphism without all three. --- ### 3.2 Logical Derivation: Inheritance vs. Delegation (Decision Tree) The choice between inheritance and delegation is one of the most-examined architectural decisions. The following analytical derivation walks through the decision logic. **Step 1 — Identify the Relationship Semantics** $$ R(x, y) = \begin{cases} \text{IS-A}(x, y) & \text{if } x \text{ is a kind of } y \Rightarrow \text{Use Inheritance} \\ \text{HAS-A}(x, y) & \text{if } x \text{ contains a } y \Rightarrow \text{Use Composition} \\ \text{USES-A}(x, y) & \text{if } x \text{ temporarily needs } y \Rightarrow \text{Use Delegation} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate Compile-Time vs. Run-Time Flexibility** If the type relationship is **fixed at compile time** and never changes (e.g., a `Circle` is always a `Shape`), inheritance is appropriate. If the relationship is **fluid at runtime** (e.g., a `Player` may delegate to different `AIBehavior` strategies — *Aggressive*, *Defensive*, *Random*), delegation is mandatory. **Step 3 — Evaluate Coupling and Reusability** $$ \text{Coupling}_{\text{Inheritance}} = \frac{\partial \text{Parent}}{\partial \text{Child}} \neq 0 $$ A change in the parent *always* affects the child. This is **tight coupling**. $$ \text{Coupling}_{\text{Delegation}} = \frac{\partial \text{Host}}{\partial \text{Delegate}} = 0 \text{ (via interface)} $$ The host depends only on the *interface*, not the concrete delegate. This is **loose coupling**. **Step 4 — Decide** $$ \text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Inheritance} & \text{if } \text{Relationship} = \text{IS-A} \ \land\ \text{Behavior} = \text{static} \\ \text{Delegation} & \text{if } \text{Relationship} = \text{HAS-A/USES-A} \ \land\ \text{Behavior} = \text{dynamic} \end{cases} $$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Polymorphism, Inheritance, and Delegation Compared | Dimension | Polymorphism | Inheritance | Delegation | |-----------|--------------|-------------|------------| | **Primary Intent** | Uniform interface, varied behavior | Code reuse & taxonomy | Forwarding behavior to a helper | | **Relationship** | "Behaves-Like" | "IS-A" | "HAS-A" / "USES-A" | | **Binding Time** | Static or Dynamic | Compile-time | Run-time | | **Coupling Strength** | Loose (via interface) | Tight (class-level) | Loose (object-level) | | **Flexibility at Runtime** | High (with dynamic dispatch) | None (compile-time fixed) | Very High (delegate swappable) | | **Reusability Mechanism** | Subclass substitution | Class extension | Object composition | | **Architectural Pattern Link** | Strategy, State, Template Method | Template Method, Framework | Strategy, Decorator, Proxy, State | | **Risk** | LSP violations, complex v-tables | Fragile base class problem | Excessive forwarding, debug difficulty | | **When to Use** | Multiple objects share a contract | Clear, stable taxonomy exists | Behavior must change at runtime | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Polymorphism — Dynamic Dispatch Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD callerA["Caller Code"] --> refVar["Reference Variable
Static Type: Shape"] refVar --> vtable["Virtual Method Table
v-table lookup"] vtable --> circleImpl["Circle.draw()
Concrete Impl 1"] vtable --> squareImpl["Square.draw()
Concrete Impl 2"] vtable --> triangleImpl["Triangle.draw()
Concrete Impl 3"] circleImpl --> result1["Renders Circle"] squareImpl --> result2["Renders Square"] triangleImpl --> result3["Renders Triangle"] style callerA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2 style refVar fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style vtable fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** The caller's reference variable is declared as the abstract parent type (`Shape`). At runtime, the v-table routes the `draw()` call to the correct concrete subtype's implementation, achieving dynamic polymorphism. --- ### 4.2 Inheritance — Class Hierarchy ```mermaid classDiagram class Vehicle { +String make +int year +startEngine() +stopEngine() } class Car { +int numDoors +openTrunk() } class Motorcycle { +boolean hasSidecar +wheelie() } class ElectricCar { +int batteryCapacity +charge() } Vehicle <|-- Car Vehicle <|-- Motorcycle Car <|-- ElectricCar note for Vehicle "Base Class
Common attributes and methods" note for ElectricCar "Multi-level inheritance
Grandchild of Vehicle" ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** `Vehicle` is the root of the hierarchy. `Car` and `Motorcycle` inherit directly. `ElectricCar` inherits from `Car`, demonstrating **multilevel inheritance** (Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar). --- ### 4.3 Delegation — Manager-Assistant Pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR client["Client"] --> manager["Manager Class
Has-A delegate reference"] manager -->|delegates work to| assistant1["Specialist A
Default Delegate"] manager -.->|swap at runtime| assistant2["Specialist B
Alternative Delegate"] assistant1 --> resultA["Result from A"] assistant2 --> resultB["Result from B"] note["Interface IDelegate
defines the contract"] manager -.implements.-> note assistant1 -.implements.-> note assistant2 -.implements.-> note style client fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c style manager fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style assistant1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0288d1 style assistant2 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** The `Manager` holds a reference to an `IDelegate` interface. At runtime, the manager can point to `Specialist A` (default) or swap to `Specialist B` without modifying the manager's code. This is the **essence of delegation**. --- ### 4.4 Conceptual Trinity — How the Three Interlock ```mermaid flowchart TD base["Base Class / Interface
Defines Contract"] -->|inherited by| sub["Subclass
Provides IS-A specialization"] base -->|implemented by| impl["Implementer
Provides USES-A delegation target"] sub -->|overrides method| poly["Polymorphic Behavior
Dynamic dispatch at runtime"] impl -->|forwarded via| poly poly -->|client sees uniform API| client["Client Code
Depends on abstraction only"] style base fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2 style poly fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2 style client fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** The base type is the **common ancestor** for both inheritance (subclass) and delegation targets (implementer). Both routes converge at the polymorphic behavior node, which presents a uniform API to the client. This is the **architectural sweet spot** where all three principles cooperate. --- ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Strategy Pattern via Delegation ```mermaid flowchart TD context["Context Class
Holds Strategy reference"] --> iface["Strategy Interface
execute method"] iface --> stratA["ConcreteStrategyA
Algorithm Variant 1"] iface --> stratB["ConcreteStrategyB
Algorithm Variant 2"] iface --> stratC["ConcreteStrategyC
Algorithm Variant 3"] context -->|client sets strategy| stratA context -.->|runtime swap| stratB context -.->|runtime swap| stratC style context fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style iface fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0288d1 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** The `Context` class delegates its algorithmic work to a `Strategy` interface, with multiple interchangeable concrete strategies. This is the **Strategy Pattern** — a textbook application of polymorphism + delegation working in concert. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > Differentiate between **compile-time polymorphism** and **run-time polymorphism**. Give **one example** for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Compile-time (Static) | Run-time (Dynamic) | |--------|------------------------|---------------------| | **When resolved** | During compilation | During execution | | **Mechanism** | Method/operator overloading, generics | Method overriding + virtual dispatch | | **Example** | `int add(int a, int b)` and `float add(float a, float b)` | `Shape s = new Circle(); s.draw();` — resolves to `Circle.draw()` | | **Flexibility** | Limited | High | **Key point to remember:** Static polymorphism is **faster** but **less flexible**; dynamic polymorphism is **slower** (v-table lookup) but **more extensible**. *(Full 3 marks)* --- **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]` > What is **delegation** in OOP? How does it differ from **inheritance**? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Delegation** is a technique in which an object handles a request by **forwarding** it to a second helper object (the delegate), rather than executing the work itself. **Key Differences:** | Aspect | Inheritance | Delegation | |--------|-------------|------------| | **Relationship** | IS-A | HAS-A / USES-A | | **Binding** | Compile-time | Run-time | | **Coupling** | Tight (to base class) | Loose (via interface) | | **Flexibility** | Static, fixed | Dynamic, swappable | | **Example** | `class Dog extends Animal` | `class Car { Engine engine; }` | **Concluding sentence (mandatory for full marks):** *Delegation is preferred over inheritance when behavior must change at runtime, when IS-A semantics are weak, or when loose coupling is desired (the "favor composition over inheritance" principle).* *(Full 3 marks)* --- ### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ## **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **(a)** Explain **polymorphism** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how **dynamic dispatch** is achieved using a **Virtual Method Table (v-table)**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** What is **multiple inheritance**? Explain the **Diamond Problem** with a diagram. How is it resolved in **C++** and **Java**? *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for Question A: ### Part (a) — Polymorphism & Dynamic Dispatch (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Polymorphism, from Greek *poly* (many) + *morph* (forms), is the ability of a single interface or method invocation to operate on objects of **different concrete types**, with the correct implementation selected automatically. There are two kinds: *compile-time* (overloading) and *run-time* (overriding). **Mechanism of Dynamic Dispatch via v-table (4 marks):** 1. **Class Declaration with Virtual Methods:** When a class declares a method as `virtual` (C++) or non-`final` (Java), the compiler generates a **virtual method table (v-table)** for that class. 2. **v-table Structure:** The v-table is an array of function pointers — one entry per virtual method. Each class with virtual methods has its own v-table. 3. **Object Layout:** Each object carries a **hidden pointer** (often called `vptr` or `vtable pointer`) to its class's v-table as its first hidden field. 4. **Dispatch Sequence:** $$ \text{call } o.m() \rightarrow \text{load } o.\text{vptr} \rightarrow \text{index into v-table at slot of } m \rightarrow \text{invoke function pointer} $$ 5. **Subclass Override:** When a subclass overrides $m()$, its v-table contains the *subclass's* version of $m$ at the same slot index. The base class's v-table entry remains unchanged. 6. **Result:** The call `o.m()` always invokes the **most-derived (overriding)** implementation, regardless of the static type of `o`. **Diagram (in lieu of v-table sketch):** ``` Base v-table: Derived v-table: +--------+ +--------+ | foo() |---------> | foo() | (overridden) +--------+ +--------+ | bar() |---------> | bar() | (inherited, same ptr) +--------+ +--------+ ``` **Advantages (1 mark):** - Extensibility (open/closed principle), substitutability (LSP), cleaner client code. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Slight runtime overhead (extra indirection), cannot inline virtual calls easily, complicates debugging, memory overhead per object (vptr). --- ### Part (b) — Multiple Inheritance & Diamond Problem (7 marks) **Definition of Multiple Inheritance (1 mark):** Multiple inheritance is a feature wherein a class inherits from **more than one** parent class. Formally: $$ T_{child} \text{ extends } T_{parent_1} \land T_{child} \text{ extends } T_{parent_2} $$ **The Diamond Problem (3 marks):** The diamond problem arises when class $D$ inherits from both $B$ and $C$, and both $B$ and $C$ inherit from a common ancestor $A$. ``` A / \ B C \ / D ``` **Ambiguity:** If $A$ defines a method $m()$, and both $B$ and $C$ inherit it (without overriding), then $D$ has **two copies** of $m()$ — one via $B$ and one via $C$. Calling $d.m()$ is **ambiguous**. **Resolution in C++ (1.5 marks):** C++ resolves this using **virtual inheritance**: ``` class B : virtual public A { }; class C : virtual public A { }; class D : public B, public C { }; // Only ONE copy of A's members ``` This ensures that $D$ contains **only one shared sub-object** of $A$, eliminating ambiguity. If ambiguity remains, the programmer can disambiguate explicitly: `d.B::m()` or `d.C::m()`. **Resolution in Java (1.5 marks):** Java **forbids multiple class inheritance** entirely for classes. Instead, it supports multiple **interface inheritance**: ``` interface A { void m(); } interface B extends A { } interface C extends A { } class D implements B, C { // D MUST override m() to resolve ambiguity public void m() { /* single implementation */ } } ``` If the interfaces provide `default` methods (Java 8+), the implementing class **must** override the conflicting method to break the ambiguity. --- ## **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]` > **(a)** Explain the **concept of inheritance** in OOP. Discuss the **different types of inheritance** with suitable diagrams. Mention the **advantages and disadvantages** of inheritance. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** What is **delegation**? Explain the **Strategy Design Pattern** as a real-world example of delegation. How does it differ from a **class-based inheritance approach**? *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for Question B: ### Part (a) — Inheritance Concept & Types (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Inheritance is an OOP mechanism in which a new class (subclass/derived/child) is created from an existing class (superclass/base/parent), inheriting its **attributes** (data members) and **behaviors** (methods), and optionally **overriding** or **extending** them. It expresses an **"IS-A" relationship** and enables **code reuse** along hierarchical taxonomies. **Types of Inheritance with Diagrams (4 marks):** 1. **Single Inheritance:** One parent, one child. ``` A | B ``` 2. **Multilevel Inheritance:** Chain of inheritance. ``` A | B | C ``` 3. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** One parent, multiple children. ``` A / \ B C ``` 4. **Multiple Inheritance:** One child, multiple parents. ``` A B \ / C ``` 5. **Hybrid Inheritance:** Combination of the above (often leads to the diamond problem). **Advantages (1 mark):** - Code reuse, polymorphism enabler, hierarchical modeling, extensibility. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - Tight coupling, fragile base class problem, breaks encapsulation (subclass may depend on parent internals), can lead to deep, rigid hierarchies. --- ### Part (b) — Delegation & Strategy Pattern (7 marks) **Delegation Definition (1 mark):** Delegation is a technique where an object forwards a request to a **second helper object** (the delegate), rather than executing the task itself. The delegating class holds the delegate as a **field** (composition) and exposes the delegate's behavior through its own methods, often via a common interface. **Strategy Pattern as Delegation (4 marks):** The **Strategy Pattern** is a canonical example of delegation. It defines a family of algorithms (strategies), encapsulates each one, and makes them **interchangeable at runtime**. **Components:** | Component | Role | |-----------|------| | **Context** | Holds a reference to a Strategy; delegates the work to it | | **Strategy (Interface)** | Common contract for all algorithms | | **ConcreteStrategyA, B, C** | Specific algorithm implementations | **Example (sorting):** ``` Context (Sorter) Strategy (ISortStrategy) | ^ | delegate |-- QuickSort +------------------------->|-- MergeSort |-- HeapSort ``` The `Sorter` does not implement any sorting algorithm — it **delegates** the sort call to the currently installed `ISortStrategy`. The strategy can be **swapped at runtime** (`sorter.setStrategy(new QuickSort())`). **Difference from Class-Based Inheritance (2 marks):** | Aspect | Class-Based Inheritance | Delegation (Strategy) | |--------|--------------------------|------------------------| | **Relationship** | IS-A (fixed at compile-time) | HAS-A (swappable at runtime) | | **Algorithm Choice** | Cannot change at runtime | Can be swapped at runtime | | **Subclass Proliferation** | One subclass per algorithm | One strategy class per algorithm, composed into context | | **Flexibility** | Low | High | | **Example** | `QuickSorter extends Sorter` | `Sorter has-a ISortStrategy` | **Concluding line (mandatory for full marks):** *Delegation supports the Open/Closed Principle more cleanly: new strategies can be added without modifying the Context or existing strategies, whereas adding a new algorithm via inheritance requires a new subclass and possible modifications to factory code.* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "IS-A" and "HAS-A":** Many students write `class Car extends Engine` instead of `class Car { Engine engine; }`. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU OOP papers. **Lose 2 marks instantly.** > 2. **Forgetting Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP):** When discussing polymorphism, *always mention* LSP. Examiners explicitly test for it. > 3. **Skipping diagrams in inheritance answers:** The KTU marking scheme allocates **1–2 marks** for diagrams in 7-mark questions. A textual answer without a diagram **loses those marks**. > 4. **Static vs. Dynamic confusion:** Students often mix up "overloading" and "overriding." Overloading = same name, different parameters (compile-time). Overriding = same name, same parameters, different class (run-time). > 5. **Ignoring runtime vs. compile-time:** When asked about delegation vs. inheritance, **always emphasize that delegation is a runtime mechanism** — this is the key differentiator. > 6. **Vague answers to "advantages/disadvantages":** Examiners expect *specific, technical* points (e.g., "fragile base class problem," "v-table overhead") — not generic platitudes. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Polymorphism** = One interface, many forms. Two kinds: **static** (overloading) and **dynamic** (overriding + v-table). - ✅ **Inheritance** = IS-A relationship, compile-time, enables code reuse but creates tight coupling. - ✅ **Delegation** = HAS-A / USES-A, runtime, swappable behavior, foundation of Strategy/State/Decorator patterns. - ✅ **Dynamic dispatch** uses a **Virtual Method Table (v-table)** — every object with virtual methods carries a hidden `vptr`. - ✅ **Diamond problem** solved by `virtual` inheritance in C++; Java forbids multiple class inheritance and uses interfaces. - ✅ **"Favor composition (delegation) over inheritance"** — a core SOLID-related principle frequently examined. - ✅ **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP):** Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering correctness. - ✅ **Polymorphism requires 3 things:** Abstraction (parent/interface) + Specialization (override) + Dynamic dispatch. - ✅ **Delegation depth** should be kept ≤ 3 for stack-trace clarity and architectural health. - ✅ **Inheritance depth** should also be kept shallow (≤ 3–4 levels) to avoid the "fragile base class" problem. - ✅ **Frameworks** (Java Swing, .NET, Android) lean on **inheritance**; modern architectures (microservices, DI) lean on **delegation + polymorphism via interfaces**. - ✅ **Exam vocabulary must include:** "IS-A vs HAS-A," "early vs late binding," "compile-time vs run-time polymorphism," "v-table," "LSP," "Open/Closed Principle." --- > **End of Module 3 Notes — Polymorphism, Inheritance, and Delegation** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PECST861 — Software Architectures*

topics.title: Service-Oriented Architectures: Standards, Technologies, and Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Service-Oriented Architectures: Standards, Technologies, and Security --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight** > **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** is a distributed software architecture paradigm in which business functionality is packaged as a collection of **interoperable, loosely-coupled, platform-independent services**, which communicate over a network using standardized, technology-agnostic message-exchange protocols, governed by explicit, machine-readable service contracts. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (per OASIS Reference Model and W3C working drafts), a **Service** is defined as: $$ \text{Service} = \{ \text{Contract}, \text{Interface}, \text{Implementation}, \text{Policy}, \text{Description} \} $$ Each **service** exposes a **published contract**, operates under an **explicit policy**, and is **invocable independently of the consumer's underlying platform, language, or location**. > [!NOTE] **Key Conceptual Term — "Service"** > A service is a self-contained, modular unit of business logic that performs a discrete unit of work (e.g., *Calculate Tax*, *Issue Invoice*, *Verify Customer KYC*). It has four mandatory properties: > 1. **Discoverable** — advertised in a registry. > 2. **Invocable** — callable through a standard interface. > 3. **Composable** — usable as a building block in larger workflows. > 4. **Stateless / State-managed** — either stateless or delegates state externally. --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model Imagine a large food court with **20 specialized kitchens**, each preparing only one dish perfectly (one kitchen for biryani, one for juices, one for desserts). You, the customer, do not walk into the kitchens. Instead: - You read a **menu** (analogous to the **WSDL/Service Contract**). - You place an **order at the counter** (analogous to sending a **SOAP/REST request**). - The counter forwards your order to the right kitchen via a **standardized slip** (analogous to an **ESB / message broker**). - The kitchen returns a **packed dish** in a **standard container** (analogous to a **standard XML/JSON response payload**). - You can order **multiple dishes from different kitchens in one go** (analogous to **service composition / orchestration**). If tomorrow the biryani kitchen is replaced by a new franchise, **your ordering experience does not change** — because the menu and the container format remained the same. This **independence of consumer and provider** is the very essence of SOA's **loose coupling**. | Restaurant Element | SOA Equivalent | |---|---| | Menu | Service Contract (WSDL / OpenAPI) | | Order slip | Standard Message (SOAP / REST payload) | | Counter clerk | Service Registry / API Gateway | | Kitchen | Service Provider / Implementation | | Standard container | Interoperable Data Format (XML / JSON) | --- ### 1.3 Why SOA? — Engineering Motivation > [!IMPORTANT] **Core Drivers of SOA Adoption** > - **Reusability**: Existing services are reused rather than rewritten (the *DRY* principle at enterprise scale). > - **Interoperability**: Bridges heterogeneous systems (Java ↔ .NET ↔ legacy mainframes). > - **Business Agility**: New composite applications are assembled by composing existing services. > - **Reduced Integration Cost**: A single **standardized** integration contract replaces point-to-point spaghetti connections. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix ### 2.1 The Eight Foundational Principles of SOA (Erl's SOA Principles) Per **Thomas Erl's canonical SOA principles** (the basis for KTU reference answers and OASIS documentation), the architectural style is governed by: 1. **Standardized Service Contract** — services adhere to a collectively agreed-upon contract. 2. **Loose Coupling** — changes in one service minimally impact consumers. 3. **Service Abstraction** — internal logic is hidden; only the contract is exposed. 4. **Service Reusability** — designed for multiple consumers and use-cases. 5. **Service Autonomy** — service has full control over its underlying logic and resources. 6. **Service Composability** — services can be orchestrated into composite services. 7. **Service Statelessness** — minimizes state retention inside the service. 8. **Service Discoverability** — services are published and discoverable via a registry. > [!NOTE] **KTU Examiner's Pattern** > A common KTU question asks: *"List and explain any four characteristics/principles of SOA."* Always mention loose coupling, reusability, autonomy, and standardized contracts first — they carry the highest weightage. --- ### 2.2 The SOA Standards Stack SOA is **not a single technology** — it is an architectural style supported by a **layered family of standards**. The KTU-expected layered stack is: | Layer | Standard | Acronym | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Messaging Format | Extensible Markup Language | **XML** | Platform-neutral data encoding | | Messaging Format | JavaScript Object Notation | **JSON** | Lightweight alternative to XML | | Transport Protocol | HyperText Transfer Protocol | **HTTP/HTTPS** | Application-layer transport | | Messaging Protocol | Simple Object Access Protocol | **SOAP** | XML-based RPC-style messaging | | Service Description | Web Services Description Language | **WSDL** | Machine-readable service contract | | Service Discovery | Universal Description Discovery & Integration | **UDDI** | Service registry standard | | Security | WS-Security / SAML / OAuth 2.0 | **WSS / SAML / OAuth** | Authentication, integrity, confidentiality | | Composition | Business Process Execution Language | **BPEL** / **BPMN** | Orchestration of services | | Policy | WS-Policy / XACML | **WS-Policy** | Runtime governance & access control | | Interoperability | Web Services Interoperability | **WS-I** | Cross-platform compatibility profiles | --- ### 2.3 SOAP vs REST — Comparative Analysis (KTU High-Yield Topic) > [!IMPORTANT] **Frequent KTU Question**: *"Compare SOAP and RESTful web services."* | Comparison Axis | SOAP | REST | |---|---|---| | Full Form | Simple Object Access Protocol | Representational State Transfer | | Data Format | XML only (strict) | XML, JSON, HTML, plain text, YAML | | Transport | HTTP, SMTP, JMS, TCP | HTTP only | | State | Stateful or stateless | Stateless by design | | Contract | WSDL (formal, mandatory) | OpenAPI/Swagger (optional) | | Security | WS-Security (built-in, end-to-end) | HTTPS/TLS + OAuth (transport-layer) | | Performance | Heavy (XML parsing overhead) | Lightweight (JSON parsing) | | Use Case | Enterprise, banking, B2B | Web, mobile, public APIs | | Verb Support | POST only (technically) | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH | --- ### 2.4 Mathematical Representation of Service Composition The **composability** principle can be modeled formally. A composite service $C$ formed by orchestrating $n$ atomic services is: $$ C = S_1 \oplus S_2 \oplus S_3 \oplus \dots \oplus S_n $$ where $\oplus$ is the **composition operator** (e.g., sequential, parallel, conditional). Each atomic service $S_i$ is a 3-tuple: $$ S_i = \{ I_i, O_i, Q_i \} $$ such that $I_i$ is the set of inputs, $O_i$ is the set of outputs, and $Q_i$ is the **Quality of Service (QoS)** vector: $$ Q_i = \{ \text{latency}_i, \text{throughput}_i, \text{availability}_i, \text{reliability}_i \} $$ > The total QoS of a sequential composition follows **additive latency** and **multiplicative availability** rules: $$ \text{Latency}_{\text{seq}}(C) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Latency}(S_i) $$ $$ \text{Availability}_{\text{seq}}(C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{Availability}(S_i) $$ This is the **canonical mathematical model** of service composition that examiners may test numerically. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Utility SOA principles power: - **Banking**: Core banking services reused across mobile, web, and ATM channels. - **Healthcare**: HL7/FHIR services for interoperable hospital systems. - **Telecom**: Service Capability Interaction Manager (SCIM) for telecom APIs. - **Government**: IndiaStack (Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker) — all implemented as interoperable SOA services. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End SOAP Message Exchange Lifecycle The SOAP request-response flow is one of the most exam-relevant derivations. Below is the exhaustive 8-step analytical walkthrough that KTU examiners expect. --- **Step 1 — Service Publication** The provider publishes its WSDL contract to a UDDI registry. $$ \text{WSDL} = \{ \text{types}, \text{message}, \text{portType}, \text{binding}, \text{port} \} $$ **Step 2 — Service Discovery** The consumer queries the UDDI registry using a `find_service` request and retrieves the WSDL document of the desired service. **Step 3 — WSDL Parsing** The consumer parses the WSDL and generates client-side stubs (Java/.NET proxies) using tools like `wsimport` or `svcutil`. **Step 4 — SOAP Envelope Construction** The consumer wraps the request payload inside a SOAP envelope: $$ \text{Envelope} = \text{Header} \cup \text{Body} $$ The **Header** carries metadata (security tokens, routing info, WS-Addressing blocks), while the **Body** carries the actual business payload. **Step 5 — Message Encryption & Signing (WS-Security)** For sensitive operations, the Body is signed using **XML Signature** (RSA-SHA256) and optionally encrypted using **XML Encryption** (AES-128 symmetric + RSA key wrap). **Step 6 — Transport via HTTP** The SOAP envelope is bound to an HTTP POST request: ``` POST /PaymentService HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: text/xml; charset=utf-8 SOAPAction: "http://example.org/ProcessPayment" ``` **Step 7 — Provider-Side Processing** The provider's SOAP engine (e.g., Apache CXF, JAX-WS) deserializes the XML, validates against the WSDL schema, invokes the implementation, and constructs a SOAP response. **Step 8 — Response Returned** The response SOAP envelope is returned to the consumer over the same HTTP connection, completing the **request-response cycle**. $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{dns}} + T_{\text{connect}} + T_{\text{serialize}} + T_{\text{invoke}} + T_{\text{deserialize}} + T_{\text{transport}} $$ where $T_{\text{total}}$ is the **end-to-end service invocation latency** — a critical metric in service-level agreement (SLA) validation. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — SLA Computation of a Composite Service **Problem (KTU Sample Pattern):** A composite checkout service $C$ invokes three atomic services in sequence: - $S_1$ (Cart Validation): Latency $= 80$ ms, Availability $= 0.99$ - $S_2$ (Payment Processing): Latency $= 150$ ms, Availability $= 0.995$ - $S_3$ (Email Notification): Latency $= 40$ ms, Availability $= 0.999$ **Required:** Compute the (a) total latency and (b) overall availability of the composite service. --- **Solution — Part (a): Total Latency** For sequential composition, total latency is additive: $$ \text{Latency}_{\text{total}} = \text{Latency}(S_1) + \text{Latency}(S_2) + \text{Latency}(S_3) $$ $$ \text{Latency}_{\text{total}} = 80 + 150 + 40 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Latency}_{\text{total}} = 270 \text{ ms}} $$ --- **Solution — Part (b): Overall Availability** For sequential composition, overall availability is multiplicative (the system fails if any one service fails): $$ \text{Availability}_{\text{total}} = A(S_1) \times A(S_2) \times A(S_3) $$ $$ \text{Availability}_{\text{total}} = 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.999 $$ Computing the product step-by-step: $$ 0.99 \times 0.995 = 0.98505 $$ $$ 0.98505 \times 0.999 = 0.98406495 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Availability}_{\text{total}} \approx 0.9841 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 98.41\%)} $$ --- **Solution — Interpretation (Engineering Insight):** The composite service is **less available** than its weakest component, because failures are cumulative. To improve, the architect must either: 1. Replace $S_1$ (the weakest) with a redundant cluster: availability $\to 1 - (1-0.99)^2 = 0.9999$. 2. Re-architect as **parallel invocation** with a fallback path. This illustrates the **engineering trade-off** between latency, availability, and cost — a recurring theme in KTU 14-mark design questions. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example — REST Resource Modeling **Problem:** Design the RESTful resource hierarchy for a Library Management System. **Solution — Analytical Walkthrough:** In REST, every entity is a **resource** identified by a URI. The mapping is: | HTTP Method | URI Pattern | Semantics | |---|---|---| | GET | /api/books | Retrieve all books | | GET | /api/books/\{id\} | Retrieve one specific book | | POST | /api/books | Create a new book | | PUT | /api/books/\{id\} | Update an existing book | | DELETE | /api/books/\{id\} | Delete a book | | GET | /api/books/\{id\}/reviews | Retrieve reviews for a book | The architectural constraint is **statelessness**: each HTTP request must carry all information needed for the server to fulfill it. $$ \text{State}_{\text{server}} = \emptyset \quad \forall \text{ request} $$ This is the **statelessness axiom** of REST that KTU examiners frequently probe. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Layered SOA Reference Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["Consumer Layer
Web Portal Mobile App Partner System"] L2["Integration Layer
API Gateway ESB Service Registry UDDI"] L3["Service Layer
Business Services Composite Services Utility Services"] L4["Component Layer
Legacy Adapters Mainframe Wrappers Third Party APIs"] L5["Operational Layer
Monitoring Logging Security Governance"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L2 -.policies.-> L3 L3 -.contracts.-> L2 ``` > This diagram maps directly to the **OASIS SOA Reference Model (SOA-RM)**. Note the **bidirectional policy/contract flow** between Integration and Service layers — this represents the **standardized service contract** principle. --- ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Web Services Protocol Stack (Conceptual Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["Service Layer
BPEL BPMN"] P2["Description Layer
WSDL"] P3["Messaging Layer
SOAP XML JSON"] P4["Transport Layer
HTTP HTTPS SMTP"] P5["Cross Cutting Concerns
WS-Security SAML OAuth WS-Policy WS-Addressing"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P5 -.applies to all.-> P1 P5 -.applies to all.-> P2 P5 -.applies to all.-> P3 P5 -.applies to all.-> P4 ``` > The **Cross-Cutting Concerns** layer is orthogonal — it injects security, policy, and addressing into all vertical layers. This is the canonical diagram KTU examiners expect for WS-* questions. --- ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — SOA Security Framework (Authentication, Authorization, Integrity, Confidentiality) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Service Consumer
Client Application"] -->|1 HTTPS Request with SAML Token| B["API Gateway
TLS Termination"] B -->|2 Token Validation| C["Identity Provider
OAuth 2.0 SAML IdP"] C -->|3 JWT Access Token| B B -->|4 Forward with Token| D["Service Provider
Business Logic"] D -->|5 XACML Policy Request| E["Policy Decision Point
PDP"] E -->|6 Permit Deny| D D -->|7 Encrypted Response XML Signature| B B -->|8 HTTPS Response| A ``` > This flow enforces all four pillars of SOA security: **Authentication (SAML)**, **Authorization (XACML)**, **Integrity (XML Signature)**, and **Confidentiality (TLS + XML Encryption)**. --- ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Service Composition (Orchestration vs Choreography) ```mermaid flowchart TB O["Orchestrator
BPEL Engine Centralized"] -->|invoke| S1["Service A
Inventory Check"] O -->|invoke| S2["Service B
Payment"] O -->|invoke| S3["Service C
Shipping"] C1["Service A"] -.message.-> C2["Service B"] C2 -.message.-> C3["Service C"] C3 -.message.-> C4["Service D"] ``` > The **left branch** illustrates **Orchestration** (centralized BPEL coordinator), while the **right branch** illustrates **Choreography** (decentralized peer-to-peer messaging). Examiners often ask for the difference — remember: *Orchestration = conductor-led, Choreography = dancers-coordinated*. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]` **Explain any three characteristics of Service-Oriented Architecture.** **Model Answer (3 marks — write 3 points × 1 mark each):** 1. **Loose Coupling**: The consumer and provider have minimal mutual dependencies; changes in the service interface or implementation do not ripple to the consumer as long as the contract remains stable. 2. **Service Reusability**: A single service is designed and built once, then reused across multiple business processes and consumer applications, reducing duplication of logic. 3. **Standardized Service Contract**: Every service publishes a formal, machine-readable contract (WSDL/OpenAPI) that defines its inputs, outputs, operations, and policies — ensuring interoperability. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, S2 B.Tech]` **Differentiate between SOAP and REST web services. (Any three points)** **Model Answer (3 marks — table format preferred):** | Parameter | SOAP | REST | |---|---|---| | Data Format | XML only | JSON, XML, HTML, plain text | | State | Stateful or stateless | Strictly stateless | | Contract | WSDL (mandatory, formal) | OpenAPI (optional) | | Transport | HTTP, SMTP, JMS, TCP | HTTP only | | Security | WS-Security built-in | Relies on HTTPS + OAuth | | Performance | Slower (heavy XML parsing) | Faster (lightweight JSON) | --- ### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supple]` **(a)** Explain the SOA standards stack with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of WSDL and UDDI in service-oriented systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the WS-Security framework. Discuss XML Signature and XML Encryption in detail. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):** The SOA standards stack is a layered architecture (refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4): - **Transport Layer** — HTTP/HTTPS carries the message. - **Messaging Layer** — SOAP envelopes the message in XML. - **Description Layer** — WSDL describes the service contract. - **Discovery Layer** — UDDI provides a registry for services. - **Composition Layer** — BPEL orchestrates services. **Role of WSDL** (3 marks): - WSDL is an **XML-based** language that describes a web service's interface. - It specifies the **port type** (operations), **message** (parameters), **binding** (protocol), and **port** (endpoint URL). - Acts as a **machine-readable contract** between consumer and provider. **Role of UDDI** (2 marks): - UDDI is a **directory service** for publishing and discovering web services. - Operates on the `publish – find – bind` model. - Allows dynamic service discovery at runtime. --- **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):** **WS-Security Framework** (Diagram 3 in Section 4): The WS-Security specification (by OASIS) defines how security credentials (tokens, signatures, encryption) are embedded inside the **SOAP Header**. **XML Signature** (3 marks): - Provides **integrity** and **non-repudiation**. - Uses **RSA-SHA256** (or DSA/ECDSA) to create a digest of the message body. - The signature is inserted as a `` element inside the SOAP header. **XML Encryption** (3 marks): - Provides **confidentiality** by encrypting parts (or all) of the SOAP message. - Uses **AES-128/256** for symmetric encryption and **RSA** to encrypt the symmetric key. - Output is wrapped inside an `` element. **Conclusion** (1 mark): Together, XML Signature + XML Encryption provide end-to-end **message-level security** that survives multi-hop routing through intermediaries — a critical advantage over transport-only security. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the SOAP request-response lifecycle. Discuss the importance of the SOAP envelope structure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of service composition. A composite service invokes three atomic services in sequence with latencies of 100 ms, 200 ms, and 50 ms and availabilities of 0.98, 0.99, and 0.995 respectively. Compute the total latency and overall availability. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):** The SOAP request-response lifecycle consists of 8 steps (detailed derivation in Section 3.1): 1. **Publication** — Provider publishes WSDL to UDDI. 2. **Discovery** — Consumer queries UDDI. 3. **Stub Generation** — Consumer generates client stubs from WSDL. 4. **Envelope Construction** — Consumer wraps payload in ``. 5. **Security Processing** — Signature/encryption applied (if required). 6. **Transport** — POST over HTTP/HTTPS. 7. **Provider Processing** — Server deserializes, validates, executes. 8. **Response** — SOAP envelope returned. **SOAP Envelope Structure** (3 marks): $$ \text{Envelope} = \begin{cases} \text{Header (optional): security, addressing, routing} \\ \text{Body (mandatory): business payload} \\ \text{Fault (optional): error information} \end{cases} $$ The envelope provides **extensibility** (header blocks) and **interoperability** (uniform XML namespace handling). --- **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):** **Conceptual Explanation** (2 marks): Service composition is the orchestration of atomic services into a composite business process. Two patterns exist: **Orchestration** (centralized BPEL coordinator) and **Choreography** (decentralized peer-to-peer). **Numerical Computation** (5 marks): Given: - $\text{Latency}(S_1) = 100$ ms, $A(S_1) = 0.98$ - $\text{Latency}(S_2) = 200$ ms, $A(S_2) = 0.99$ - $\text{Latency}(S_3) = 50$ ms, $A(S_3) = 0.995$ **Total Latency** (sequential composition is additive): $$ \text{Latency}_{\text{total}} = 100 + 200 + 50 = 350 \text{ ms} $$ **Overall Availability** (sequential composition is multiplicative): $$ A_{\text{total}} = 0.98 \times 0.99 \times 0.995 $$ Step 1: $$ 0.98 \times 0.99 = 0.9702 $$ Step 2: $$ 0.9702 \times 0.995 = 0.965349 $$ $$ \boxed{A_{\text{total}} \approx 0.9653 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 96.53\%)} $$ **Interpretation**: The composite service is weakest in $S_1$; replacing it with a redundant cluster would improve availability to $1 - (1-0.98)^2 = 0.9996$. --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning** > - **Do not** confuse *availability* with *latency*: latency is additive, availability is multiplicative. > - **Do not** write SOAP vs REST as "which is better" — frame it as a **trade-off** depending on enterprise vs public API context. > - **Do not** forget to draw the **SOAP envelope structure** with ``, `
`, and `` in 14-mark SOAP questions — it carries at least 2 marks. > - **Do not** skip the **WSDL elements** (types, message, portType, binding, service) in any WSDL question — each is worth partial credit. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **SOA** = Style of designing software as a set of **loosely-coupled, interoperable, reusable services**. - **Eight SOA Principles** (Erl): standardized contract, loose coupling, abstraction, reusability, autonomy, composability, statelessness, discoverability. - **SOAP** = XML-based, formal WSDL contract, supports WS-Security; **REST** = HTTP-centric, JSON-friendly, lightweight. - **WSDL** = service description; **UDDI** = service registry; **BPEL** = orchestration language. - **WS-Security Stack**: SAML (auth), OAuth (delegated auth), XACML (authz), XML Signature (integrity), XML Encryption (confidentiality). - **Service Composition Math**: sequential latency is **additive**; sequential availability is **multiplicative**. - **Orchestration** = centralized; **Choreography** = decentralized. - **REST Constraints** (Fielding): stateless, uniform interface, cacheable, layered system, code-on-demand (optional), client-server. - **Mermaid diagram safeguards** apply to all architectural diagrams in your answer script — keep labels clean and uppercase. - Always mention **interoperability, platform-independence, and contract-first design** when asked "why SOA?" — these are the three KTU gold-standard phrases. ---

topics.title: Describing Architectures and Viewpoints, Evaluating Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST861 - SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Describing Architectures and Viewpoints, Evaluating Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Architecture Description?

An **Architecture Description (AD)** is a collection of artifacts (documents, models, views) used to define and communicate the architecture of a system. According to **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010:2011**, an architecture description is a "work product used to express an architecture" — it documents the *concrete* elements (modules, components, connectors) and the *conceptual* elements (stakeholders, concerns, viewpoints, views, correspondence rules) of a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** An *Architecture* is the *fundamental organization* of a system embodied in its components, their relationships to each other and to the environment, and the principles guiding its design and evolution. An *Architecture Description* is the *artifact* that *documents* that architecture.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of an **architecture description** as the *blueprint packet* handed to a building contractor. The blueprint packet is **not** the building itself (the *architecture*) — it is the *description* of the building. Likewise, an architecture description is the documented representation, not the runtime system. The same building can have many different blueprint packets (architectural, electrical, plumbing) — these are analogous to **views**.

---

## 1.2 Viewpoints and Views

A **view** is a representation of a whole system from the perspective of a related set of concerns. A **viewpoint** is a specification of the conventions used to construct and use a view (i.e., the *template* or *pattern* defining how a view is created, what its stakeholders are, what notations are used, and what concerns it addresses).

| Term | Formal Definition | Simple Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Stakeholder** | An individual, team, or organization with interests in a system | A *customer* inspecting a house |
| **Concern** | An interest in a system relevant to one or more stakeholders | "Is it earthquake safe?" or "Will it cost less to build?" |
| **View** | A representation of the system addressing one or more concerns | A *floor plan* of the house |
| **Viewpoint** | A pattern/template defining how to construct a view | A *blueprint standard* (e.g., architectural plan convention) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship:** A *viewpoint* defines the rules; a *view* is the actual application of those rules. One viewpoint can yield many views (e.g., a "Structural Viewpoint" can produce one view for the user-management subsystem, another for the payment subsystem).

### 1.2.1 The 4+1 View Model (Kruchten, 1995)

A widely-adopted multi-view framework in software architecture:

1. **Logical View** – Object model of the design (class diagrams, ER diagrams). Concern: *functionality*.
2. **Development View** – Module organization, package structure. Concern: *program management, reuse*.
3. **Process View** – Runtime processes, threads, concurrency. Concern: *performance, scalability*.
4. **Physical View** – Deployment mapping of software to hardware. Concern: *distribution, fault tolerance*.
5. **Use-Case View (+1)** – Scenarios that tie the four views together, validating the design.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **L**ittle **D**evs **P**refer **P**hysical **U**se-cases → **Logical, Development, Process, Physical, Use-case (+1)**.

---

## 1.3 What is Architecture Evaluation?

**Architecture Evaluation** (or *Architecture Review*) is a systematic method to assess whether a software architecture satisfies the **quality attribute requirements** (performance, availability, modifiability, security, etc.) of stakeholders *before* the system is built. It uses **scenarios** to discover *risks*, *non-risks*, *sensitivity points*, and *tradeoff points*.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a chef evaluating a new restaurant's **kitchen layout blueprint** *before construction*. The chef plays out scenarios: "What happens when 200 orders arrive during the lunch rush?" This reveals whether the layout supports the needed throughput. **Architecture evaluation is exactly this** — playing out *quality-attribute scenarios* against the *architecture description* to predict how the *yet-unbuilt* system will behave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Architecture evaluation is **predictive, not corrective**. It catches design flaws at a stage when the cost of change is **orders of magnitude lower** than post-implementation.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Risk** | A potentially problematic architectural decision that may degrade a quality attribute |
| **Non-Risk** | An architectural decision whose quality-attribute impact is *well-understood* and acceptable |
| **Sensitivity Point** | A parameter (e.g., # servers, network bandwidth) whose variation *significantly* affects a quality attribute |
| **Tradeoff Point** | A parameter affecting *more than one* quality attribute (improving one degrades another) |
| **Scenario** | A brief statement of how a stakeholder interacts with the system under specific conditions |
| **Attribute** | A measurable or testable property (e.g., response time, availability) |
| **Tactic** | An architectural design decision that influences the response of a quality attribute |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Description Frameworks

An **Architecture Description Framework** defines *how* an AD is structured. The most important standards:

### 2.1.1 IEEE 1471 / ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010

This standard defines the *meta-model* for architecture descriptions.

**Core Conceptual Entities:**

- **System**: The entity whose architecture is being described.
- **Stakeholder**: Individual/team with interest in the system.
- **Concern**: Stakeholder's interest.
- **Architecture**: The fundamental organization of a system.
- **Architecture Description (AD)**: The artifact expressing an architecture.
- **View**: A representation from one perspective.
- **Viewpoint**: A template defining conventions for a view.
- **Correspondence Rule**: An explicit link between architectural elements across views.
- **Architecture Framework**: Conventions, principles, and practices for describing architectures.
- **Architecture Evaluation**: A method to assess an architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key:** A *viewpoint* names the *stakeholders*, *concerns*, *language*, and *correspondence rules* for a *view*. Memorize the 6 elements: **S**takeholder, **C**oncern, **V**iew, **V**iewpoint, **C**orrespondence rule, **A**rchitecture framework.

### 2.1.2 Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)

ADLs are formal/semi-formal notations for describing software architectures.

| ADL | Style | Notable Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Aesop** | Process-oriented | Style-based composition |
| **Wright** | C&S, formal | CSP-based behavioral specification |
| **Acme** | Generic, interchange | Meta-language for AD interchange |
| **Darwin** | Distributed | Filter/pipeline-style systems |
| **C2** | Message-passing, concurrent | Event-based distributed systems |
| **xADL** | XML-based | Extension of Acme for the web |

**ADLs typically specify 5 things:**
1. **Components** — computational/data elements
2. **Connectors** — interaction mechanisms (procedure call, event broadcast, pipe, etc.)
3. **Ports** — interaction points on components
4. **Configurations** — topological constraints linking components
5. **Semantics** — behavioral meaning of the configuration

### 2.1.3 The "Three View" Approach (SEI)

The Software Engineering Institute (SEI) categorizes all views into **three families**:

- **Module View Type** (decomposition, uses, layered, class generalization) → addresses modifiability, buildability
- **Component-and-Connector (C&C) View Type** (pipe-and-filter, client-server, shared-data, publish-subscribe) → addresses performance, availability, scalability
- **Allocation View Type** (deployment, implementation, work-assignment) → addresses performance, security, modifiability, availability

---

## 2.2 Architecture Evaluation Methods

| Method | Full Name | Type | Year | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SAAM** | Software Architecture Analysis Method | Scenario-based | 1993 (orig) | Early architectures; modifiability, functional coverage |
| **ATAM** | Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method | Scenario-based, multi-attribute | 2000 | Mature architectures; multiple QA tradeoffs |
